title: Sydney Harbour Bridge

cover.hook: The steel arch that finally stitched the two shores of Sydney together, and the stories buried in its six million rivets.

chapters.title: The Idea
chapters.hook: A harbour crossing dreamed of for almost a century.

beats.heading: A century of plans
beats.body: Sydney had talked about bridging its harbour since the convict architect Francis Greenway proposed one in 1815. Engineers drew crossing after crossing through the nineteenth century, and none of them was built. The city kept moving by ferry, and the two shores stayed strangers to one another.

beats.heading: Bradfield's arch
beats.body: The scheme that finally won belonged to John Bradfield, the engineer who spent years arguing for a single great arch across the water. In March 1924 the contract went to the English firm Dorman Long, and the drawing on paper began to turn into steel.

images.caption: The arch reaching out from the shore during construction, around 1930. (State Library of NSW)

chapters.title: Construction
chapters.hook: Two half-arches reaching for each other over the water.

beats.heading: Building the arch
beats.body: Work began with the approaches and pylons, and then the arch itself on 26 October 1928. Two half-arches were built out from either shore, each held back by a fan of steel cables anchored deep in the sandstone. With much of the city watching, the two halves met above the water on 19 August 1930 and the arch became self-supporting. More than two thousand men worked on the bridge, and sixteen of them died before it was done.

images.caption: The half-arches reaching out across the harbour. (State Library of NSW)

beats.heading: Six million rivets
beats.body: The steelwork was held together by almost six million rivets, nearly all driven by hand. A rivet was heated until it glowed, then a cooker tossed it through the air to a catcher who fielded it in a metal can and hammered it home while the steel was still soft. The men worked hundreds of feet above the harbour, often with little more than rope for safety, dodging the scalding metal as it flew.

beats.heading: Granite Town on the Moruya River
beats.body: The granite that faces the four pylons was quarried at Moruya, about 300 kilometres south of Sydney. Around 250 workers of more than a dozen nationalities cut some 18,000 cubic metres of stone, with skilled masons brought from Scotland and Italy. Dorman Long built them a settlement called Granite Town, with cottages, a store, a hall and a school. When the cutting finished at the end of 1931 the town was dismantled, and little of it remains.

beats.heading: The neighbourhoods cleared away
beats.body: To carve the approaches and rail lines, the government resumed and demolished hundreds of homes, shops, churches and hotels through The Rocks, Milsons Point and North Sydney. Roughly 469 buildings came down on the north shore alone, and about 3,000 people were displaced. Many received little or no compensation, and there was no offer of rehousing. The grand arch the city celebrates today rose directly over streets that were simply erased.

chapters.title: The Opening
chapters.hook: The largest crowd Sydney had ever seen, and a man on a horse with a sword.

beats.heading: Three hundred thousand came to watch
beats.body: On 19 March 1932 about 300,000 people crowded the southern approach to watch Premier Jack Lang open the new bridge. It was the largest gathering the city had ever seen. The ceremony was meant to be a simple thing, a ribbon cut and a few speeches, before the crowd could finally walk across.

beats.heading: De Groot and the slashed ribbon
beats.body: Among the mounted escort sat Captain Francis de Groot, a Dublin-born antique dealer who had no place in the official party. He belonged to the New Guard, a militant anti-Labor group that despised Lang. Before the Premier could reach the ribbon, de Groot spurred his horse forward, drew his ceremonial sword and slashed it, declaring the bridge open in the name of the decent citizens of New South Wales. He was hauled from his horse and arrested within moments.

images.caption: Francis de Groot in New Guard uniform on his horse, 19 March 1932.
De Groot is pulled from his horse after slashing the ribbon.
The arrest of Francis de Groot at the opening of the bridge.

beats.heading: The ribbon retied
beats.body: Officials knotted the ribbon back together and Lang cut it as planned, so the ceremony went on. De Groot was fined for offensive behaviour and briefly held for a psychiatric assessment, which found him perfectly sane, then released. The stunt became one of the most retold moments in Sydney's history, a piece of theatre that has long outlived the politics that drove it.

beats.heading: The boy who rode a thousand miles
beats.body: Nine-year-old Lennie Gwyther rode his pony Ginger Mick roughly a thousand kilometres from country Victoria, alone, to see the bridge open. He became a small national celebrity along the way, and a statue in Leongatha still remembers the ride.

chapters.title: The Bridge Today
chapters.hook: A working bridge that became the stage for the city's biggest nights.

beats.heading: Pylons that hold up nothing
beats.body: The four stone pylons that frame each end look as though they carry the weight of the bridge, but they are very nearly decorative. The two-hinged steel arch does all the structural work, and the pylons could be removed without the span coming down. Bradfield and his architects faced them in granite to give the bridge the monumental dignity they felt the harbour deserved.

images.caption: The south-eastern pylon, now a museum and lookout.

beats.heading: The Pylon Lookout
beats.body: The south-eastern pylon found a second life as a lookout, reached by a long climb of about 200 stairs. It has held everything from a museum to, for a while in the mid-twentieth century, a cattery run by the caretaker's family. Today it offers one of the best free-standing views of the harbour, level with the deck.

beats.heading: BridgeClimb
beats.body: The idea came to businessman Paul Cave in 1989, and he spent close to a decade winning the permissions and working out how to do it safely. BridgeClimb opened to its first paying guests on 1 October 1998. Climbers go up in single file, clipped to a static line that runs the length of the arch, until they reach the summit some 134 metres above the water.

images.caption: Climbers on the arch with BridgeClimb.

beats.heading: The city's fireworks stage
beats.body: Every New Year's Eve the arch becomes the centrepiece of one of the world's largest fireworks displays, watched by more than a million people around the harbour and broadcast around the world. The bridge that took eight years to build now marks the turning of the year in a few bright minutes.

title: The Sydney Cricket Ground

chapters.heading: Yabba, the voice of the Hill
chapters.body: Stephen Harold Gascoigne, born in Redfern in 1878, sold dressed rabbits door to door around Balmain and spent his spare afternoons heckling from the Hill for more than a generation until his death in 1942. His lines passed straight into cricket folklore, among them 'your length's lousy but you bowl a good width' and, to an umpire, 'put a penny in him George, he's stopped registering'. When the open bank was rebuilt the new section was named Yabba's Hill, and in 2008 a bronze statue of him by Cathy Weiszmann took up a seat there.

chapters.heading: The Ladies' Pavilion and its Moorish lines
chapters.body: The Ladies' Pavilion opened on 12 December 1896, built for women members at a time when the main pavilion was a male preserve. The Sydney architect John Kirkpatrick gave it a Moorish flavour that still sets it apart from the brick and iron around it, and the McLeod Brothers did the building work. The same day a cycle track was opened around the playing field, a reminder that the ground hosted far more than cricket.

chapters.heading: The Hill
chapters.body: For more than a century the great grass bank at the southern end was simply 'the Hill', general admission with no seats and the loudest, wittiest, most merciless crowd in cricket. From here the rabbit-seller Yabba bellowed the lines that became legend, and the barrackers made and unmade reputations. Around them rose the ground's older stands, including the Members' Pavilion rebuilt in 1886 and the Ladies' Pavilion of 1896, both of which still stand and are heritage listed today. Individual seating finally arrived on the Hill in 1991, and the open bank gave way to the Victor Trumper Stand in 2008.

images.caption: The SCG and its packed Hill, c.1900-1910 (State Library of NSW)

chapters.heading: From soldiers' garden to Association Ground
chapters.body: The land began as part of the Sydney Common that Governor Macquarie set aside in 1811, and in 1851 a portion south of Victoria Barracks was handed to the British Army for a garden and cricket pitch. The 11th North Devonshire Regiment levelled the field, and it opened as the Garrison Ground in 1854. When the army left in 1870 the New South Wales Cricket Association took over, the ground was dedicated as the Association Ground in 1876, and only in 1894 did it take the name the Sydney Cricket Ground.

chapters.heading: What the Brewongle name really means
chapters.body: The Brewongle Stand that fronts the ground today is not a Victorian survivor at all. It opened in 1980 on the site of a much older stand, at a cost of about 8.9 million pounds in the money of the day, with corporate boxes and media rooms built in. The name is often said to come from an Aboriginal word for a camping place, but it actually came from a tea room that once served patrons at the ground.

title: Sydney Opera House

chapters.heading: Tubowgule, the island before the house
body: Long before any sail entered the harbour, this point was Tubowgule, a low tidal island of the Gadigal that joined the shore only at low water. It was a gathering and feasting place, and generations of discarded shell built up a midden on the rock, a quiet record of meals eaten here over thousands of years.

In November 1790 Governor Arthur Phillip had a small brick hut built on the point for Woollarawarre Bennelong, the Wangal man who moved between the Eora and the colonists as the young settlement's foremost go-between. The hut gave the headland the name it still carries. The Gadigal place that had been Country for millennia would, within a few generations, carry a fort, a tram depot and finally the Opera House.

chapters.heading: Fort Macquarie, 1817-1821
chapters.body: Between 1817 and 1821 a square castellated sandstone fort rose on the point to a design by the convict architect Francis Greenway. Its battlemented towers and gun deck were built for Governor Macquarie, on the rocky outcrop the Gadigal knew as Tubowgule, where Governor Phillip had earlier raised a brick hut for Bennelong. For about eighty years its cannon looked out over the cove.

images.caption: Fort Macquarie on Bennelong Point, photographed in 1885 (State Library of NSW / Wikimedia)

chapters.heading: The tram sheds, 1902-1958
chapters.body: The old fort came down in 1901, and a tram depot opened in its place the following year, built castellated and battlemented to echo the very fort it replaced. Electric trams trundled in and out of it until the depot closed in October 1955. In 1958 the sheds were swept away too, clearing Bennelong Point for the building that would replace them both.

images.caption: The castellated Fort Macquarie tram depot on Bennelong Point, demolished 1958 (State Library of NSW / Wikimedia)

chapters.heading: Utzon wins the competition, 1957
chapters.body: An international competition drew 233 entries from architects in some thirty countries. The judges, among them the American architect Eero Saarinen, plucked from the discard pile a set of loose sketches by an unknown 38-year-old Dane, Jorn Utzon, his entry numbered 218. It showed a cluster of soaring white shells over the harbour. He had never built anything on this scale, nor even visited Australia, and the win was announced in Sydney on 29 January 1957.

chapters.heading: The house the lotteries built
chapters.body: Premier Joe Cahill knew the public purse could never carry the bill alone, so in 1957 he launched the Opera House Lottery to fund the work. It became the biggest lottery the state had ever run, with a first prize of 100,000 pounds, and over the years it sold tens of millions of tickets. The building was paid for not by general taxes but by punters chasing a windfall, which is why it is sometimes called the house that the lotteries built.

chapters.heading: A final cost of 102 million
chapters.body: The original 1957 estimate of three and a half million pounds, about seven million dollars in the later decimal currency, proved wildly short of the mark. By its opening the Opera House had cost about 102 million dollars and run roughly ten years late. The shells that nearly broke the project became one of the most recognised silhouettes on earth, and in 2007 the building was listed as a UNESCO World Heritage site, with Utzon alive to know it was admired at last.

chapters.heading: Building the shells, 1959-1966
chapters.body: Work began in March 1959, before anyone knew how to build the shells. For years the Arup engineers and Utzon wrestled with their impossible geometry, until he hit on cutting every shell from the surface of a single imaginary sphere, so they could be cast from standard segments. It worked, but the cost climbed far past the original estimate of three and a half million pounds.

images.caption: The shells under construction, 1966 (Wikimedia Commons, CC BY-SA 3.0)

chapters.heading: The money is cut off, 1966
chapters.body: In 1965 Robert Askin's new Liberal government took office, and his minister for public works, Davis Hughes, began questioning Utzon's costs and withholding the fee payments owed to him. Starved of money and authority, and his designs for the interiors blocked, Utzon resigned in February 1966 and left Australia within weeks, never to return. Thousands marched through the city behind 'Bring Back Utzon' banners, but he was gone.

images.caption: Premier Robert Askin in 1966, under whose government the payments to Utzon were withheld (State Library of NSW / Wikimedia, public domain)

chapters.heading: Finished without him, 1973
chapters.body: A panel of local architects, Peter Hall, Lionel Todd and David Littlemore, completed the building, redesigning the interiors Utzon never finished, with acoustics still argued over today. Queen Elizabeth II opened the Sydney Opera House on 20 October 1973. Utzon's name was not so much as mentioned.

images.caption: The completed Opera House, opened by Queen Elizabeth II in October 1973 (Wikimedia Commons, CC BY-SA 3.0)

chapters.heading: Reconciliation with Utzon
chapters.body: Utzon never saw the finished building, yet late in his life Sydney made its peace with him. In 1999 he was re-engaged to draw up a set of design principles to guide the building's future, and a reception room was reworked to his plans and reopened in 2004 as the Utzon Room, the one interior space that follows his hand. He died in Denmark in 2008, aged 90, having reconciled with the city that once drove him out.

title: Hyde Park Barracks

chapters.heading: A barracks for convicts, 1819
chapters.body: Before the barracks, convicts in Sydney slept where they could, in huts, lodging houses and the backstreets of the town, beyond anyone's control after dark. Governor Macquarie's answer was a brick dormitory on the edge of Hyde Park, opened on 4 June 1819 to lodge the men assigned to government labour. It was built for around 600, yet at busy times it held closer to 1,400, packed into hammocks slung in long upstairs rooms. Roughly 30,000 men and boys passed through its gates over the next three decades.

images.caption: The Convict Barracks, Sydney, c.1819, watercolour (State Library of NSW / Wikimedia, public domain)

chapters.heading: Punishment and the treadmill, 1830s
chapters.body: From 1830 a bench of magistrates sat here as a Court of General Sessions, dealing with government convicts and handing down sentences served on the spot. A man might face solitary confinement, the treadmill, or up to 150 lashes on the triangle outside the barracks wall. For the men inside, the building was muster point, courtroom and place of punishment all at once.

chapters.heading: The immigration depot, 1848
chapters.body: When the last convicts were moved out to Cockatoo Island, the barracks emptied and then filled with new arrivals. From 1848 it became a female immigration depot, the first roof in Australia over thousands of single women stepping off the ships, among them girls orphaned by the Irish Famine. The colony had learned, partly from the campaigns of Caroline Chisholm, that unprotected women needed shelter from the predators waiting on the docks.

images.caption: Caroline Chisholm in 1852, painted with her map of Australia, 'the emigrants' friend' (State Library of NSW / Wikimedia, public domain)

chapters.heading: An asylum for destitute women, 1862
chapters.body: In 1862 the top floor was turned into an asylum for women who had nowhere else to go, the aged, the destitute, the terminally ill and the insane. At its peak it held a little over 200 of them, and many lived out the rest of their days within these walls. The building that had begun as a place of male confinement had become, by stages, a refuge for the colony's poorest women.

chapters.heading: Chancery Square: courts and offices, 1887
chapters.body: The asylum closed amid overcrowding in 1886, its last residents moved to the new Newington Asylum near Parramatta. The following year the old barracks were renamed Chancery Square and remade as law courts and government offices. Greenway's open dormitories were chopped into rooms, outbuildings crowded the yard, and for nearly a century the place ran on paperwork rather than punishment.

chapters.heading: Rats, relics and a museum
chapters.body: When the floorboards were lifted for conservation in 1980 and 1981, they gave up an extraordinary hoard of around 120,000 objects, scraps of clothing, buttons, pipes and letters, dragged into the cavities by generations of rats building their nests. That accidental archive made the building one of the richest convict and institutional collections in the country. It first opened to the public as a museum in 1984, was reborn in 1990 as a museum of its own history, and in 2010 was inscribed on the UNESCO World Heritage List among the Australian Convict Sites.

images.caption: The barracks in 1970, hemmed in by city offices, before its restoration as a museum (Wikimedia Commons, CC BY-SA 3.0)

chapters.heading: Francis Greenway, the convict architect
chapters.body: The barracks were designed by Francis Greenway, himself a convict, transported for forgery, whom Macquarie pardoned and made his colonial architect. The restrained Georgian elegance of the building was deliberate, because Macquarie wanted his convict settlement to look like a proper town. Greenway gave him the barracks, St James' Church across the road, and a string of other works that still shape the centre of Sydney.

images.caption: Francis Greenway, the transported forger who became the colony's architect (State Library of NSW / Wikimedia, public domain)

chapters.heading: The forger who earned his freedom
chapters.body: Greenway had been sentenced to hang in Bristol for forging a building contract, a sentence commuted to fourteen years' transportation. Macquarie was so pleased with the finished barracks that he granted Greenway a full pardon soon after the building opened in 1819. The architect's later years were less kind, since he quarrelled over fees, lost his official post and died in obscurity, yet for a time his face appeared on the old paper ten dollar note, the only convicted forger to feature on a nation's currency.

chapters.heading: Mary Ann and the matron's regime
chapters.body: Life in the immigration depot ran to strict rules set by a resident matron, with the women locked in at night and marched out by day to be hired as servants by employers who came to inspect them. Many of the famine orphans were teenage girls who had buried their parents in Ireland and crossed the world alone. The depot was meant to keep them safe until a place could be found, though for some that place was hard labour in a stranger's kitchen far up the country.

chapters.heading: What the floor remembered
chapters.body: Rats are unlikely heroes of Australian history, yet at the barracks they were the great collectors. Working at night for more than a century, they pulled threads, paper, fish bones and the small possessions of convicts and asylum women down between the joists and into their nests. When archaeologists opened the floors they found the desiccated rats still curled among the loot, so that the rodents that once tormented the inmates ended up preserving their lives in fragments.

title: Queen Victoria Building

chapters.heading: Work for idle hands, 1893
chapters.body: The 1890s brought a savage depression to New South Wales, with bank failures and crowds of men thrown out of work. The Sydney City Council chose to clear the tired market halls and raise a grand new building in their place, partly to put skilled tradesmen back on the wage. Stonemasons, plasterers, tilers, ironworkers and stained glass artists found steady employment on the project through the worst of the slump.

chapters.heading: McRae and the choice of style, 1893
chapters.body: The city architect George McRae, a Scot who had reached Sydney in 1884, did not settle on one look straight away. He drew the building up in four different manners, Gothic, Renaissance, Queen Anne and Romanesque, and let the council pick. They chose the Romanesque, a heavy, round-arched American style associated with the architect Henry Hobson Richardson, and McRae built it in sandstone across an entire block.

chapters.heading: A palace for the markets, 1898
chapters.body: The building opened with ceremony on 21 July 1898, named the Queen Victoria Markets, and Mayor Matthew Harris did the honours. Below were shops, showrooms and stalls, and upstairs near the Market Street end was a concert hall seating around five hundred people, with a carved proscenium over its stage. The day was marked with evening celebrations in the Town Hall next door.

images.caption: The Queen Victoria Markets on George Street, c.1900, photographed by Kerry & Co (Powerhouse Museum Collection)

chapters.heading: A slow failure, 1898–1918
chapters.body: For all its grandeur the building struggled to make money. In the year it opened only forty-seven shops were let, and it was not until about 1915 that every space was finally taken. The dream of a marvellous centre of trade faded, and the council began looking at other uses for its enormous halls.

chapters.heading: Library and offices, 1918
chapters.body: In 1918 the building was renamed the Queen Victoria Building, and over the following years it was steadily turned to civic use. Alterations carried out around 1917 and again in the mid-1930s converted the interiors into council offices, with Art Deco shopfronts added. The old concert hall was floored over into separate levels and became the City Library, which stayed there into the 1980s.

images.caption: The building in the early tram era, sliding from markets to council offices (State Library of NSW)

chapters.heading: The fight to save it, 1959–1986
chapters.body: From 1959 the building lived under threat, when Lord Mayor Harry Jensen proposed pulling it down for a civic square and underground car park. Demolition was raised again through the 1960s, but a wave of protest, including a group called the Friends of the Queen Victoria Building and supporters such as Barry Humphries, held it off, and the National Trust classified the building in 1974. The Malaysian developer Ipoh took on a ninety-nine year lease and restored it between roughly 1982 and 1986 at a cost near eighty-six million dollars, reopening it on 18 November 1986.

chapters.heading: Market ground, 1810s onward
chapters.body: Long before the great dome rose over George Street, this block was the working belly of Sydney. Governor Macquarie shifted the produce markets here from The Rocks in 1810, drawn by the slope down to Darling Harbour, and from the 1820s and 1830s a run of stone market halls stood on the site. For decades Sydneysiders came here to buy meat, vegetables and fish, and by the 1870s the ageing halls were being criticised as cramped and unsanitary.

chapters.heading: The great dome
chapters.body: McRae gave the building a vast scale, roughly thirty metres wide and a hundred and ninety metres long, filling the block between George, Market, York and Druitt streets. Its crowning feature is the central dome, a glass inner shell sheathed in copper on the outside and topped by a cupola. The dome was framed in steel by the local firm Ritchie Brothers, a company that also turned out trains, trams and farm machinery.

images.caption: The central dome, a glass inner shell sheathed in copper (Wikimedia Commons, CC BY-SA 4.0)

chapters.heading: Clocks, a letter and a compliment
chapters.body: The reborn building drew the fashion designer Pierre Cardin, who called it the most beautiful shopping centre in the world. Two great hanging clocks now mark the hours, the Royal Clock installed in 1986 with its parade of British royalty, and Chris Cook's ten metre Great Australian Clock, which arrived in June 2000 and turns through thirty-three scenes of Aboriginal and European history. Sealed inside the building since 1986 is a letter from Queen Elizabeth II, addressed to the people of Sydney, to be opened and read aloud by the lord mayor in 2085.

images.caption: The Royal Clock, whose hourly tableaux include the beheading of Charles I (Wikimedia Commons, CC BY-SA 3.0)

title: Sydney Town Hall

chapters.heading: Sydney's first cemetery, 1792 to 1820
chapters.body: Governor Phillip and the Reverend Richard Johnson chose this rise beside George Street as the colony's first general cemetery in September 1792. For nearly thirty years convicts, soldiers, settlers and their children were buried here, with more than two thousand names recorded before the ground filled and closed in 1820. Burials then shifted south to the Brickfield Hill or Sandhills cemetery, where Central Station now stands, while the walled, weathered graveyard sat on in the heart of the growing town.

images.caption: George Street looking north in 1842, the walled burial ground and markets stood on what is now the Town Hall site, watercolour by John Rae (State Library of NSW, public domain)

chapters.heading: A foundation stone over the graves, 1868
chapters.body: When the city fixed on the site for its town hall, the dead had to make way. Prince Alfred, Duke of Edinburgh, laid the foundation stone in January 1868. Exhumation ran from April to September 1869, and the remains that could be found were carried out to the new necropolis at Haslem's Creek, now Rookwood. The undertaker followed the council engineer's advice and cleared only the building's footprint, so a great many burials were left in the ground where they lay.

chapters.heading: Twenty years and a wedding cake, 1889
chapters.body: The winning design came from J.H. Willson, an obscure Tasmanian architect who died in 1869, soon after the work began. A succession of hands finished it, with Albert Bond shaping the ornate vestibule and Thomas Sapsford engineering the Great Hall. What rose was an exuberant pile of Pyrmont sandstone, all towers, columns and carved ornament, that Sydneysiders nicknamed the wedding cake. The clock tower climbed above George Street, and the vast Centennial Hall was opened in 1889, twenty-one years after the first stone.

images.caption: The completed Town Hall, engraved c.1893 (from 'Australia and New Zealand', no known copyright restrictions)

chapters.heading: The Grand Organ, 1890
chapters.body: The year after the hall opened, its Grand Organ was installed, built by William Hill and Son of London and, at the time, the largest in the world. It remains the largest pipe organ on earth that runs entirely on tubular pneumatic action, with no electric components at all. It also holds one of only two full-length 64-foot pipes in the world, the other being at Boardwalk Hall in Atlantic City, and that note is so low it is felt more than heard.

images.caption: Centennial Hall and the Grand Organ (Wikimedia Commons, public domain)

chapters.heading: Four thousand for the opening recital, 1890
chapters.body: The organ was unveiled on Saturday 9 August 1890 before some four thousand guests packed into Centennial Hall. The city brought out W.T. Best, organist of Liverpool and reckoned the finest concert player in the world, to christen the instrument. To stop the organ's deep vibrations from shaking plaster down on the audience, Sapsford had roofed the hall with a structured system of pressed Wunderlich metal panels rather than fragile plaster ceilings.

chapters.heading: The steps that the railway built, 1934
chapters.body: The famous broad steps were not part of the original design. Where they now stand there was once a large porte-cochere, a covered carriageway where horse-drawn vehicles set down their passengers. Tunnelling for the underground railway in 1934 undermined the formal entry and forced its demolition, and the open sandstone steps took its place. They quickly became the city's default meeting point, and the phrase 'meet me on the Town Hall steps' needs no further direction.

chapters.heading: A civic stage, and the dead beneath it
chapters.body: For well over a century the hall has carried Sydney's public life, from civic pomp and grand concerts to the rallies, marches and protests that gather on its steps. The first body of the old cemetery has never entirely left the building either. When archaeologists opened the floor of the Lower Town Hall from 2007 into 2008, they recorded dozens of graves still in place, most of them disturbed by the nineteenth-century works but never removed, more than a century after the hall was raised over them.

title: Central Station

chapters.heading: The Sandhills cemetery, 1820
chapters.body: When the old town burial ground filled and closed, Sydney's dead were carried out to the sand hills on the southern edge of the settlement. Consecrated in 1820, the Devonshire Street, or Sandhills, cemetery became the colony's main graveyard for most of the nineteenth century, with separate grounds for the Church of England, Roman Catholics, Presbyterians, Congregationalists, Wesleyans, Quakers and Jews. It was officially closed to new burials in 1867, the year Rookwood opened to the west, though families with vaults and purchased plots kept burying there for years afterward.

images.caption: 'The Old Cemetery, Devonshire Street', Julian Ashton's 1894 oil of the Sandhills graveyard, with the city rising behind (State Library of NSW, public domain)

chapters.heading: The first two stations, 1855-1906
chapters.body: Sydney's railways began on 26 September 1855, when the first line in the colony ran from a modest timber-and-iron terminus on Cleveland Fields. That first service did not reach Parramatta town but terminated at Parramatta Junction, the site of present-day Granville. A more substantial brick station, designed by the railways engineer-in-chief John Whitton, replaced it in 1874, and it grew to fourteen platforms but was cramped and hemmed in from the start.

images.caption: Inside the cramped second Sydney station, c.1880, which served from 1874 until 1906 (public domain)

chapters.heading: Clearing the dead, 1901
chapters.body: The government resumed the cemetery in 1901 and gave families two months to claim their dead. Exhumations began on 29 February 1901, and it is estimated that more than 30,000 bodies were eventually removed. Claimed remains went to suburban cemeteries chosen by descendants, while the unclaimed were carried by a special tramline to Bunnerong, an extension of the Botany cemetery. The work was never truly finished, and for decades railway workmen digging on the site kept turning up forgotten graves.

images.caption: A surveyor's 1894 view of the Sand Hills Burying Grounds, soon cleared for the new station (State Library of NSW, public domain)

chapters.heading: The third Central, 1906
chapters.body: The new terminus, Sydney's third Central, was designed by the government architect Walter Liberty Vernon and opened on the cleared cemetery on 4 August 1906, its long sandstone front facing the city. It opened without its tower, and the great clock tower that crowns it was not brought into use until 12 March 1921. Rising about 85 metres with four faces, the tower could be read from miles off and quickly became a city landmark.

images.caption: Crowds line the tracks as the new (third) Central opens in 1906 (Wikimedia Commons, CC BY 2.0)

chapters.heading: A neglected ground
chapters.body: By the close of the century the cemetery had become a sorry, dangerous place. Burials had been crammed into every square inch, even beneath the paths, and the whole ground was overgrown and uncared for. Neighbours tipped rubbish over the walls from the terraces above, and at night the place had an evil reputation. It was the railway, more than respect for the dead, that finally decided its fate.

images.caption: A surveyor's 1894 view of the Sand Hills Burying Grounds, soon cleared for the new station (State Library of NSW, public domain)

chapters.heading: The Mortuary Station
chapters.body: A short way to the west stands a stranger relic of the cemetery age, the Mortuary Station, a small Gothic sandstone terminal built in 1869 by the colonial architect James Barnet. From here funeral trains ran out to the Rookwood Necropolis, coffins loaded into special vans at the rear and mourners ticketed to travel on the same train for a shilling. It is one of the few buildings of its kind anywhere, and a reminder that this whole quarter was, for a century, a landscape of the dead.

images.caption: The Gothic Mortuary Station (1869), from which funeral trains left for Rookwood (Wikimedia Commons, CC BY-SA 4.0)

chapters.heading: The name that would not stick
chapters.body: For half a century Sydneysiders called the first two terminals Redfern Station, even though they sat well north of Cleveland Street and never actually stood in Redfern at all. The confusion ran the other way too, because the station we now call Redfern was at first named Eveleigh. Only with the third station of 1906 did the name Central finally settle on the place and stay there.

chapters.heading: From steam to the electric grid
chapters.body: Central was built for the steam age, but it soon had to carry the electric one. Electric suburban trains began running in stages from October 1926, reaching out along the Bankstown, Hornsby and western lines over the following years. The work was tied to John Bradfield's grand scheme for an electrified city railway, and the underground platforms cut beneath Central fed the new City Circle and the line across the Harbour Bridge.

chapters.heading: A clock tower the architect never finished
chapters.body: Vernon set out the station with a tower in mind, but austerity and the First World War kept it on paper for years. By the time the sandstone campanile finally rose in 1920 and 1921, the design work had passed to other hands, and Vernon himself had retired in 1911 and died in 1914. The four-faced clock that has timed Sydney's comings and goings ever since was, in truth, a memorial to a design its author never saw built.

chapters.heading: The funeral platform at Central
chapters.body: Even after the dead were lifted from Devonshire Street, the railway kept its ties to mourning. Central had its own mortuary platform where coffins and grieving families could join the Rookwood funeral trains away from the bustle of ordinary travel. That traffic ran until 1938, when motor hearses took over the work and the long Sydney habit of sending the dead to their rest by rail came quietly to an end.

title: Customs House

chapters.heading: Sydney's ground zero, 1788
chapters.body: Customs House sits at the head of Sydney Cove, where the Tank Stream once ran out into the harbour. This is close to the spot where Governor Phillip's party came ashore in late January 1788 and raised the flag, the beginning of the European settlement. For its first years the cove was a muddy tidal flat fringed by the colony's earliest huts, stores and gardens.

images.caption: Thomas Watling's 'A Direct North General View of Sydney Cove', 1794, showing the Tank Stream valley and the infant settlement at the head of the cove (State Library of NSW, public domain)

chapters.heading: Building the Quay, 1845
chapters.body: Through the 1840s the muddy head of the cove was walled and filled with convict labour to form the Semi-Circular Quay, the stone waterfront that became Circular Quay. Through this gateway passed almost everything entering the colony. On the new front the colonial architect Mortimer Lewis built a plain two-storey customs house, opened in 1845, to weigh, tax and control the goods coming off the ships. A line of dark granite set into the forecourt still marks the original high-water mark.

images.caption: Circular Quay in 1886, the port's working waterfront built out over the head of the cove (State Library of NSW, no known restrictions)

chapters.heading: Barnet's grand front, 1887
chapters.body: As the port's trade swelled, the plain building was transformed into something monumental. The colonial architect James Barnet partially dismantled and rebuilt it to three storeys between 1885 and 1887, adding a columned portico, a clock above the pediment, and long side wings raised between 1883 and 1889. Walter Liberty Vernon added two more floors and a rear wing in stages from 1896 to 1903, lifting the building to five storeys in an exuberant French neoclassical manner. The result was one of the finest public buildings in the city, presiding over the Quay.

images.caption: Barnet's Customs House, photographed by Henry King c.1900, with the clock and portico he added (State Library of NSW, no known restrictions)

chapters.heading: Federation takes the keys
chapters.body: When the Australian colonies joined together in 1901, the power to collect customs and excise passed from the states to the new Commonwealth. From that point the building at Circular Quay worked for the federal government rather than the colony of New South Wales. A plaque inside records the moment when, after Federation, the collection of customs duties moved from state to national control, one of the first practical signs of the new nation in everyday Sydney life.

chapters.heading: A model city under glass
chapters.body: The scale model beneath the lobby floor was built by Modelcraft in 1998 at a scale of about 1:500, and it weighs close to a tonne. It maps the streets, blocks and major buildings of the city centre in miniature, and it is updated as the real skyline changes. Standing on the glass, a visitor looks down on the whole of central Sydney, including the very building they are standing in.

chapters.heading: From customs to library, 2005
chapters.body: After 145 years the Customs Service finally moved out in 1990, and the great building stood looking for a purpose. In 2005 it reopened as the City of Sydney's flagship library. Its best-loved feature is in the lobby, a glass floor laid over a detailed scale model of the city centre, so visitors can stand directly above this very building rendered in miniature, and over the buried line of the Tank Stream where the whole story began.

images.caption: The glass floor of the Customs House library, set over a scale model of the city (Wikimedia Commons, CC BY-SA 3.0)

chapters.heading: The colonel who built it
chapters.body: The driving force behind the sandstone building was Colonel John George Nathaniel Gibbes, who served as Collector of Customs for New South Wales from 1834 to 1859, an unusually long term of twenty-five years. In 1844 he persuaded Governor Gipps to begin work on a proper customs house to match the port's growing trade. The colony was deep in an economic depression at the time, so the project doubled as relief work for out of work stonemasons and labourers. Lewis gave the facade thirteen tall windows so that officers could keep a clear watch over the shipping in the cove.

chapters.heading: Where the colony paid its way
chapters.body: For most of the nineteenth century, customs duties on imported spirits, tobacco, tea and sugar were the single largest source of the colonial government's revenue. The money that built roads, gaols and schools was largely counted and recorded inside these walls. The ground floor was a crowded and noisy place of weighing, measuring and argument, ringed by public houses and shipping agents, where delays and disputed dealings were part of the daily trade.

title: General Post Office

chapters.heading: The nerve centre of a colony
chapters.body: The GPO was never only about letters. The electric telegraph arrived in 1867 and the telephone in the early 1880s, and both were run from this building, so the news of the world reached Sydney through its switchboards and wires. The clock tower doubled as a signal mast, flying coloured flags and balls to telegraph weather warnings to shipping in the harbour. For a colony strung out along a vast coastline, this single corner of Martin Place was where the pulse of communication was felt first.

chapters.heading: Barnet's palace of the mails, 1874
chapters.body: The first stage of the colonial architect James Barnet's General Post Office opened on 1 September 1874, a vast Italian Renaissance palace in local sandstone built to handle the mail of a booming colony. With its long colonnades and carved detail it was, on completion, the grandest building in the colony. It gave the city the new civic space that would become Martin Place.

images.caption: Barnet's General Post Office rising over the city in the late nineteenth century (State Library of NSW, no known restrictions)

chapters.heading: The scandalous carvings, 1883
chapters.body: Around the Pitt Street arches, the sculptor Tomaso Sani carved not gods or governors but ordinary working people, among them a postman, a shearer, a barmaid, a fishmonger and a sailor. From 1883 they were denounced as vulgar caricatures, an insult unworthy of so grand a building, and they earned the nickname Barnet's blot. A board of enquiry urged that they be cut away and replaced with plain blocks of stone. Barnet flatly refused, and in December 1890 the Legislative Assembly voted 54 to 5 to keep them, where they remain today.

chapters.heading: The tower taken down, 1942
chapters.body: The clock tower that crowns the building was topped out by 1887, but the long fight over its bells and clockwork meant it was not finished until Lady Jersey set the works in motion on 16 September 1891. Half a century later it nearly disappeared. In 1942, with Japanese submarines in the harbour and fears the landmark might guide enemy bombers to the heart of the city, it was carefully dismantled stone by stone and put into storage. There it stayed for twenty-two years, until it was rebuilt and returned to the skyline in 1964.

images.caption: The GPO clock tower, flag at half-mast on Remembrance Day 1936, photographed by Sam Hood six years before it was taken down (State Library of NSW, no known restrictions)

chapters.heading: Eternity in the bell
chapters.body: When the tower came down in 1942, workers lifting out the great bell found a single word chalked or scratched inside it, Eternity. It was the signature of Arthur Stace, the reformed Sydney drunk who for decades wrote that one word in flowing copperplate on footpaths across the city at dawn. How his handwriting reached the inside of a bell high above Martin Place is still a small mystery. It is a quiet reminder that the GPO sat at the very centre of a working, scribbling, walking city.

chapters.heading: From mails to Martin Place, 1996
chapters.body: For more than a century it was the working heart of the nation's mail. In 1996 the building was leased to private operators on a 99-year lease, and in September 1999 it reopened as a hotel and dining arcade timed to the approach of the Sydney Olympics, known as No. 1 Martin Place. A glazed atrium now rises through the old fabric, with Barnet's sandstone and Sani's carvings preserved around it, and the clock tower still keeping time over the square.

images.caption: The old clock tower seen through the glass roof of the GPO's modern hotel and dining arcade (Wikimedia Commons, CC BY 2.0)

chapters.heading: The Tank Stream beneath
chapters.body: The GPO sits astride the Tank Stream, the small freshwater creek that was the whole reason Sydney was founded on this spot. From 1788 it was the colony's water supply, its banks cut with storage tanks that gave it its name. Fouled and built over within a few decades, it was bricked into a tunnel and vanished beneath the streets. It still runs there today, directly under the foundations of the post office.

images.caption: The Tank Stream in 1842, watercolour by John Skinner Prout. It still runs in a tunnel beneath the GPO (State Library of NSW, public domain)

chapters.heading: A slow road to a grand front
chapters.body: Barnet's design took far longer to build than anyone planned. The old post office on the site was pulled down in 1863 and the work was carried on in two long stages, the George Street block from 1866 to 1874 and the Pitt Street extension through the 1880s. Labour shortages and shifting government budgets dragged the first stage out about five years past its deadline. By the time the whole thing was done, Barnet had spent the better part of his career on one building.

title: St Mary's Cathedral

chapters.heading: The first St Mary's, 1821-1865
chapters.body: Governor Macquarie laid the foundation stone of the first St Mary's on 29 October 1821, on land at the edge of Hyde Park granted to a colony that had begun by forbidding the Catholic mass altogether. A modest Gothic stone church built by James Dempsey rose slowly over the following decades, later reworked to designs by Augustus Welby Pugin. Then, on 29 June 1865, fire tore through it overnight and left the colony's Catholics with a blackened ruin.

images.caption: The first St Mary's, photographed c.1860 as a stereoscopic card. This cathedral burned down overnight in 1865 (State Library of NSW, public domain)

chapters.heading: A cathedral for the ages, 1868
chapters.body: Archbishop John Bede Polding turned to William Wardell, a pupil of Pugin who had arrived in the colony in 1858, with an open brief for anything beautiful and grand to the limit of their means. Polding laid the foundation stone on 8 December 1868, and Wardell set about a vast Gothic Revival church in honey-coloured Sydney sandstone, built to outlast everyone who worked on it. The first stage was dedicated in 1882, yet the building crept forward by generations and the nave was not finished until 1928. Neither Wardell nor Polding lived to see it complete.

images.caption: Wardell's still-spireless cathedral seen from Hyde Park, c.1900, photographed by Kerry & Co (Powerhouse Museum Collection / Wikimedia, public domain)

chapters.heading: The spires at last, 2000
chapters.body: Finally, with a government grant to mark the new millennium, the city resolved to finish the job. In 2000 the twin southern spires were clad in Gosford sandstone and lifted into place by helicopter, 132 years after Wardell's cathedral was begun and 179 years after the first stone was laid. At last the building stood close to the way it had always been meant to, completing one of the longest construction stories in Australia.

images.caption: The southern spires under construction in 2000, 132 years after Wardell's cathedral was begun (Wikimedia Commons, public domain)

chapters.heading: The spire that never came
chapters.body: Even with the southern pair raised in 2000, the cathedral is not quite the church Wardell drew. His plan placed a third, taller spire over the central crossing tower, where the nave meets the transepts, and that one was never built. The stocky crossing tower holds the bells and reaches about 46 metres above the floor, finished with tall crocketed pinnacles that lend it some grace. So the silhouette over the city remains, in one sense, forever incomplete.

chapters.heading: The great nave
chapters.body: Inside, Wardell's cathedral runs to about 107 metres, the longest church in Australia, a soaring sandstone nave rising in three stages to a vaulted ceiling and lit through great traceried windows. Beneath it lies its quiet masterpiece, the crypt floor laid by the Melocco brothers, the Italian craftsmen who first brought terrazzo to Australia. They rendered a Celtic cross like an illuminated page from the Book of Kells, the floor running some 42 metres by 21 metres and finished in 1934.

images.caption: The Gothic nave looking east, c.1900, photographed by Kerry & Co (Powerhouse Museum Collection / Wikimedia, public domain)

chapters.heading: A century without spires
chapters.body: For all its grandeur, the cathedral stood unfinished in plain sight. Wardell's design called for twin spires over the southern towers, but the money and the will ran out, and for more than a century the towers ended in bare, flat stumps. Generations of Sydneysiders grew up never having seen the building as its architect intended, a cathedral famous in part for what it lacked.

images.caption: Still without its spires in 1930, the southern towers ending in bare stumps (RAHS / Wikimedia, no known restrictions)

chapters.heading: From banned faith to minor basilica
chapters.body: The cathedral marks how far the colony's Catholics travelled from the days when their worship was illegal. Father John Therry, who arrived in 1820, dreamed of a great golden church raising twin spires over Sydney, a vision realised only long after his death. Archbishop Roger Vaughan dedicated Wardell's first completed section on 8 September 1882, and in 1932 Pope Pius XI raised St Mary's to the rank of minor basilica, naming it for the Immaculate Mother of God, Help of Christians, Patroness of Australia.

chapters.heading: A resting place for the colony's churchmen
chapters.body: The crypt is more than a showcase for the Melocco floor. It holds the remains of many of the early priests and bishops who built the Catholic Church in New South Wales, including Archbishop Polding himself, who began the present cathedral but died in 1877 with it far from done. The chamber was not formally dedicated until 1961, when Cardinal Norman Gilroy completed the long work below the nave, a fitting echo of the slow rise of the church above.

title: Darlinghurst Gaol

chapters.heading: The walls, 1822-1841
chapters.body: Convicts began cutting the great sandstone perimeter wall in 1822, on a site that Francis Greenway had pegged out the year before, and then the money ran out and the work languished for more than a decade. The gaol that finally rose inside those walls was designed by the Colonial Architect Mortimer Lewis and reworked by the Royal Engineer George Barney, on a radial plan with six cell wings spreading from a central rotunda so that a few warders could watch the lot. The first prisoners moved in on 7 June 1841, into buildings that were still being finished around them, and cells meant for one man were soon holding three.

images.caption: John Rae's 1842 view across Hyde Park toward Darlinghurst, the new gaol having opened the year before (State Library of NSW, public domain)

chapters.heading: The hangman on the hill
chapters.body: For roughly a decade from 1842, the colony's executioner Alexander Green was allowed to build a cottage at his own expense on land just outside the eastern wall. He lived in a whitewashed hut on the rise that locals came to know by his trade, the ground that is now Green Park beside the gaol. Green carried out hundreds of executions across New South Wales in his long career, and few neighbours can have been more closely tied to the work going on behind the wall at his back.

chapters.heading: The gallows, 1852
chapters.body: The first hangings came quickly. On 29 October 1841 Robert Hudson and George Stroud died before a crowd of several hundred in the gaol yard, and for a time the worst spectacles were staged on a makeshift gallows out in Forbes Street, where John Knatchbull dropped before a vast crowd in February 1844. The last of those public hangings outside the gates was in 1852, after which executions retreated inside the walls and went on into the new century. Over the gaol's life around 76 to 79 men and women died on its gallows, among them the bushranger Captain Moonlite in 1880 and Louisa Collins in 1889, the last woman hanged in New South Wales.

images.caption: The gallows at Darlinghurst Gaol, Illustrated Sydney News, 1883 (State Library of NSW, public domain)

chapters.heading: Starvinghurst, 1908
chapters.body: Not everyone behind the walls was a murderer. The poet Henry Lawson, by then famous but broke and drinking, was gaoled here seven times between 1905 and 1909, mostly for failing to pay maintenance to his estranged wife, for a total of about 159 days. Bitter at the meagre rations, he renamed the place 'Starvinghurst Gaol' in his 1908 poem 'One Hundred and Three', after the prison number he was given. The name stuck among the inmates.

images.caption: Henry Lawson, painted by John Longstaff in 1900, who called the gaol 'Starvinghurst' (Art Gallery of NSW / Wikimedia, public domain)

chapters.heading: Easels in the cells, 1922
chapters.body: In 1914 the prisoners were moved out to the new gaol at Long Bay and Darlinghurst was closed, though the empty cells were pressed back into service as an internment camp through the First World War. The buildings passed to the Education Department in 1921, and East Sydney Technical College opened in the cell blocks soon after. Students bent over microscopes, lathes and drawing boards in the very rooms where prisoners had once been locked down each night.

images.caption: A student at East Sydney Technical College, in the old gaol, 1949 (State Library of NSW, public domain)

chapters.heading: The National Art School, 1996
chapters.body: Art teaching had grown inside the old gaol for decades, and in 1996 the National Art School gained its independence and became the sole occupant of the site. Easels now crowd the sandstone cell blocks, sculpture fills the yards, and the tall circular chapel at the hub of the radial plan serves the school. Art students have by now occupied Darlinghurst far longer than many of its sentences ever ran.

images.caption: The former gaol today, as the National Art School (Wikimedia Commons, CC BY 3.0)

chapters.heading: A wheel for watching
chapters.body: The radial design borrowed from the reform thinking of the day, the idea that constant surveillance might reform a prisoner more surely than the lash. Six rectangular cell blocks fanned out from the central rotunda, so that a warder standing at the hub could in theory keep every wing in view. In practice the gaol was crammed far past its design, and the high sandstone walls came to define a slab of Darlinghurst the way few buildings define a Sydney suburb.

chapters.heading: Who the cells really held
chapters.body: The gallows draw the eye, but most who passed through Darlinghurst were not condemned at all. Debtors, drunks, petty thieves and the destitute filled the wings, along with women in their own quarters and people locked up simply for being poor or unwell. The Bulletin editor J. F. Archibald did a stint inside, as did Jimmy Governor before his hanging in 1901, and the everyday traffic of a hard nineteenth-century city moved steadily through the gates.

title: Parliament House & the Rum Hospital

chapters.heading: A hospital paid for in rum, 1816
chapters.body: Governor Macquarie wanted a proper hospital, but London refused to pay for it. So in November 1810 he struck a bargain with merchants Garnham Blaxcell and Alexander Riley and the principal surgeon D'Arcy Wentworth. They would build it at their own cost in return for a monopoly on importing 45,000 gallons of spirits into the thirsty colony. The 'Rum Hospital' rose on the Macquarie Street ridge between 1811 and 1816, three long verandahed Georgian buildings whose form is thought to echo the colonial architecture Macquarie had known during his army years in India.

images.caption: The Rum Hospital's southern wing, later the Mint, photographed in 1870, showing the verandahed Georgian style of the 1816 complex (State Library of NSW, public domain)

chapters.heading: Greenway's damning report, 1816
chapters.body: The contractors found the rum trade less lucrative than they had hoped, so they sublet the work and used poor materials. When the buildings opened in 1816 they were already failing, with subsidence, rising damp and roofs close to collapse. Macquarie sent the newly arrived Francis Greenway to inspect, and his blunt report described weak foundations, in places no foundations at all, and walls of rubble that sheltered rats and bedbugs. The report impressed Macquarie so much that he appointed Greenway the colony's first salaried Civil Architect on 30 March 1816, and Greenway's remedial work is part of why two wings still stand.

chapters.heading: Democracy moves in, 1829
chapters.body: The colony's first legislature, the Legislative Council created in 1824, needed a home, and on 21 August 1829 it began meeting in a downstairs room of the hospital's northern wing, the old surgeon's quarters. It was the largest public building in the colony at the time. When responsible government arrived in 1856, the first elected Parliament sat here too, and the debating chambers were built out around the original hospital fabric. As members of both houses have been reminded ever since, democracy in New South Wales began as a sub-tenant of a hospital paid for in rum.

chapters.heading: Gold and the Mint, 1855
chapters.body: The southern wing took a different road. When the gold rushes flooded the colony with raw gold, it was converted into the Royal Mint's first branch outside London, opening on 14 May 1855. In its first five years it struck more than a million pounds' worth of sovereigns and half-sovereigns every year, each carrying a small mint mark to show it had been made in Sydney. The Mint closed in January 1927, served decades as rotating government offices, and was restored as the headquarters of what is now Museums of History NSW.

images.caption: A gold sovereign struck at the Sydney Mint in 1855, its first year (State Library of NSW, public domain)

chapters.heading: The hospital that remained, 1894
chapters.body: The central building stayed a hospital, the colony's main one, descended from the First Fleet's tent hospital pitched along George Street in 1788. In March 1868 Lucy Osburn and five Nightingale-trained nurses arrived from London and began to remake Australian nursing. The decaying Rum Hospital centre was pulled down in 1879, and after long delays a grand sandstone Sydney Hospital opened on the site in 1894, still a working hospital today with the lucky bronze boar Il Porcellino at its gate.

images.caption: Thomas Rowe's Sydney Hospital, c.1900, on the site of the Rum Hospital's central building (State Library of NSW, public domain)

chapters.heading: A boar rubbed shiny for luck
chapters.body: Il Porcellino is a bronze copy of a famous Florentine fountain figure, given to the hospital in 1968 in memory of the surgeon Thomas Fiaschi. Coins dropped in the basin at its feet go to the hospital, and visitors rub the boar's snout for good health, leaving it polished gold against its darker body. It has become one of the small daily rituals of Macquarie Street, performed by people who often have no idea they are standing on the footprint of the old Rum Hospital.

chapters.heading: Older than the institutions inside them
chapters.body: Macquarie Street still carries this extraordinary run of survivors, Parliament, the Mint and Sydney Hospital, with the Hyde Park Barracks close by, buildings older than the parliaments, mints and hospitals that came to fill them. The Mint even lost its original wrought-iron gates, removed in the 1930s to ease the way for motor cars and acquired in 1937 by Barker College at Hornsby, where they still stand. When the Mint sought them back in the 1980s the college declined, so faithful iron copies were cast for the Macquarie Street frontage instead.

title: Conservatorium of Music

chapters.heading: A castle for the Governor's horses, 1817
chapters.body: On 16 December 1817 Governor Macquarie laid the foundation stone of a grand castellated stable block designed by his architect Francis Greenway. Battlemented walls and octagonal towers made a little Gothic fortress, meant to front a new Government House that Macquarie would never get to build. Completed in February 1821 at an estimated 9,000 pounds, it housed the colony's horses in more comfort than most people in Sydney enjoyed. Even Macquarie was uneasy at how grand it had become.

images.caption: Conrad Martens' 1842 painting of the castellated Government House stables in the Domain (State Library of NSW, public domain)

chapters.heading: From stables to Conservatorium, 1915
chapters.body: Nearly a century on, the stables found a new life. The State Government put 22,000 pounds towards converting the building, the open courtyard was roofed over to make an auditorium, and the new Conservatorium opened its doors on 6 April 1915. Teaching began on 6 March 1916 under its first director, the Belgian conductor Henri Verbrugghen. In 1919 the Conservatorium High School moved in, and musicians have been schooled in the old castle ever since.

images.caption: The Conservatorium from the air in 1937, ringed by the Royal Botanic Garden (State Records NSW, no known restrictions)

chapters.heading: The director who walked out, 1921
chapters.body: Verbrugghen was the Conservatorium's only salaried staff member at first, conducting an orchestra and a string quartet as well as running the school. When he asked the Government to pay him separately for his concert work and his teaching, the answer was to withdraw its subsidies for both the orchestra and the quartet. He resigned in 1921, after taking the Conservatorium orchestra on tour to Melbourne and New Zealand, and soon left for the United States. The institution he founded outlasted the quarrel that drove him away.

chapters.heading: The town beneath the floor, 2001
chapters.body: In March 1997 the State Government committed 69 million dollars to a redevelopment, designed by Government Architect Chris Johnson with the firm of Daryl Jackson, Robin Dyke and Robert Tanner. Before the new halls went up, three years of digging under the floor turned up colonial Sydney intact, including a cistern from the 1790s, a bakehouse from around 1800 to 1813, and a rubbish dump spanning 1840 to 1913. Today concerts in Verbrugghen Hall, built over the old stable yard, take place directly above a preserved slice of the early town.

images.caption: The Conservatorium today, the central roof covering Verbrugghen Hall, built over the old stable courtyard (Wikimedia Commons, public domain)

chapters.heading: The scandal that helped end Macquarie's Sydney
chapters.body: There was a problem. The colony's horses now had a Gothic castle, while the Governor himself still made do with the cramped, leaking cottage of 1789. The mismatch was irresistible to Macquarie's critics, and when Commissioner Bigge arrived to investigate the colony he objected to the stables in October 1819. He grumbled that the work was already too far advanced to stop without waste, and the building became a prime exhibit in the case against Macquarie's spending, part of what brought his ambitious programme and his governorship to an end.

images.caption: The Gothic stables in 1872, battlemented towers built for the Governor's horses (State Library of NSW, public domain)

chapters.heading: Greenway's Gothic, and whose idea it was
chapters.body: The fortress look was unusual for a working stable, and the credit is shared. Greenway had worked in England alongside the fashionable architect John Nash, and Elizabeth Macquarie, the Governor's wife, knew her architecture well, with a cousin who had been a pioneer of the Gothic Revival. Seen from the harbour, the battlemented stables, the new Government House and the squat guns of Fort Macquarie were meant to read as one romantic scene. Only the stables were ever finished to that vision.

chapters.heading: John Palmer's mill, hidden under the music
chapters.body: The bakehouse the archaeologists uncovered was no random ruin. Its foundations belonged to a mill and bakery worked from around 1800 by John Palmer, the colony's commissary, on land beside the early Government House. When the courtyard was roofed for Verbrugghen Hall in the 1910s, those footings were sealed underneath without anyone clearing them away. The remains of Palmer's enterprise now sit, quietly, beneath the floor where students perform.

chapters.heading: Carved into the State's memory
chapters.body: The building is one of the oldest surviving works of Greenway, the convict transported for forgery who became Macquarie's architect, and it is protected on the NSW State Heritage Register. The 2001 redevelopment tucked a concert hall, twin recital halls and around 130 practice rooms beneath landscaped roof gardens that restored a slice of the Botanic Garden above them. That careful blend of new music school and buried colonial fabric won an Australian Award for Urban Design Excellence in 2002.

title: Powerhouse Museum

chapters.heading: Born from a fire, 1880
chapters.body: The museum's roots reach back to the 1879 Sydney International Exhibition, held in the great domed Garden Palace above the Botanic Garden. A Technological, Industrial and Sanitary Museum opened there in 1880 with thousands of objects from the exhibition. On the morning of 22 September 1882 the timber palace burned to the ground in a few hours, taking almost the entire collection with it. The young museum had to begin again from a handful of charred survivors, among them a graphite elephant that fell five storeys and lived.

images.caption: The Garden Palace, built for the 1879 International Exhibition, the museum's first home, destroyed by fire in 1882 (State Library of NSW, public domain)

chapters.heading: A cathedral of brick and iron
chapters.body: The Ultimo Power Station was raised between 1897 and 1899 by the Department of Public Works, its long brick halls and tall chimneys a piece of industrial architecture as grand as any church of the day. Inside, American steam engines first turned the generators, later replaced by British turbo-alternators as the city's hunger for current grew. When the museum moved in, the engineers chose to keep the great spaces open rather than carve them up, so visitors still read the building as the machine it once was.

chapters.heading: The station that drove the trams, 1899
chapters.body: Meanwhile, on the Ultimo side of Darling Harbour, a great power station fired up in 1899, Sydney's first major one, built to drive the city's new electric tram network. From 1902 its current also swung open the Pyrmont Bridge across the bay, one of the first electrically powered swing spans in the world. For 64 years its boilers and turbines powered the trams and the inner city, until the last machines fell silent in 1963 and the cavernous building was left empty.

images.caption: The electric trams the Ultimo power station drove, King Street, Sydney, 1900 (State Library of NSW, public domain)

chapters.heading: Frigate Bird II, 1951
chapters.body: Hung high in the old boiler hall is the museum's most romantic exhibit, the Catalina flying boat Frigate Bird II. In 1951, with a crew of five and no rescue cover, she island-hopped from Rose Bay across the open Pacific to Chile, the first aircraft ever to cross the South Pacific to South America. Her captain, P. G. Taylor, gave her to the museum in 1961, and since being hoisted into the roof in 1987 she has flown, motionless, above the visitors.

images.caption: The Catalina flying boat Frigate Bird II, hung in the museum's roof (Wikimedia Commons, CC BY-SA 4.0)

chapters.heading: A museum in the powerhouse, 1988
chapters.body: The two stories met in 1988, when the museum moved out of its old Harris Street home and into the disused power station nearby, reopening as the Powerhouse Museum. The building became its own greatest exhibit. Among the engines and boilers now stands Locomotive No. 1, which hauled the colony's first passenger train to Parramatta in 1855, resting where the coal once burned.

images.caption: Locomotive No. 1, which hauled the colony's first passenger train in 1855, in the converted boiler house (Wikimedia Commons, CC BY-SA 3.0)

chapters.heading: Renewal, 2024
chapters.body: In the 2010s the government announced it would close the Ultimo museum and move the Powerhouse to Parramatta. A long public campaign fought to save it, and the plan was reversed. A second campus, Powerhouse Parramatta, would be built, while Ultimo would stay. In February 2024 the old power station closed for a renewal of around 300 million dollars, due to reopen in 2027, the museum made over once again, like the building it lives in.

chapters.heading: Joseph Maiden begins again
chapters.body: The man who rebuilt the museum from the ashes was Joseph Henry Maiden, later one of Australia's great botanists. After the 1882 fire he gathered a new collection in a temporary home in the Domain, an old exhibition annexe that doubled for a time as a makeshift shelter. By the time the purpose-built Technological Museum opened on Harris Street in 1893, designed by Government Architect W. E. Kemp, the institution had grown from a heap of scorched relics into one of the busiest museums in the colony.

chapters.heading: The Boulton and Watt engine
chapters.body: Deep in the collection sits one of the oldest working relics of the industrial age, a rotative steam engine built by Boulton and Watt in 1785. It pumped and ground at a London brewery and a flour mill before being shipped to Sydney, and it is thought to be the oldest surviving rotative steam engine of its kind anywhere. The Powerhouse fires it up under steam on occasion, so the heartbeat of the early factory age still turns over inside a former power house.

title: Sydney Observatory

chapters.heading: Fort Phillip on the hill, 1804
chapters.body: The hill above The Rocks was fortified long before anyone watched the stars from it. In 1804, shaken by the Castle Hill convict rising and fearing a French attack, Governor King began building Fort Phillip here, a work meant to command both the harbour and the town below. It was never properly finished or armed, and the high point instead became a signal and flagstaff station that relayed news of approaching ships to the colony. The fort was largely pulled down, but the hill kept the name Observatory Hill once its new building arrived.

images.caption: 'Fort Philip & the Signal Staff', the fort and flagstaff that once crowned the hill, watercolour c.1840s (State Library of NSW, public domain)

chapters.heading: The colony's clock, 1858
chapters.body: The Sydney Observatory was built on the hill between 1857 and 1859 as the seat of the NSW Government Astronomer, after Governor Sir William Denison chose the site. Its most public duty was timekeeping. Each day the big copper time ball on the tower was hauled up and dropped at one o'clock, its fall marked by a gun fired first from Dawes Point and later from Fort Denison, so that every ship in the harbour could set its chronometers by it. The ball still drops at 1pm today.

images.caption: Kerry & Co's photograph of the Observatory, c.1900, with the time ball atop the ivy-clad tower (Powerhouse Museum Collection / Wikimedia, public domain)

chapters.heading: Russell, the man who ran it longest
chapters.body: Henry Chamberlain Russell took charge as Government Astronomer in 1870 and held the post for some thirty-five years, the longest tenure the observatory ever had. He was Australian-born, a rarity among colonial scientists, and he turned the place into far more than a clock and a telescope. Under Russell the observatory began publishing weather maps in the daily papers from 1877, and his decision to commit the institution to the international star survey shaped its work for the next eighty years.

chapters.heading: Mapping the southern sky
chapters.body: Beyond keeping time, the observatory's astronomers did serious science. They observed the 1874 transit of Venus, and from the late 1880s, under Government Astronomer Henry Chamberlain Russell, the observatory joined the great international effort to photograph the entire sky. It took on its own assigned zone of the southern heavens, a painstaking survey of stars that ran for decades. The historic telescopes under its domes are among the oldest still in working order in Australia.

images.caption: An historic refracting telescope under one of the observatory's domes (Wikimedia Commons, CC BY 2.0)

chapters.heading: From science to museum, 1982
chapters.body: In the end the city defeated the stars. A century of growth wrapped the hill in light and smoke, and serious astronomy became impossible from the centre of Sydney. The observatory ended its scientific career in 1982 and passed to the Powerhouse Museum, reopening as a museum of astronomy. Visitors still climb the hill to look through its old telescopes by night, and at one o'clock each afternoon, the time ball still falls.

images.caption: Sydney Observatory today, on its hill above The Rocks (Wikimedia Commons, CC BY-SA 4.0)

chapters.heading: Windmill Hill before the stars
chapters.body: Long before either fort or fort, the high ground was simply where the colony ground its grain. In 1796 an Irish convict named John Davis raised a stone tower windmill on the summit, the first of several mills that gave the place its early name of Windmill Hill. The wind off the harbour was steady, but the exposed timber sails and machinery did not last, and the mills had fallen idle by the time the fort works began. The names tell the hill's story in order, from Windmill Hill to Flagstaff Hill to Observatory Hill.

chapters.heading: A clock built by William Weaver's plans
chapters.body: The sandstone building was drawn up by the Colonial Architect William Weaver, whose plans for an observatory and astronomer's residence Governor Denison approved in 1855. When work began the following year, the new Colonial Architect Alexander Dawson took over and built to those plans, finishing the job by 1859. The design married two Italian forms, the High Renaissance palazzo and the country villa, in a combination Sydney had not seen on this scale before.

chapters.heading: London machinery still on the job
chapters.body: The time ball is not just a daily show. Its dropping mechanism was made by Maudslay, Sons and Field of London in 1855 and has never been replaced. That makes Sydney one of only a handful of places in the world where a time ball still falls by its original Victorian gear. The first drop came at noon on 5 June 1858 before the signal was fixed at one o'clock, the hour ships' masters came to trust.

title: Capitol Theatre

chapters.heading: Belmore Markets, 1893
chapters.body: The Capitol began life as a market hall. The new Belmore Markets, built at Haymarket in 1893 to a design by the City Architect George McRae, were a long brick building of arched openings dressed with terracotta detail, where the city's produce was bought and sold and horse carts crowded the kerb. The trade never quite paid its way, and within twenty years the council was looking for something else to do with the place. It is one of the more unlikely beginnings for a theatre famous for its painted sky.

images.caption: The Belmore Markets, c.1900, the building that became the Capitol, photographed by Kerry & Co (State Library of NSW, no known restrictions)

chapters.heading: Circus in the markets, 1916
chapters.body: From 1916 the old market hall took a turn for the spectacular. Wirth Brothers' Circus moved in and had it rebuilt as a hippodrome, the work overseen by the architects R.H. Brodrick and J.H. Merriman, who kept much of McRae's brick and terracotta facing. The centrepiece was a ring that could be sunk and flooded from a great concrete tank, so seals and even elephants performed where market stalls had stood. For a decade audiences watched circus turns and water spectacles under the same roof.

chapters.heading: An atmospheric palace, 1928
chapters.body: In 1928 the building was reborn as the Capitol, one of the new atmospheric picture palaces, a style imported from the American designer John Eberson and realised here by the New Zealand-born architect Henry Eli White. Its auditorium was dressed as a Mediterranean garden, with classical statues and creeping vines around the walls, and overhead, instead of a ceiling, an open evening sky of drifting clouds and twinkling stars. White had crossed to the United States with the cinema chief Stuart Doyle to study the latest American houses before settling on Eberson's scheme. You watched the film as if from a warm courtyard in a kinder country.

images.caption: Uniformed ushers in the Capitol, c.1935, in its picture-palace heyday, photographed by Sam Hood (State Library of NSW, no known restrictions)

chapters.heading: The night it opened
chapters.body: The Capitol threw open its doors on Easter Saturday, 7 April 1928, with the silent feature His Lady starring John Barrymore and Dolores Costello. Patrons walked in off a grubby Haymarket street and found themselves seemingly outdoors in a moonlit Italian piazza, the stars overhead arranged to mimic the real southern sky. For Sydney in the late twenties it was the most lavish picture house the city had seen, and tickets sold hard through that first season.

chapters.heading: Saved and restarred, 1995
chapters.body: As cinema audiences dwindled the Capitol declined, scraping by as a live and rock venue from 1972 while demolition was proposed again and again. It survived, and after a reconstruction costing more than thirty million dollars it reopened on 24 January 1995, the Mediterranean garden rebuilt and the starlit ceiling switched back on. Today it stages major musicals at Haymarket and, with the State Theatre, is one of only two survivors of Sydney's picture-palace age, and the only atmospheric one left.

images.caption: The restored Capitol today, a working lyric theatre at Haymarket (Wikimedia Commons, CC BY-SA 4.0)

chapters.heading: Two openings in 1995
chapters.body: The restored Capitol came back to life in stages. It reopened on 24 January 1995 with the Italian revue Carisma Italiano fronted by the singer Mauro Gioia, a short season that tested the rebuilt house. The first of the big musicals followed that July, when Miss Saigon landed at Haymarket with its full stage machinery, helicopter and all, proving the theatre could carry the modern blockbuster shows it now lives on.

chapters.heading: How the sky was made
chapters.body: The illusion overhead was a piece of careful theatre engineering.
