To fit it in, White raised the roof trusses and stretched the slope of the gallery, then had the ceiling moulded into a smooth dark vault and pricked with small electric lamps for stars. A machine known as a Brenograph projected slow clouds drifting across the dome, so the painted heavens never quite sat still. The effect was meant to soften the boundary between the room and the night, and it still works on an audience today.

title: Australian Museum

chapters.heading: Founded on 200 pounds a year, 1827
chapters.body: The Australian Museum traces its beginning to a despatch sent in March 1827 by the Colonial Secretary, Earl Bathurst, to Governor Ralph Darling, committing 200 pounds a year to a public museum in New South Wales. It was the first museum in the country, and for years its growing pile of specimens was shuffled between borrowed rooms around the young town. The job could be deadly. The museum's first custodian, the English carpenter William Holmes, appointed zoologist in 1829, was accidentally shot dead while collecting bird specimens at Moreton Bay in 1831, barely two years into the role.

chapters.heading: A Greek temple for science, 1846
chapters.body: By the 1840s the museum had outgrown its temporary quarters and won a site on the corner of College and William Streets facing Hyde Park. Construction of a grand Greek Revival building in Sydney sandstone, designed by the Colonial Architect Mortimer Lewis, began in 1846. Its Long Gallery, opened to the public in May 1857, was the first purpose-built museum gallery in the country, and from the 1860s a sandstone wing by James Barnet addressing College Street roughly tripled the space, filling it with skeletons, minerals and specimens gathered from across the colony.

images.caption: The Australian Museum on College Street, c.1900, photographed by Henry King (State Library of NSW, public domain)

chapters.heading: The Long Gallery restored
chapters.body: The 1857 Long Gallery is still the oldest museum gallery in Australia, and in 2017 it reopened after a careful restoration as the Westpac Long Gallery. Its centrepiece exhibition, 200 Treasures of the Australian Museum, pairs one hundred remarkable objects with one hundred people who shaped the country. The timing was deliberate, marking the museum's 190th birthday and the bank's 200th in the same restored room.

chapters.heading: Carried out in his chair, 1874
chapters.body: The museum's most famous drama was its curator. Gerard Krefft, a gifted naturalist who corresponded with Darwin and accepted the new theory of evolution, fell out bitterly with his more conservative trustees. When a gold specimen went missing they had their excuse and dismissed him, but Krefft refused to go and barricaded himself in his museum residence. On 21 September 1874 a bailiff and two hired prizefighters removed him through the front door, still seated in his chair.

images.caption: Gerard Krefft, curator until 1874, sacked by his trustees and carried out the front door in his chair (State Library of NSW, public domain)

chapters.heading: A century on College Street
chapters.body: The classical building, watched over by the Captain Cook statue across the road in Hyde Park, has anchored College Street ever since, added to and reworked many times as the collections grew into the millions. After a major redevelopment known as Project Discover, the museum reopened at the end of 2020, nearly two centuries after it began as a scatter of curiosities funded with 200 pounds a year.

images.caption: The museum and the Captain Cook statue, seen from Hyde Park, c.1905 (State Library of NSW, no known restrictions)

chapters.heading: It was not always the Australian Museum
chapters.body: At its founding the institution had no settled name and was known variously as the Colonial Museum and the Sydney Museum. The title we use today was only fixed in June 1836, when a sub-committee resolved to call it the Australian Museum. By then it had taken on a more formal life, and an Act of 1853 placed it under a board of trustees, the body that would later clash so famously with Gerard Krefft.

chapters.heading: The bronze captain across the road
chapters.body: The Captain Cook statue that faces the museum is no older than the building's busiest years. Sculpted by the Pre-Raphaelite artist Thomas Woolner and cast in England, it was unveiled in Hyde Park on 25 February 1879 before a crowd estimated in the tens of thousands. The day was declared a public holiday, and the figure on its Moruya granite pedestal has stood opposite the College Street steps ever since, its colonial framing of Cook now openly debated.

title: State Theatre

chapters.heading: The Empire's greatest theatre, 1929
chapters.body: The State Theatre opened on Market Street on 7 June 1929, billed without modesty as 'the Empire's greatest theatre'. It was a picture palace built at the very peak of the form, with seating for more than two thousand and a style that threw Gothic, baroque and Hollywood glamour together without apology. On opening night the crowds packed the footpath beneath the blazing marquee to see Emil Jannings in Ernst Lubitsch's 'The Patriot', with Price Dunlavy at the Wurlitzer.

images.caption: Opening night at the State Theatre, 1929, photographed by Sam Hood (State Library of NSW, public domain)

chapters.heading: Home of the Film Festival, 1974
chapters.body: Since 1974 the State has been the home of the Sydney Film Festival, which fills the auditorium for a few weeks every June, the grandest possible room in which to watch a film. Live concerts and award nights crowd its calendar the rest of the year, and the old picture palace, built for silent-era spectacle, plays on.

images.caption: The State's marquee for the Sydney Film Festival, which it has hosted since 1974 (Wikimedia Commons, CC BY-SA 2.0)

chapters.heading: The green ban that saved it
chapters.body: When redevelopment loomed in the 1970s the National Trust classified the building in 1978 and the campaign to keep it drew on builders as much as on conservationists. The 1980 restoration brought the State back to life, and it reopened with Bette Midler in the concert film 'Divine Madness'. Formal protection came at last in 1999, when the theatre was entered on the NSW State Heritage Register.

chapters.heading: Gothic vaults
chapters.body: The whole building was designed so that a patron need never stop being amazed between the street and the screen. The Grand Foyer is pure theatrical Gothic, with a ceiling of intricate fan-vaulting, walls hung with bronze and suits of armour, and great lanterns for light. Below it the basement once held a ballroom that ran as a popular dance venue for decades.

images.caption: The Gothic fan-vaulted Grand Foyer, lined with armour and bronze (Wikimedia Commons, public domain)

chapters.heading: The Koh-i-Noor chandelier
chapters.body: At the heart of the building is the Grand Assembly, a great domed hall from which hangs the theatre's crowning glory. The Koh-i-Noor chandelier is a mass of cut crystal weighing more than four tonnes, and when it was hung it ranked as the second largest in the world. It still presides over the marble staircase and gilded dome, glittering above the crowds the way it did in 1929.

images.caption: The Grand Assembly and its Koh-i-Noor chandelier, beneath the gilded dome (Wikimedia Commons, CC0)

chapters.heading: The last picture palace
chapters.body: Between the wars Sydney built a string of these dream palaces, the Regent, the Prince Edward, the Trocadero among them, and then, as television emptied the cinemas, bulldozed almost all of them. The State very nearly went the same way. It was the centre of one of Sydney's great conservation battles in the early 1970s, and it survives today as essentially the only one of the great inter-war picture palaces left, so that to step inside is to step back into the cinema-going 1930s.

images.caption: The State's neon sign on Market Street today (Wikimedia Commons, CC BY-SA 4.0)

chapters.heading: Stuart Doyle's million-pound gamble
chapters.body: The State was the dream of Stuart Doyle, the showman who ran Union Theatres, and he set Sydney architect Henry Eli White to design it with help from the great American picture-palace architect John Eberson. Union bought the Market Street site, the former home of the Evening News, in 1926 and built from 1926 to 1929. The budget began at four hundred thousand pounds and finished near a million, a staggering sum, and within two years Union had collapsed into liquidation and the theatre passed to Greater Union.

chapters.heading: A tower of shops above the stalls
chapters.body: The auditorium was only part of what rose on Market Street. Above it stood the State Shopping Block, eleven storeys of around a hundred and fifty shops served by a row of lifts, so the building worked as a small vertical arcade as well as a cinema. The shops were later converted to offices, but the structure still binds the picture palace into the everyday commercial life of the city block.

chapters.heading: The mighty Wurlitzer
chapters.body: Hidden behind the proscenium is a 21-rank Wurlitzer organ, the kind of instrument that once supplied music and sound effects for the silent films the State was built to show. For most of the late twentieth century it sat largely silent. A restoration that ran for ten years was finished in 2019, and the organ that played on opening night in 1929 can once again rise and sound in the room it was built for.

title: Old Government House, Parramatta

chapters.heading: Where the colony was saved, 1788
chapters.body: Within months of the First Fleet landing, with Sydney's sandy soil failing, Governor Phillip pushed inland and founded a farm settlement at Rose Hill, renamed Parramatta in 1791. Its richer river-flat soils produced the colony's first real harvests and quite simply saved it from starvation. For decades Parramatta's farms mattered more than Sydney's harbour, and the governors needed a residence out here, on the rise where the town began.

images.caption: Joseph Lycett's view of Parramatta from May's Hill, c.1824, the inland town whose farms fed the colony (Joseph Lycett, public domain)

chapters.heading: The governors' country house, 1799
chapters.body: A first cottage went up around 1790. Governor Hunter cleared away its remains and rebuilt in brick in 1799, and that Georgian core still stands today, the oldest surviving public building on the Australian mainland and older than anything in Macquarie Street. Ten of the early governors used it as their country house. For several of them, with the colony's food and its convict farms concentrated here, Parramatta was effectively the capital.

images.caption: Government House, Parramatta, painted in 1838 (State Library of NSW, public domain)

chapters.heading: Macquarie's house in the park, 1815
chapters.body: Governor and Mrs Macquarie made it their own. From about 1815 the house was enlarged to a design by the military engineer Lieutenant John Watts, gaining the two-storey block and side pavilions it wears now, while a timber portico added soon after is credited to the convict architect Francis Greenway. Elizabeth Macquarie reshaped the surrounding grounds along picturesque lines, the beginnings of today's Parramatta Park. It was the grandest house in the colony, set in its own sweep of parkland by the river.

images.caption: Old Government House in 1903, by then no longer a vice-regal residence (no known copyright restrictions)

chapters.heading: A death in the grounds, 1847
chapters.body: The house met its quiet end through tragedy. On 7 December 1847, Lady Mary FitzRoy, wife of Governor Sir Charles FitzRoy, was killed when the horses bolted and her carriage overturned in the grounds. After her death the governors used Parramatta less and less, preferring the new Government House at Bennelong Point in Sydney. An obelisk raised to Lady FitzRoy still stands in the park, and the conservation of that memorial was completed in 2010, the same year the house won World Heritage status.

chapters.heading: The oldest public building, 2010
chapters.body: The governors gave the house up in the 1850s, and it was let to a private tenant in 1856. For more than a century afterward it passed through schools and lessees, slowly fading, with The King's School occupying it from 1910. The National Trust took it on in 1967 and restored it as a museum, and in 2010 it was inscribed on the UNESCO World Heritage List among the Australian Convict Sites. The plain brick house on the hill where Parramatta, and arguably the colony itself, survived its first hungry years still stands in its park.

images.caption: Old Government House today, in Parramatta Park (Wikimedia Commons, CC BY-SA 3.0)

chapters.heading: The Burramattagal and the river bend
chapters.body: The house sits on a low rise within a bend of the river the colonists called the domain, ground that belonged to the Burramattagal clan of the Darug people. The very name Parramatta comes from a Burramattagal word for the place where eels lie down, or the head of the waters, describing the tidal limit of the river. The farms that fed the colony were carved out of country these families had fished and burned for thousands of years, and the choice of this spot owed much to the freshwater and rich flats they had long known.

chapters.heading: Built by convict hands
chapters.body: Convicts quarried the stone, burned the lime and laid the bricks for Hunter's house, and the work was not always smooth. In June 1799 a fierce southerly storm tore through the partly finished building and forced the brickwork to be rebuilt. The result is one of the clearest survivals of the convict labour that raised early Sydney, which is precisely why it sits on the World Heritage list rather than simply a local register.

title: University of Sydney

chapters.heading: Australia's first university, 1850
chapters.body: The University of Sydney was founded by an Act of the colonial parliament in 1850, the first university in Australia. From the start it was meant to be open to students whatever their religion, a deliberately secular and non-residential institution at a time when Oxford and Cambridge were still bound to the Church of England. Lectures began on 11 October 1852 in a borrowed building in the town, now part of Sydney Grammar School, before the university moved out to its own grounds on a hill at Grose Farm to the west.

images.caption: The University and its Great Hall, c.1900, photographed by Henry King (State Library of NSW, no known restrictions)

chapters.heading: Blacket's colonial Oxford, 1862
chapters.body: The architect Edmund Blacket gave the young colony a dream of Oxford rendered in honey-coloured Sydney sandstone. His Great Hall, opened in July 1859 with the first conferring of degrees, has a soaring timber hammerbeam roof modelled on Westminster Hall, and the first range of his Gothic quadrangle was complete by 1862. Look closely among the gargoyles and grotesques and you will find not just medieval monsters but distinctly Australian creatures, including a kangaroo perched on the clocktower and kookaburras over a doorway.

images.caption: Blacket's Gothic interior, vaulted sandstone and a carved grotesque on the stair (Wikimedia Commons, CC BY 2.0)

chapters.heading: Women admitted, 1881
chapters.body: In 1881 the university senate voted to admit women on the same terms as men, among the earliest such decisions anywhere in the world and decades ahead of Oxford and Cambridge. The first women to take the degree of Bachelor of Arts, Mary Brown and Isola Thompson, graduated in 1885, and Thompson became the first woman to take a Master of Arts in 1887. The sandstone quad that had been built to copy an exclusively male English tradition became something its models were not.

chapters.heading: The first medical women
chapters.body: The university went further than admitting women to arts degrees. In 1884 its medical school enrolled the first women medical students at any Australian university, opening a profession that had been closed to them. Within a generation Sydney women were practising as doctors, a direct consequence of the senate's quiet decision of 1881.

chapters.heading: The jacaranda
chapters.body: After the First World War the quadrangle gained a memorial carillon, unveiled on Anzac Day 1928 and rung out over the courtyard ever since. That same year the German professor and keen horticulturist Eben Gowrie Waterhouse planted a row of jacarandas in the quad, and one of them became the university's emblem and the heart of a student superstition. If you haven't started studying by the time the jacaranda blooms purple in November, the saying goes, it's already too late.

images.caption: The quadrangle jacaranda in bloom, November 2019 (Wikimedia Commons, CC BY-SA 4.0)

chapters.heading: The tree that fell, 2016
chapters.body: On the night of 28 October 2016 the famous jacaranda toppled in the quad, its roots eaten away by a fungal infection after 88 years. Staff had seen it coming and taken cuttings, and in July 2017 a clone grown from the old tree was planted in the same spot. An Illawarra flame tree was planted nearby at the same time, so the ritual continues with two trees instead of one.

chapters.heading: A carillon for the fallen
chapters.body: The carillon high in the clocktower commemorates the 197 students, graduates and staff of the university who died in the First World War. Its forty-nine bells were cast by John Taylor and Company of Loughborough in England and arrived in Sydney in March 1928, paid for by donations gathered from families and faculties across the country. The instrument was rebuilt and enlarged to fifty-four bells in 1973, and a carillonist still plays it live over the quadrangle each week.

title: Anzac Memorial

chapters.heading: Eighteen years in the making
chapters.body: Fundraising for a memorial to the New South Wales war dead began on 25 April 1916, the first anniversary of the Gallipoli landing, while the war still had more than two years to run. A single collection day in August 1918 brought in over forty-five thousand pounds, yet money, argument and the Great Depression then stalled the project for the better part of two decades. Bruce Dellit won the design competition launched in 1929, and his memorial was finally opened in Hyde Park on 24 November 1934 by the Duke of Gloucester, before a crowd estimated at one hundred thousand.

images.caption: The opening of the Anzac Memorial, 24 November 1934 (State Library of NSW, public domain)

chapters.heading: A wider remembrance, 2018
chapters.body: In 1984 the memorial was rededicated to all Australians who have served in war, not only the fallen of 1914-18. In 2018 a long-intended southern extension was finally built, running down to the Pool of Reflection, with a Hall of Service holding soil gathered from across New South Wales. There is a sample for each of the more than seventeen hundred towns, suburbs and districts that sent its people away.

images.caption: The memorial and its Pool of Reflection in Hyde Park (Wikimedia Commons, CC BY-SA 4.0)

chapters.heading: Earth from the front, set in the floor
chapters.body: Long before the soil of 2018 was gathered, Dellit had already brought the battlefields home. Into the floors of the Hall of Memory he set four stones carried back from Gallipoli, France, Palestine and New Guinea, arranged in the shape of the Rising Sun badge of the Australian Imperial Force. A visitor walking the hall therefore stands, quite literally, on ground from the places where these soldiers fought and died.

chapters.heading: 120,000 golden stars
chapters.body: Dellit's design is one of the finest works of art deco monumentality in the country, a stepped tower of pink granite rising about thirty metres above a reflecting pool, its lines stripped and severe. Inside, beneath a white marble hall, the great dome is set with 120,000 small gold stars, one for every man and woman of New South Wales who embarked for service in the First World War. It was conceived not as a monument to victory but as a place to reckon with loss.

images.caption: The illuminated memorial at night, c.1934, photographed by Sam Hood (State Library of NSW, public domain)

chapters.heading: Hoff's Sacrifice
chapters.body: Beneath the dome stands the sculptor Rayner Hoff's bronze masterpiece, 'Sacrifice': the naked body of a dead young soldier, borne up on a shield and sword by three grieving women, his mother, his sister and his wife with a child. You do not look up to him in triumph. You look down on him from a gallery above, into the well of mourning. Hoff designed two more sculptures for the building that were never made, and his anti-war group 'The Crucifixion of Civilisation', a nude female figure on a cross, was denounced by churchmen as blasphemous and quietly dropped.

images.caption: Rayner Hoff's bronze 'Sacrifice' beneath the dome, seen from the floor of the Hall of Silence (Wikimedia Commons, CC BY-SA 4.0)

chapters.heading: A star for two shillings
chapters.body: The dome of stars was as much an act of public mourning as of fundraising. During the depths of the Depression, with the building fund short, the returned soldiers' league sold the 120,000 stars to the public at two shillings each, so that ordinary families could buy a star for a son or husband who had served. Each star is plaster, gilded and glued into place, and the gesture made the ceiling a register of names paid for one at a time.

chapters.heading: A poplar avenue and a martial pool
chapters.body: The long Pool of Reflection that leads to the memorial was no accident of landscaping. Dellit lined it with Lombardy poplars, the tall slender trees that grew along the roads and canals of the Western Front, so that the approach itself would call France to mind. The pool runs about fifty metres toward the steps, and the eternal flame above the water keeps the memorial in conversation with the trenches its dead had known.

title: Art Gallery of New South Wales

chapters.heading: The Dobell case
chapters.body: William Dobell won the 1943 Archibald Prize with a gaunt, elongated portrait of his friend Joshua Smith, and the painting touched off the most famous art controversy in Australian history. Two losing entrants, Mary Edwards and Joseph Wolinski, sued in 1944, claiming the work was a caricature rather than a portrait and so ineligible. With modern art itself effectively in the witness box, Dobell, defended by the future Chief Justice Garfield Barwick, won when Justice Roper ruled on 8 November 1944 that it was indeed a portrait.

chapters.heading: Sydney Modern
chapters.body: In December 2022 the gallery roughly doubled in size, opening a second building, the Sydney Modern Project, on the far side of a road gully and linked to the old temple across the land. Designed by Kazuyo Sejima and Ryue Nishizawa of the Japanese firm SANAA, the new wing steps down in light-filled terraces toward the harbour. In April 2024 the two halves were given Gadigal names, Naala Nura for the original sandstone gallery and Naala Badu for the new one. A century and a quarter on, Vernon's unfinished temple finally had its other half.

images.caption: The Sydney Modern building (Naala Badu), opened 2022 (Wikimedia Commons, CC BY-SA 4.0)

chapters.heading: A temple of art on the Domain
chapters.body: The gallery began with the New South Wales Academy of Art, formed at a public meeting in Sydney on 24 April 1871, and it opened to the public as the Art Gallery of New South Wales on 22 September 1880. For its permanent home the government architect Walter Liberty Vernon designed a strictly classical sandstone temple on the edge of the Domain, built in stages from 1896. The first picture courts opened in May 1897, Vernon's oval entrance vestibule followed in 1902, and the columned front was finished in 1909.

images.caption: The National Art Gallery, c.1900-1910, with Vernon's classical sandstone wings (State Library of NSW, public domain)

chapters.heading: Permanently unfinished
chapters.body: Look closely at the front and you are looking at a building that was never finished. Of the six great bronze relief panels of the epochs of art proposed by the sculptor Percival Ball in 1899, only four were ever cast, showing Assyria, Egypt, Greece and Rome. Ball died in London in April 1900 before the first could be cast, and the niches meant for the Gothic and Renaissance periods stand empty to this day. Out front, two works were completed, Gilbert Bayes' allegorical bronze horsemen 'The Offerings of Peace' and 'The Offerings of War', installed in 1926.

images.caption: Gilbert Bayes' bronze 'The Offerings of War' (1926) before the gallery's facade (Wikimedia Commons, CC BY-SA 4.0)

chapters.heading: From the art barn to the temple
chapters.body: Before Vernon's sandstone galleries rose, the collection was housed in a plain brick building on the same Domain site, completed in December 1885 to a design by the architect John Horbury Hunt. Its thick walls and sawtooth roof drew open scorn, and critics nicknamed it the art barn. Vernon's classical front was built to mask and replace Hunt's utilitarian sheds, and parts of that older fabric survived behind the scenes for decades.

chapters.heading: The names carved above the empty niches
chapters.body: Above the bronze relief panels runs a frieze of artists' names cut into the sandstone, chosen by Frederick Eccleston Du Faur, a founder of the gallery and president of its trustees from 1892 to 1915. Curiously, the names on the entablature do not line up with the historical periods shown in the reliefs below them. The mismatch is a quiet reminder that the facade was assembled piecemeal over years, by different hands and to a design that was always meant to grow grander than it did.

chapters.heading: The grand court that never came
chapters.body: Vernon did not see his building as complete. He drew a far larger scheme reaching back from the oval vestibule, crowned by a great central court that would have given the gallery a monumental heart. The trustees never accepted those plans, and later proposals in the Depression years of the 1930s stalled as well. For more than a century the entrance hall opened not onto Vernon's intended court but onto galleries grafted on as money and need allowed.

title: Elizabeth Bay House

chapters.heading: Macleay's garden came first, 1826
chapters.body: In 1826 Governor Darling granted the newly arrived Colonial Secretary, Alexander Macleay, fifty-four acres running down to Elizabeth Bay. Macleay was an entomologist of real standing who had spent decades as secretary of the Linnean Society in London, and he knew his plants. With assigned convict labour and the Scottish gardener Robert Henderson, whom he had recruited at the Cape of Good Hope, he began laying out terraced lawns, winding gravel walks and a vast collection of exotic plantings above the water. The garden was the toast of the colony years before he built a house grand enough to stand in it.

images.caption: Elizabeth Bay from Darling Point, c.1900, photographed by Henry King (State Library of NSW, no known restrictions)

chapters.heading: The finest house in the colony, 1839
chapters.body: For the house Macleay engaged John Verge, the most fashionable architect of the day, who drew an elegant villa in the Greek Revival manner and built it between 1835 and 1839. The Macleays moved in during 1839, and the place was at once acclaimed the finest house in the colony, a verdict that has rarely been challenged since. But the combined cost of house and garden was ruinous, and Macleay had already lost the Colonial Secretary's salary when he was forced out of the post in 1837.

images.caption: Elizabeth Bay House in the 1930s, by then hemmed in by apartment blocks built over its old garden (RAHS / Wikimedia, no known restrictions)

chapters.heading: Ruin and rebirth
chapters.body: The grandeur was Macleay's undoing. With debts mounting in the depression of the 1840s, in 1845 he handed the house to his son William Sharp Macleay in return for the son taking on his debts, and the two were estranged over the bargain. The family held the property until the last of them left in 1903, the great garden was carved up for the apartment blocks of Elizabeth Bay, and in 1941 the house itself was divided into about fifteen flats. It was rescued and opened as a house museum in 1977.

chapters.heading: The oval saloon
chapters.body: At the heart of the house sits its masterpiece, a domed oval saloon from which a cantilevered staircase curves upward in a single sweep, seeming to hang unsupported beneath a glazed cupola that floods the space with light. It is still routinely called the finest colonial interior in Australia, the high point of John Verge's career and of Georgian taste in the colony. Recent study has even found that at the winter solstice the rising sun runs straight through the house, from the front door to the back.

images.caption: The cantilevered staircase rising beneath the saloon dome (State Library of NSW, public domain)

chapters.heading: The house that was never finished
chapters.body: Verge's design called for a single-storey Doric colonnade to wrap the harbour front, a feature that appears in Conrad Martens's watercolours of the villa. The money ran out before it could be built, so the morning room's French doors opened onto a sheer drop instead of a shaded verandah. The small portico seen today was only added in 1893, after a later resident, Lady Macleay, feared a guest might step through the doors and fall. For all the splendour of its interior, the outside of Elizabeth Bay House has stayed unfinished to this day.

chapters.heading: A naturalist's dynasty
chapters.body: Alexander Macleay brought with him a library of some four thousand volumes and one of the largest private insect collections in the world, the very obsession that helped sink him financially. His son William Sharp Macleay, who lived on alone in the house until his death in 1865, was himself a noted naturalist and added to it. The Macleay collection passed down through the family to William John Macleay, and in 1888 was given to the University of Sydney, where it became the founding treasure of the Macleay Museum.

chapters.heading: Saved by the public
chapters.body: By the 1930s the gardens had vanished under flats, the kitchen wing had been pulled down, and during 1940 and 1941 the house was carved into bedsitters and small apartments. Demolition was a real prospect until the Cumberland County Council took it on, and a long campaign to restore the villa followed. Today it is run by Museums of History NSW as a house museum, furnished to evoke the Macleays' years and standing as one of the few grand colonial villas left on the harbour.

title: State Library of New South Wales

chapters.heading: A members' library, 1826
chapters.body: The State Library traces an unbroken line back to February 1826, when a group of well-off Sydney men met to form the Australian Subscription Library and Reading Room, the colony's first. Members pooled their fees to order books, newspapers and periodicals from agents in London, so that Sydney could read what Europe was reading. By 1869 the venture was deep in debt, and the colonial government bought it out for 5,100 pounds, opening the Sydney Free Public Library that September with some 20,000 volumes. It was renamed the Public Library of New South Wales in 1895 as the reading rooms and holdings grew.

chapters.heading: The Mitchell Library, 1910
chapters.body: The library's character was transformed by one obsessive collector. David Scott Mitchell spent a fortune amassing the greatest private collection of Australiana ever assembled, and in 1898 he offered it to the colony on the condition that a proper building be raised to hold it. He died in July 1907, leaving the trustees his collection and an endowment of 70,000 pounds. The grand sandstone Mitchell wing on Macquarie Street, designed by Government Architect Walter Liberty Vernon, opened in March 1910, and with it the library became the keeping-place of the nation's documentary memory.

images.caption: The Mitchell Library reading room, c.1911, soon after the wing opened (State Library of NSW, public domain)

chapters.heading: Two hundred years, 2026
chapters.body: It took the name State Library of New South Wales in 1975, and in 1988 the modern Macquarie Street Wing opened, dedicated by Queen Elizabeth II to a design by Andrew Andersons. The reading rooms remain in daily use, the old Subscription Library now a research library serving millions. In 2026 the institution marks two hundred years, Australia's oldest library, in an unbroken line from a small members' collection of 1826.

images.caption: The Mitchell reading room today, still in daily use (Wikimedia Commons, CC BY-SA 3.0)

chapters.heading: The nation's memory
chapters.body: What it holds is extraordinary, the richest trove of Australiana anywhere, from First Fleet journals and explorers' diaries to convict records and the papers of colonial life. Among its treasures is the Tasman Map, drawn in 1644 at Batavia to record the Dutch navigator Abel Tasman's two voyages around the coast of New Holland. A great marble mosaic copy of it is set into the floor of the library's entrance vestibule, so visitors walk in across one of the earliest charts to take in the shape of the continent.

images.caption: The Tasman Map of 1644, one of the library's treasures, with a marble copy set into the entrance floor (Wikimedia Commons, CC BY-SA 4.0)

chapters.heading: The recluse who collected a nation
chapters.body: David Scott Mitchell was a wealthy bachelor who lived quietly in Darlinghurst and turned almost his entire life to the chase. From the 1880s he set himself the impossible goal of owning a copy of every document touching Australia, the south-west Pacific, the East Indies and Antarctica. He refused to be interviewed and would not even sit for a portrait, so the man behind the great gift remains a shadowy figure. By the time the wing that bears his name opened in 1910 it held around 60,000 volumes, along with manuscripts, maps, prints and pictures.

chapters.heading: How the Tasman Map came to Sydney
chapters.body: The original map is no ordinary chart. It was hand-drawn and decorated on Japanese gampi paper, and it carried the date 1644 and the arms of Amsterdam. Prince Roland Bonaparte, a great-nephew of Napoleon and an enthusiastic anthropologist, bought it in 1891, and his daughter, Princess George of Greece, presented it to the Mitchell Library in 1933. It arrived in Sydney to considerable fanfare, and is now often called the Bonaparte Tasman Map.

chapters.heading: Marble, war and the Melocco brothers
chapters.body: The mosaic in the vestibule was not laid when the Mitchell wing first opened. It was made between 1939 and 1941 by the Melocco brothers, Sydney's leading marble and terrazzo craftsmen, using Wombeyan marble chosen partly because European stone could not be had during the war. The brothers set 45 slabs of marble with coloured granules and inlays of brass, framed by cherubs holding navigation instruments to stand for the four winds. It was unveiled in 1942 with the grand central extensions, the sandstone portico and the main reading room.

chapters.heading: The Dixson gift and the sandstone front
chapters.body: Mitchell's bequest set off a chain of giving. Sir William Dixson, a Sydney businessman and collector, offered his own pictures and Australiana on the same terms, that a wing be built to hold them, and the Dixson wing on the south-west corner was finished in 1929. The central section, with the Tasman vestibule and the long sandstone facade on Macquarie Street, was completed in 1942, and a final sandstone section followed on the south-east corner in 1964. The grand classical front that visitors see today was therefore built in stages across more than half a century.

title: The Strand Arcade

chapters.heading: A glass-roofed street of shops, 1892
chapters.body: The Strand Arcade opened on 1 April 1892, a covered shopping street cut clean through the block from Pitt Street to George Street. It ran 104 metres on three galleried levels, lit by gas and electric light, served by hydraulic lifts, and roofed in tinted glass that was meant to soften the hard Sydney glare. The architect John B. Spencer designed it with his partner Charles Fairfax, and the builders Bignell and Clark put it up over 1890-1892 for the tobacco merchant Hugh Dixson, filling the galleries with jewellers, tailors, milliners and tea rooms behind cedar-framed windows.

images.caption: The Strand's galleries beneath its glass roof, with the tiled floor and cedar shopfronts (Wikimedia Commons, CC BY-SA 4.0)

chapters.heading: A shop that outlasted everything
chapters.body: The Nut Shop opened in the arcade in 1939 and is still trading from the same spot, roasting and selling nuts the way it always has. It is one of a long line of stubbornly old-fashioned tenants, alongside bootmakers, engravers and antique dealers who stayed for decades. That continuity is part of why the building matters, because few city retail spaces anywhere in Australia have kept their trade and their look together for so long.

chapters.heading: Fire and rescue, 1976
chapters.body: In the early hours of 25 May 1976 a fire broke out and tore through the arcade, gutting much of it, especially the George Street end. Twelve fire crews fought it for hours, and demolition loomed. In a city that had already torn down every one of its sibling arcades, that could easily have been the end. Instead the owner, Prudential Assurance, paid for a careful restoration that rebuilt the galleries, staircases, hydraulic lifts and cedar shopfronts close to Spencer's original drawings, and the Strand reopened in 1977.

images.caption: The George Street facade, 'THE STRAND 1891', among the modern city (Wikimedia Commons, CC BY-SA 4.0)

chapters.heading: Listed for the nation it remembers
chapters.body: In 1985 a plaque went up in the arcade marking it as the last surviving example of a kind of shopping street once common in Sydney. The recognition went further on 13 December 2011, when the Strand was added to the New South Wales State Heritage Register. It is valued as the only intact Victorian shopping arcade left in the city, a working museum of nineteenth-century retail that people still walk through to buy a watch strap or a cup of tea.

chapters.heading: The last Victorian arcade
chapters.body: Sydney once had a string of these grand glass-roofed arcades. The Royal and Sydney Arcades came first in the early 1880s, the Victoria Arcade followed late that decade, and the Imperial Arcade opened the year before the Strand. One by one they were demolished, and the Strand is the only one left in something close to its original form. Its galleries have kept the city's watchmakers, jewellers, milliners and tailors in business through every retail age since the gaslight era.

chapters.heading: The finest thoroughfare in the colonies
chapters.body: When the Strand opened, more than 600 guests were invited and barely a soul stayed away. The newspapers called it the finest public thoroughfare in the Australian colonies, and there was real pride in the modern touches, including electric lamps paired with gas jets in every chandelier so the lights would still work if one supply failed. The site itself had a past: the old White Horse Hotel was pulled down in July 1890 to make room for it.

title: Anzac Bridge: Three Crossings of Johnstons Bay

chapters.heading: A timber crossing for the abattoirs
chapters.body: The first bridge across Johnstons Bay went up in the early 1860s as part of a scheme to push Sydney's slaughtering trade out of the town and onto Glebe Island. Opened in 1862, it was a long timber beam structure built largely from Tasmanian blackbutt, which earned it the nickname Blackbutt Bridge. A small hand cranked swing span near the Pyrmont shore could be wound open by muscle alone to let shipping pass.

images.caption: Charles Pickering photographed the first timber Glebe Island Bridge for the New South Wales government in 1871.

chapters.heading: Percy Allan's electric swing bridge
chapters.body: By the turn of the century the timber bridge was worn out, and the Public Works Department engineer Percy Allan designed a sturdier replacement using his patented Allan truss. Built between 1899 and 1903 by the contractor H. McKenzie and Sons, it carried two swing spans that pivoted on a central pier, powered by electricity drawn from the nearby Ultimo power station. It was opened on 1 July 1903 by Lily See, the daughter of Premier John See, and was among the first electrically operated swing bridges anywhere in the world.

chapters.heading: A bottleneck on the western edge
chapters.body: Allan's bridge served Victoria Road traffic for most of the twentieth century, and it survived major works in the 1930s. The problem was that every time a ship needed to reach Blackwattle Bay the swing spans had to open, shutting a busy arterial road and stacking up cars. As Sydney's western traffic swelled, that arrangement became untenable, and planners settled on a high level fixed crossing that ships could simply sail beneath.

chapters.heading: The cable-stayed span of 1995
chapters.body: The new bridge was designed by a Roads and Traffic Authority team led by chief bridge engineer Ray Wedgwood and built by Baulderstone for about 170 million dollars. Its 345 metre main span hangs from two slender concrete towers on a fan of steel cables, and at its opening on 3 December 1995 it was the longest cable-stayed span in Australia. The deck was cast in heavy segments and launched out over the water, with land based counterweights anchoring each side.

images.caption: The cable-stayed bridge carries eight lanes plus a footpath and cycleway across Johnstons Bay.

chapters.heading: From Glebe Island to Anzac
chapters.body: The bridge opened under its old name, Glebe Island Bridge, but in 1998 Premier Bob Carr chose to mark the eightieth anniversary of the First World War armistice by renaming it the Anzac Bridge on Remembrance Day, 11 November. The change honoured the soldiers of the Australian and New Zealand Army Corps. The Australian flag was raised on the eastern pylon and the New Zealand flag on the western one, so the two nations stand at opposite ends of the span.

chapters.heading: Two soldiers keep watch
chapters.body: In 2000 a bronze digger by the sculptor Alan Somerville was placed at the eastern approach, his Lee-Enfield rifle reversed in the mourning pose known as rest on arms reverse. A matching New Zealand soldier, also by Somerville and made a touch taller than his Australian counterpart, was unveiled on 27 April 2008 by New Zealand Prime Minister Helen Clark alongside NSW Premier Morris Iemma. The old swing bridge still sits below, locked permanently open and heritage listed, dwarfed by the span that replaced it.

images.caption: The disused 1903 swing bridge sits low on the water beneath its towering replacement.

title: Bare Island: The Fort That Never Fired

chapters.heading: A fear out of the north
chapters.body: By the 1870s the British garrison troops had been pulled out of the Australian colonies, and New South Wales was left to defend itself. War between Russia and Britain in 1876 turned a vague anxiety into a real one, and Sydney suddenly worried that a Russian squadron might slip into Botany Bay and strike from the south. The military engineers William Jervois and Peter Scratchley were brought in to map out a chain of coastal defences. The small rocky islet at La Perouse, low and bare, sat right at the bay's mouth and seemed made for a fort.

chapters.heading: Building on the rock
chapters.body: Scratchley turned Jervois's strategy into a design, working with the civil engineer Gustave Morell and the Colonial Architect James Barnet, who was responsible for the construction. The contractor John McLeod started work on 7 April 1881 for the NSW Department of Public Works. The main fortification was finished by 1885 at a cost of about 34,000 pounds, with its guns set low behind concrete to sweep the bay. A footbridge was added in 1887, replacing the earlier crossing by barge and flying fox.

images.caption: A postcard view of Bare Island and its footbridge from 1907.

chapters.heading: The concrete scandal
chapters.body: Almost as soon as the fort was finished, its fabric began to fail, and officers complained the concrete was crumbling. A Royal Commission investigated and was so unimpressed it could barely bring itself to call the material concrete at all. The contractor McLeod was made to repay money and was barred from future government work, while the clerks of works were found to have neglected their inspections. The Colonial Architect James Barnet, the man behind so many of Sydney's grand sandstone buildings, was blamed for the mismanagement and resigned, ending a long and distinguished public career under a cloud.

images.caption: Bare Island fort photographed for a postcard around 1910.

chapters.heading: A home for old soldiers
chapters.body: The Russian fleet never came, and the guns were never fired in anger. Military use wound down after the fort passed to the Commonwealth in 1901, and by 1908 there was little activity on the island. After petitions from local women's groups, the fort opened in 1912 as a home for war veterans, the first of its kind in Australia, with the first seven men moving in. They were old soldiers of campaigns in the Crimea, Sudan and China, living out their days on a windswept rock at the edge of the bay.

chapters.heading: War returns, then quiet
chapters.body: When Japan entered the Second World War in 1942, the army reclaimed the island and a small detachment of about two dozen servicemen replaced the elderly veterans. After the war the home reopened, but its time was nearly done. In 1962 the army offered to sell the island back to the veterans home for a single pound, an offer that was declined, and in 1963 the home closed for good. The Randwick Historical Society then stepped in as caretakers and began restoring the neglected fort.

images.caption: The fort on its rocky island in 1974, by then a public historic site.

chapters.heading: A historic site for everyone
chapters.body: In 1967 Bare Island was gazetted as a historic site and passed to the National Parks and Wildlife Service, which still cares for it within Kamay Botany Bay National Park. For years visitors could watch the big nine-inch guns fired on weekends, a crowd favourite until 1974. Today guided tours lead people across the old footbridge and into the gun emplacements and tunnels. The fort even had a moment of fame on screen, standing in as a villain's lair in the film Mission Impossible 2.

images.caption: The footbridge linking the mainland at La Perouse to Bare Island, photographed in 2016.

title: Cadmans Cottage: The House the Harbour Left Behind

chapters.heading: A barracks on the edge of the cove
chapters.body: The cottage went up between late 1815 and early 1816 on the western shore of Sydney Cove, beside the government dockyard and commissariat stores near where the Museum of Contemporary Art now stands. It was raised against a rock shelf below the level of George Street so the men inside could watch over the government boats. The simple two-storey sandstone building was the coxswains barracks, home and office for the crews who rowed officials, carried stores and worked the harbour. Its design has long been attributed to the convict architect Francis Greenway, though no record confirms his hand.

chapters.heading: The convict whose name stuck
chapters.body: John Cadman arrived in the colony in 1798, transported for stealing a horse, and was working on the government boats by at least 1806. He earned a free pardon in 1821 and served as the longest-running of the government coxswains, from 1827 until he retired in 1845. He lived in the cottage with Elizabeth Mortimer, a fellow former convict whom he married at St Phillips in 1830, and her two daughters. He was only one of four coxswains to live here, but it was his name that the building kept.

images.caption: The sandstone cottage as it survives today in The Rocks.

chapters.heading: Police, cells and lock-ups
chapters.body: After Cadman left, the Sydney Water Police moved in around 1846 and made the cottage their headquarters. They extended the building for offices and added lock-up cells on the lower floor, giving the modest barracks a harder edge. The police stayed until the late 1850s, when they shifted to purpose-built quarters, and the cottage passed on to its next and longest tenant.

chapters.heading: A home for sailors
chapters.body: From the mid-1860s the cottage became part of the Sydney Sailors Home, a benevolent body devoted to housing and reforming itinerant seamen. A four-storey home was built just to the north in 1864, and the cottage served as the superintendents residence and later as overflow accommodation. This connection to the welfare of sailors ran on in some form into the 1950s, the longest single chapter in the building's working life.

images.caption: Cadmans Cottage sits below the taller former Sailors Home building beside it.

chapters.heading: The water that walked away
chapters.body: When the cottage was new the harbour came within about 2.4 metres of its door, close enough to step almost straight into a boat. The reclamation of the foreshore for Circular Quay, carried out through the early 1870s, filled the cove and pushed the waterline roughly 100 metres away. The cottage was never moved. The shoreline simply left it stranded, a maritime building marooned inland by the city it once served.

chapters.heading: Saved, stripped back and reopened
chapters.body: By the mid-twentieth century the cottage was recognised as a rare survivor, and the loss of nearby colonial buildings helped spare it from demolition. In 1972 the firm Philip Cox, Storey and Partners restored it, replacing the corrugated iron roof with shingles and returning its Georgian proportions, though the work erased much physical evidence of its later changes. The National Parks and Wildlife Service took ownership by 1976 and ran it as an information centre, and a major archaeological dig in 1988 uncovered the rocky foreshore on which it was built.

images.caption: The cottage seen from the northeast in The Rocks.

chapters.heading: What it is now
chapters.body: Cadmans Cottage was added to the New South Wales State Heritage Register in 1999 and is counted among the handful of Georgian buildings left from pre-1840 Sydney. It stands at 110 George Street, set back from the quay it once overlooked. Today it serves as a heritage site and visitor centre, a small sandstone reminder of the days when the harbour ran right up to its step.

title: Cockatoo Island: Wareamah, the Rock That Built Ships

chapters.heading: Wareamah, a Wangal place
chapters.body: Long before the dry docks and cranes, the sandstone island in the harbour was Wareamah, sitting in Wangal clan country where the Parramatta and Lane Cove rivers meet. First Nations people likely fished its shallows and may have stripped its trees for canoe bark. When Governor Phillip's party compiled a list of local place names around 1791, Wareamah was among them.

chapters.heading: Gipps chooses a prison rock, 1839
chapters.body: In 1839 Governor George Gipps picked the island for a penal establishment, liking that it was surrounded by deep water and yet, as he put it, under the very eye of authority. It became a place of secondary punishment for men who had reoffended in the colony. Convicts quarried sandstone that went into Circular Quay and cut about seventeen bottle-shaped grain silos deep into the bedrock, although London later vetoed the plan to store the colony's grain there.

images.caption: The bottle-shaped grain silos that convicts cut into the island bedrock from 1839.

chapters.heading: Carving the Fitzroy Dock
chapters.body: From the late 1840s convict gangs blasted and chiselled a graving dock out of the foreshore, work so demanding that it took about ten years before the Fitzroy Dock was finished in 1857. The engineer Gother Kerr Mann designed it, and the result was the earliest dry dock begun in Australia and the only one built with convict labour to survive. The job was dogged by friction between the prison managers and the dock builders over who controlled the men.

images.caption: Cockatoo Island painted by an unknown artist around 1864, with the convict barracks above the dock.

chapters.heading: Biloela and the training ships
chapters.body: The convict prison closed in 1869 and the island, renamed Biloela, took on a new and harder kind of welfare work. From 1871 the old barracks held an industrial school for orphaned and neglected girls alongside a reformatory for girls under sixteen who had been convicted of crimes, and the reformatory was moved to Watsons Bay in 1879. Offshore the training ship Vernon, an idea backed by Henry Parkes, drilled up to several hundred homeless and orphaned boys in seamanship, and her successor the Sobraon took over from 1891.

chapters.heading: The Sutherland Dock and a working harbour
chapters.body: Ships kept getting bigger than the Fitzroy Dock could hold, so a second graving dock was excavated through the 1880s and opened in 1890. At more than two hundred metres long it could take the largest vessels visiting Sydney, and it cemented the island's role as a serious shipbuilding and repair yard. A short-lived gaol for vagrants lingered until 1908, which finally ended roughly seventy years of using the rock as a prison.

images.caption: The Sutherland Dock, completed in 1890 to take the largest ships of the day.

chapters.heading: The Navy's yard through two world wars
chapters.body: In 1913 the Commonwealth took over the island and turned it into the Royal Australian Navy's main dockyard, employing thousands of workers by the First World War. Between the wars it even built aircraft, including the Codock designed under Lawrence Wackett for Charles Kingsford Smith. In the Second World War the island became the chief ship repair base in the south-west Pacific, fitting a new bow to the torpedoed USS New Orleans and patching the kamikaze damage to HMAS Australia.

images.caption: Sydney ferries sitting in the convict-built Fitzroy Dock in 1926.

chapters.heading: Closure and a second life
chapters.body: The yard launched warships and refitted submarines into the 1980s, including HMAS Success, the largest naval vessel built in Australia, before the dockyard finally closed in 1991. The Sydney Harbour Federation Trust was set up in 2001 to clean up the site and open it to the public. In 2010 the convict remnants were inscribed on the UNESCO World Heritage List as one of eleven Australian Convict Sites, and today the island draws visitors for tours, art events and camping.

title: Dawes Point: Tar-ra and the First Guns

chapters.heading: Tar-ra before the colony
chapters.body: The headland on the western arm of Sydney Cove was Tar-ra to the Gadigal people of the Eora nation, who fished from the flat rocks at its tip and cut engravings into the sandstone there. Those carvings, recalled in the nineteenth century as showing a whale and ancestral figures, were destroyed long ago and none survive. The point has carried deep meaning for Aboriginal Sydney for thousands of years before the First Fleet ever dropped anchor.

images.caption: Cannons stand on the Dawes Point Tar-ra interpretive park today.

chapters.heading: Dawes and his observatory
chapters.body: In early 1788 Lieutenant William Dawes of the Royal Marines built a small observatory on the point, the first building raised there and the colony's first scientific station. He came armed with a recommendation from the Astronomer Royal, Nevil Maskelyne, and instruments from the Board of Longitude, and he kept Sydney's first weather records here. Dawes even pinned the settlement's first clock to a rock face, so the point told the young town the time.

chapters.heading: Patyegarang and the language notebooks
chapters.body: While he lived on the point in 1790 and 1791, Dawes recorded the language of Sydney in three small notebooks, working closely with a young Gadigal woman named Patyegarang. She was around fifteen, and she taught him vocabulary, grammar and whole phrases of everyday Eora speech. The notebooks were thought lost until the librarian Phyllis Mander-Jones found them in London in the twentieth century, and they are now the richest surviving record of the Sydney language.

chapters.heading: The first guns
chapters.body: A powder magazine went up in 1789, and in 1791 a gun battery followed, prompted by fears of a Spanish attack after the Nootka Sound dispute. Its first cannon came from HMS Sirius, wrecked off Norfolk Island the year before. This made Dawes Point the colony's first permanent fortification, and the point soon took the lieutenant's name after he sailed home.

images.caption: A View of Dawe's Battery at the Entrance of Sydney Cove, engraved after Captain James Wallis in 1821.

chapters.heading: Greenway and Barney rebuild the fort
chapters.body: In 1819 Governor Macquarie set the convict architect Francis Greenway to redesign the works, and Greenway raised a semicircular battery behind a decorative castellated guardhouse, with sandstone quarried on the spot. During the Crimean War scare of the 1850s, Colonel George Barney reinforced the defences against a feared Russian fleet, adding new magazines, an officers' quarters and a lower battery, with the work finished around 1860. For decades a gun was fired here at one o'clock each day, a daily time signal for the harbour until 1906.

chapters.heading: Cleared for the Bridge
chapters.body: Most of the fort was demolished in 1925 to clear ground for the southern approach of the Sydney Harbour Bridge. The last buildings, which had served as the headquarters of Dorman Long, the contractor that built the bridge, came down in 1932, and the headland was landscaped for public use. The great steel arch now springs from almost the exact spot where the colony first mounted its guns.

images.caption: Dawes Point Battery photographed in the early 1900s, not long before its demolition.

chapters.heading: What survives beneath the pylon
chapters.body: Archaeologists dug the site from 1995 and found the floor of the original magazine, the circular battery with four gun emplacements, underground magazines, a stone ramp and the footings of the officers' quarters. The remains opened as the Dawes Point Tar-ra interpretive park in 2001, naming the place for the Gadigal at last. Five muzzle-loading forty-two pounders cast in the 1840s sit in the park, one of them mounted on a replica carriage on its original emplacement.

title: Dawn Fraser Baths: A Tidal Pool at White Horse Point

chapters.heading: A point on the Parramatta River
chapters.body: The baths sit at White Horse Point on the Balmain foreshore, in what became Elkington Park. Balmain Council acquired the waterfront land in 1880 with the intention of building public baths for a fast growing harbourside suburb. In 1881 the council accepted a tender from the builder James Reynolds, and the work began on the tidal edge of the Parramatta River.

chapters.heading: Open by January 1882
chapters.body: The baths were in use by January 1882, making them the oldest surviving harbour swimming baths in Australia. The first enclosure was a modest roofed pool of about 30 yards, fed and flushed by the tide through its timber walls. Bathing was strictly segregated, with men using the baths through daylight hours and women admitted only at limited times.

images.caption: The entrance to the heritage listed Dawn Fraser Baths at Balmain.

chapters.heading: The Balmain Swimming Club
chapters.body: In 1884, only two years after the baths opened, local swimmers formed the Balmain Swimming Club. It is regarded as probably the oldest swimming club in Australia, and it was the first to reach its centenary. From the start the club used the tidal enclosure as its home water, and its members went on to distinction in swimming, water polo, diving and life saving.

chapters.heading: Elkington Park and a wider pool
chapters.body: The baths were first known as White Horse Point Baths after the point itself. They were renamed Elkington Park Baths in honour of Alderman Elkington, the mayor who had worked to secure the land for the public. In 1902 the council replaced the original enclosure with a wider pool and added a two storey changing and viewing pavilion to the south, with boardwalks reaching out from the shore.

chapters.heading: Grandstand, springboards and a tower
chapters.body: A major enlargement in 1924 gave the baths much of the look they still carry today. The works added a north facing changing pavilion and grandstand, along with two springboards and a diving tower over the deeper water. A manager's cottage had already been built in the park in 1904, a sign of how busy the baths had become.

images.caption: The timber galleried tidal enclosure seen from the water side at Balmain.

chapters.heading: A local girl named after the pool
chapters.body: Dawn Fraser was born in 1937 and grew up nearby in Balmain and Birchgrove, taking up swimming in part to ease her asthma. She learnt in the local waters and went on to win the Olympic 100 metres freestyle at three consecutive Games, in Melbourne in 1956, Rome in 1960 and Tokyo in 1964. On 1 December 1964, soon after her third gold, the council renamed the baths in her honour.

images.caption: A plaque at the baths commemorating their dedication to Dawn Fraser.

chapters.heading: Heritage and the present
chapters.body: The baths were added to the New South Wales State Heritage Register on 23 June 2000, recognised as the only surviving fully enclosed public tidal baths of their kind in Australia. They remain a working pool, still open to the tide and still home to the Balmain Swimming Club. The timber galleries and grandstand keep the feel of a Victorian and Federation era harbour bath that has rarely lost its purpose.

title: Pinchgut: The Rock That Became a Fort

chapters.heading: Mattewanye, before the colony
chapters.body: Before 1788 the Eora knew this small sandstone outcrop north of Woolloomooloo Bay as Mattewanye, sometimes recorded as Muttewai or Muddawahnyuh. It was a steep little island rising sheer from the harbour, well placed for fishing and watching the water. Governor Arthur Phillip first labelled it Rock Island, but the colonists soon settled on a harsher name.

chapters.heading: Pinchgut and the bread and water rock
chapters.body: In 1788 a convict named Thomas Hill was sentenced to a week in irons on the rock with only bread and water. That meagre ration is the likeliest reason the place became known as Pinchgut. For the colony's first years the island served as an open-air punishment cell, a hungry exile within sight of the settlement it could not reach.

chapters.heading: Francis Morgan in chains
chapters.body: In late 1796 a gibbet went up on the island. Francis Morgan, transported for life and then convicted of murder in the colony, was hanged on 30 November 1796 and his body left to hang in chains as a warning to every ship coming through the Heads. His skeleton was still swinging there four years later, a grim sea mark for the harbour.

images.caption: Fort Denison photographed around 1885, less than thirty years after the tower was finished.

chapters.heading: Barney levels the island
chapters.body: From 1841 the colonial engineer George Barney began cutting the island down, partly for harbour defence and partly to win sandstone for the new works at Circular Quay. The Presbyterian minister John Dunmore Lang protested the destruction of what he called a natural ornament of the harbour. The defensive scheme stalled for want of money, and for over a decade the half levelled rock sat unfinished.

chapters.heading: The Russian scare and the Martello tower
chapters.body: Fear of a Russian raid during the Crimean War revived the project, and work resumed in 1855 with William Randle supervising construction. The fort was completed on 14 November 1857 and named after Sir William Denison, Governor of New South Wales. Its round stone keep is a Martello tower, the only one built in Australia and the last raised anywhere in the British Empire, armed with heavy muzzle loaders and a ring of thirty-two pounders.

images.caption: Fort Denison sits in the centre of the harbour as a naval squadron departs Port Jackson in 1881.

chapters.heading: The fort that never fired in anger
chapters.body: The guns were obsolete almost as soon as they were mounted, and Fort Denison never fired a shot in battle. It found a quieter purpose instead, with tide measurements beginning at the island in 1857 and the gauge becoming a long running record of Sydney's sea level. During the Japanese midget submarine attack of 1942, shells from the American cruiser USS Chicago struck the tower, and that minor damage can still be seen.

images.caption: The squat Martello tower that gives Fort Denison its distinctive profile.

chapters.heading: A national park island today
chapters.body: In 1992 the island passed to the National Parks and Wildlife Service as part of Sydney Harbour National Park, and after restoration it reopened fully to visitors around 2000. The tide gauge and a weather station still operate on the rock that once starved convicts. Today people cross the water for the history, the harbour views and a meal in the old fort, a long way from the bread and water of Thomas Hill.

images.caption: Fort Denison and its lighthouse seen across Sydney Harbour in modern times.

title: Luna Park: The Face on the Harbour

chapters.heading: Where the bridge was born
chapters.body: Before there was a funfair, this slice of the Milsons Point foreshore belonged to the Sydney Harbour Bridge. From 1924 the contractor Dorman Long ran a steel fabrication and assembly workshop here, shaping the great arch that opened in 1932. Once the bridge was finished the workshops came down, and North Sydney Council called for tenders to put the cleared waterfront to new use.

chapters.heading: Mr Moon opens his eyes, 1935
chapters.body: The men behind Luna Park Glenelg in Adelaide, Herman Phillips with his brothers and the showman A.A. Abrahams, had fallen out with their local council and were hunting for a new home. They won the Milsons Point tender, dismantled their Adelaide rides and shipped them east over about three months, employing close to a thousand workers. Luna Park opened on 4 October 1935 to instant crowds. The grinning entrance Face, modelled on the Man in the Moon and nicknamed Mr Moon, was painted by Melbourne scenic artist Rupert Browne, flanked by Art Deco towers whose scalloped spires echoed New York's Chrysler Building.

images.caption: An aerial view of the new Luna Park at Milsons Point in 1937.

chapters.heading: A face that would not stay still
chapters.body: The original Face was built from plaster on chicken wire, so it weathered badly and needed constant remaking. Browne redesigned it in 1938, and by the late 1950s it had sagged so far that it looked grotesque. Arthur Barton gave it a fresh, happy version in 1960, loosely inspired by Old King Cole. In 1973 the pop artist Martin Sharp repainted it as a bold, clown-like mask in strong primary colours, beginning a long association between Sharp and the park.

chapters.heading: Television, cars and decline
chapters.body: The Phillips brothers sold out in 1950 to David Atkins and Ted Hopkins, who added a Rotor in 1951 and a Wild Mouse coaster in the 1960s to compete with the lure of television and the family car. Hopkins retired in 1969 and the lease passed to a company that let things slide, abandoning the old habit of closing in winter for maintenance. The rides aged hard. In April 1979 a steel runner worked loose on the Big Dipper and a collision injured thirteen people.

images.caption: Luna Park photographed in 1968, in its postwar heyday.

chapters.heading: The Ghost Train fire, 9 June 1979
chapters.body: On the evening of 9 June 1979 fire broke out inside the Ghost Train, a dark timber tunnel painted black, and seven people died. They were John Godson and his two young sons, Damien and Craig, aged six and five, and four Waverley College students, Seamus Rahilly, Jonathan Billings, Richard Carroll and Michael Johnson, all thirteen. The Glebe coroner found the deaths accidental but condemned the operator for cost-cutting and a failure of duty of care, noting that safety measures urged in 1977 had never been carried out. The exact cause of the fire was never settled, and the case has been reopened and debated for decades since.

chapters.heading: Closed, fought over, reborn
chapters.body: The park shut at once after the fire and the government called for redevelopment tenders. A community campaign called Friends of Luna Park, with Martin Sharp prominent among them, marched and rallied to save it, and the Face won national heritage protection. Most original structures were cleared apart from the Face, the Crystal Palace, the Rotor and Coney Island, and the site reopened as Harbourside Amusement Park on 29 April 1982 before quickly reverting to the Luna Park name. It closed again in 1988 amid failed redevelopment schemes, and in 1990 the government terminated the lease and passed the Luna Park Site Act to dedicate the land for amusement and public recreation.

images.caption: Luna Park around 1982, seen from the Sydney Harbour Bridge after rebuilding.

chapters.heading: The long road back, 1995 to today
chapters.body: A rebuilt park reopened in January 1995 with a towering new Big Dipper, but poor weather, low crowds and a barrage of noise complaints from harbourside residents forced it shut again on 14 February 1996. After years of plans and disputes the Metro Edgley group won the redevelopment, the old Big Dipper went to Dreamworld in Queensland, and the park reopened on 4 April 2004. Special legislation later shielded it from noise litigation, and in February 2010 Luna Park was added to the NSW State Heritage Register.

images.caption: The Face entrance and Art Deco towers as they greet visitors today.

title: Macquarie Lighthouse: Two Centuries of Light at South Head

chapters.heading: A fire on the cliff
chapters.body: Long before any tower stood here, the colony marked the harbour mouth with the crudest of signals. From the early 1790s a flagstaff and a navigation column stood on the high bluff at South Head, and by 1793 sailors were tending an iron basket on a tripod, burning first wood and then coal through the night. It was a dangerous coast, and the open fire was a poor substitute for a proper light. Ships still groped their way past the cliffs in the dark.

chapters.heading: Greenway's tower, 1818
chapters.body: Governor Lachlan Macquarie wanted a real lighthouse, and he turned to Francis Greenway, the forger transported to New South Wales who became the colony's most gifted architect. Macquarie laid the foundation stone on 11 July 1816, and the sandstone tower was lit for the first time on 30 November 1818. It was the first lighthouse to operate in Australia, and it pleased Macquarie so much that he granted Greenway an emancipation. The light burned whale oil, and a keeper climbed the tower each night to tend it.

images.caption: A drawing of Greenway's original tower at South Head made around 1840 to 1850.

chapters.heading: The stone that would not last
chapters.body: The local sandstone was soft, and the sea air worked at it from the start. By the 1820s the tower was already crumbling, and workers wrapped iron bands around it to hold the stone together. Emergency repairs followed across the decades, in 1822, 1830, 1836 and 1866, each one buying a little more time. By 1878 the colonial government accepted that patching could not save Greenway's tower and that a fresh one was needed.

chapters.heading: Barnet builds its twin
chapters.body: The job went to James Barnet, Colonial Architect through the building boom of Victorian Sydney. Rather than reinvent the landmark, Barnet raised a near copy of Greenway's design only a few metres away, built this time in harder stone and stronger detail. Construction ran from 1881, and the new tower showed its light for the first time in 1883. It carried a first order Fresnel lens with a range of around 25 nautical miles, a far cry from the old oil lamp.

images.caption: The old and new towers standing side by side in 1884, shortly before the original was pulled down.

chapters.heading: From gas to electricity
chapters.body: The two towers stood together for a few years, an old ghost beside its replacement, until the Greenway tower was demolished around 1887. The new light kept pace with technology. Early electric arc lamps gave way to kerosene in 1912, and in 1933 the station was wired for mains electricity with a new lens to match. In 1976 the lighthouse was fully automated, and there was no longer any need for a hand on the lamp.

chapters.heading: The last keepers leave
chapters.body: Keepers and their families had lived on the windswept headland since 1818, and the cottages around the tower had been a small working community for generations. The last of them left in 1989, closing more than a century and a half of staffed operation. Robert Watson, the colony's first keeper and the man Watsons Bay remembers, was an old man when he took the post and died within a year of his appointment.

chapters.heading: A working light today
chapters.body: The Australian Maritime Safety Authority still runs the light, which guides shipping into Sydney just as it has for two hundred years. The Sydney Harbour Federation Trust took over the surrounding lightstation in 2001 and opens the tower for guided tours, while the headland walk past it draws a steady stream of visitors. The site is listed on the NSW State Heritage Register and the Commonwealth Heritage List. It remains the oldest lighthouse site in Australia.

images.caption: Barnet's tower as it stands today above the cliffs at Vaucluse.

title: McIver's Ladies Baths: A Pool of Their Own

chapters.heading: A sheltered pool below the cliffs
chapters.body: Tucked into the rocks of Grant Reserve, just south of Coogee Beach, sits a natural rock pool screened from the open beach by the headland. Aboriginal women are reported to have used the spot for bathing well before European Sydney took an interest, while men gathered on the northern side of the beach. The seclusion that nature provided here is the reason the place became, and remained, a pool for women.

chapters.heading: Council makes it official, 1876
chapters.body: In 1876 Randwick Council formally opened the site as women-only baths, excavating the rock and enclosing the pool with concrete walls to shape a proper swimming area. Further works to deepen and finish the pool were completed by 1886. The pool that resulted runs roughly twenty metres in length and is fed and refreshed by the sea, so the water moves with the tide rather than sitting still.

chapters.heading: Training ground for champions
chapters.body: By the early 1900s the baths had become a place where Sydney girls and women learned to swim properly and to race. Two of them, Fanny Durack and Mina Wylie, honed their swimming on this stretch of coast. At the 1912 Stockholm Olympics, the first Games to allow women to swim competitively, Durack won gold and Wylie took silver in the 100 metres freestyle.

images.caption: Fanny Durack, the 1912 Olympic freestyle champion who trained on the Coogee coast.

chapters.heading: Mina Wylie and the women of Coogee
chapters.body: Mina Wylie came from the family that ran the neighbouring Wylie's Baths, and she remained a fixture of Coogee swimming for decades. She was photographed at Coogee in 1913, the year after her Olympic silver, in the plain one-piece costume of the day. Women like her gave the baths a competitive pedigree that sat alongside its quieter role as a refuge.

images.caption: Mina Wylie photographed at Coogee in 1913, the year after her Olympic silver medal.

chapters.heading: The McIvers give the baths their name
chapters.body: In 1918 the operation of the baths passed to private operators Robert and Rose McIver, and it is their name that has stuck ever since. Rose McIver was no mere caretaker, as she helped found the Randwick and Coogee Ladies Amateur Swimming Club alongside figures such as Mina Wylie and Bella O'Keefe. From 1923 that club took over the lease, and it has run the baths from then to the present day.

chapters.heading: Women only, written into law
chapters.body: Keeping a public bathing place reserved for one sex eventually ran up against anti-discrimination law. In 1995 the baths were granted an exemption under the Anti-Discrimination Act 1977 so they could remain for women and children only, a recognition of how much the place mattered to women who wanted to swim in privacy. The pool draws older women, women with disabilities, and women of the Catholic, Muslim and other communities who value the seclusion.

images.caption: The modest entrance to McIver's Ladies Baths at Grant Reserve, Coogee.

chapters.heading: Heritage and the present day
chapters.body: On 18 November 2011 McIver's Baths were added to the NSW State Heritage Register, recognised as reportedly the only women's swimming venue in continuous use in the state, and perhaps in Australia. The baths remain owned by Randwick City Council and run by the swimming club, asking only a small honesty-box donation from visitors. Nearly a century and a half after the council first cut into the rock, women still climb down to the same sheltered pool to swim.

title: North Fort: The Guns That Watched the Heads

chapters.heading: Car-rang-gel before the guns
chapters.body: Long before the army arrived, the Gayamaygal people knew this headland as Car-rang-gel and used it for ceremony. Rock engravings, campsites, burials and shell middens still mark the sandstone. When the First Fleet sailed through the Heads in 1788, North Head was the first land they passed on the way into the harbour.

images.caption: An aerial view of North Head guarding the northern entrance to Sydney Harbour.

chapters.heading: Cutting the battery into the rock
chapters.body: In March 1934 the army bought two 9.2 inch Mark 10 guns on Mark 7 mountings, the heaviest coastal pieces ever set up to defend Sydney. Engineers cut two emplacements into the headland and joined them with a network of tunnels running about 200 metres through the sandstone. The work guarded the seaward approach to the harbour, the angle that older forts inside the Heads could not cover.

chapters.heading: The bomb proof brain underground
chapters.body: Eight metres below the surface, behind a camouflaged shed entrance, the army built a concrete plotting room to direct the fire. It actually held two rooms, the Fortress Plotting Room and the smaller Battery Plotting Room, along with a shell store and an engine room sealed deep in the rock. Target bearings were worked out here and passed up to the gunners above.

chapters.heading: Fortress Sydney at war
chapters.body: By 1938 the new guns were test fired at North Head, and through the Second World War the fort was manned around the clock. Each gun could throw a shell more than 26 kilometres out to sea.
