From 1941 women of the Australian Women's Army Service took on key roles in the plotting room, helping coordinate batteries that stretched along hundreds of kilometres of the New South Wales coast.

images.caption: A new 9.2 inch gun firing on test at North Head in 1938.

chapters.heading: Camouflaged and waiting
chapters.body: The big guns never fired a shot in anger, but they sat ready behind camouflage netting for the length of the war. A photograph from January 1944 shows one of the 9.2 inch coast defence guns draped and disguised against the headland scrub. North Fort was among the most heavily fortified positions in the country at the time.

images.caption: A camouflaged 9.2 inch coast defence gun of the North Head Battery in January 1944.

chapters.heading: From school of gunnery to museum
chapters.body: In 1946 the Army School of Artillery opened on the highest part of the headland and trained gunners there until it moved to Puckapunyal in Victoria in the late 1990s. The two 9.2 inch guns were removed in 1952 once such heavy coastal weapons had had their day. In 1990 the old fort became home to the Royal Australian Artillery National Museum.

chapters.heading: A sanctuary on the headland
chapters.body: In the early 2000s the Sydney Harbour Federation Trust took over this part of North Head, and in 2007 it opened to the public as North Head Sanctuary. Today visitors walk the Memorial Walk and take guided tours down into the restored plotting room and tunnels. The cliffs that once hid the guns now look out over bushland and the open sea.

images.caption: The cliffs at North Head Sanctuary at Manly today.

title: Prospect Reservoir: The Lake That Watered a City

chapters.heading: A hill on the Cumberland Plain
chapters.body: Long before any dam, Prospect Hill rose from the flat western plains as the worn-down core of an ancient volcano, a rare hard outcrop of diorite in country that is otherwise soft shale and clay. The land and the nearby Eastern Creek carried deep cultural meaning for the Darug people, who lived across this stretch of the Cumberland Plain for many thousands of years. When colonial Sydney later went looking for stone and a place to hold its water, this old volcanic ground offered both.

images.caption: An aerial view of Prospect Reservoir on the western plains of Sydney.

chapters.heading: A city running dry
chapters.body: By the 1860s Sydney had outgrown its earlier water sources, having moved from the Tank Stream to Busby's Bore and then the Botany Swamps. In 1867 the Governor appointed a commission to find a lasting supply, and by 1869 it recommended the Upper Nepean Scheme. The plan was to capture the rivers of the Upper Nepean catchment and carry the water by gravity, with no pumping, across many kilometres to a great holding reservoir at Prospect.

chapters.heading: Moriarty's earthen wall
chapters.body: Credit for the scheme rests largely with Edward Orpen Moriarty, engineer in chief of the Harbours and Rivers Branch of the Public Works Department. From two diversion weirs at Pheasants Nest and Broughtons Pass, water ran through the Upper Canal of tunnels, aqueducts and channels toward Prospect. The dam itself was built between roughly 1882 and 1888 as an earthfill embankment, with a watertight puddle clay core, shoulders of selected earth laid in thin compacted layers, and an upstream face pitched with diorite blocks quarried close by.

chapters.heading: Water reaches Sydney, 1888
chapters.body: The reservoir was finished in 1888 and held back a lake of around 50,000 megalitres behind a wall some 26 metres high and about 2.2 kilometres long. It was the first earthfill embankment dam in Australia and, for its day, an outstanding piece of civil engineering. From this point Prospect became the main distribution reservoir for Sydney, a role it would hold for well over a century.

images.caption: The valve house at Prospect Reservoir, which controlled the flow of water into Sydney's mains.

chapters.heading: Streeton paints the new lake
chapters.body: The young reservoir caught the eye of Arthur Streeton, the leading painter of the Heidelberg school, who set up his easel here in 1895. His long, luminous canvas of the water and the dry surrounding country was owned by the Metropolitan Water and Sewerage Board for decades before being given to the Art Gallery of New South Wales in 1937. It remains one of the finest images of late colonial western Sydney.

images.caption: Arthur Streeton's 1895 painting of Prospect Reservoir, now held by the Art Gallery of New South Wales.

chapters.heading: Mending and raising the wall
chapters.body: An earth dam of this scale needed constant care. Between 1893 and 1916 engineers carried out remedial work to fix slumping on the upstream face, and in 1898 the crest was raised by half a metre. Later strengthening work followed in 1979 and 1980 on the downstream side, and again on the upstream face in 1997, keeping the Victorian structure safe under modern standards.

chapters.heading: Warragamba changes the flow
chapters.body: When the vast Warragamba Dam was completed in 1960, it did not replace Prospect but fed into it, with the new water piped down for distribution across the city. A second pipeline between Warragamba and Prospect opened in 1966 to lift capacity at times of peak demand. For a few more decades Prospect sat at the centre of Sydney's water network, taking in supply from the dams to the west and sending it on to the suburbs.

chapters.heading: From storage to settling basin
chapters.body: The opening of the Prospect Water Filtration Plant in 1996 changed everything. Raw water from Warragamba and the Upper Nepean dams now bypasses the old reservoir and runs straight to treatment, so Prospect became an off-line settling basin and standby service reservoir rather than the main store. In 1999 the reservoir and its surrounds were placed on the NSW State Heritage Register, and the wider Prospect Nature Reserve now protects endangered Cumberland Plain Woodland alongside the lake that once watered the whole city.

title: North Head Quarantine Station: The Headland Where Sydney Held Its Breath

chapters.heading: A headland that was Country first
chapters.body: Long before any ship anchored here, North Head was a place of meaning for the Aboriginal people of the harbour, who knew the wider Manly area as Kayyemy. The headland sat within the lands of the Gayemaygal clan, and ceremonies and gatherings were held across these slopes and clifftops. That older significance still runs beneath the colonial story, because quarantine took over Country that had been used and valued for thousands of years.

chapters.heading: The Bussorah Merchant and the first isolation, 1828
chapters.body: In 1828 the immigrant ship Bussorah Merchant arrived carrying both smallpox and whooping cough, and it was held at the sheltered Spring Cove on the Manly side of North Head. The sick stayed aboard while healthier passengers were landed and housed in tents on the shore. This improvised response was the first time the headland was used to keep disease away from the young town, and it set the pattern for everything that followed.

chapters.heading: A station set in law, 1832
chapters.body: Fear of the cholera then sweeping Europe, together with a sharp rise in immigrant ships, pushed Governor Darling to act. A Quarantine Act set aside the whole of North Head, and the station began formal operation on 14 August 1832. From then on a vessel flying the yellow flag could be ordered to anchor off the headland while its passengers waited out their isolation between hope and the hospital.

images.caption: Buildings of the North Head Quarantine Station above Sydney Harbour.

chapters.heading: Smallpox, plague and the long roll of disease
chapters.body: The station caught a procession of infections carried in by sea, including smallpox, typhus, typhoid, cholera, scarlet fever and bubonic plague. The smallpox epidemic of 1881 and 1882 filled the site and saw dozens of Sydney residents held at North Head for months. When plague reached Sydney in 1900, infected people and their contacts were again sent out to the headland to wait.

chapters.heading: Names cut into the rock
chapters.body: People held in quarantine left their mark in the most direct way they could, by carving into the sandstone. More than 1,600 inscriptions have been recorded across the site, with thick clusters around the wharf, and they run from the 1830s through to the station's final years. They were cut in English, Greek, Arabic, Russian, Finnish and Chinese among other scripts, naming ships, dates and the dead, and they form one of the most remarkable records of ordinary people left anywhere in Sydney.

images.caption: An inscription carved into the sandstone at North Head by people held in quarantine.

chapters.heading: The pneumonic influenza, 1918 to 1919
chapters.body: During the pneumonic influenza pandemic the station was worked to its limits, holding both returning soldiers and civilians from infected ships. By late January 1919 at least 326 cases and 49 deaths had been recorded at the station, and the staff suffered too. Nurse Annie Egan died on 3 December 1918 at the age of 27, one of the carers lost while tending the sick.

images.caption: A view across the grounds of the former Quarantine Station at North Head.

chapters.heading: Closure and a second life as Q Station
chapters.body: Air travel and modern medicine slowly made the headland station obsolete, and it closed on 29 February 1984 after more than 150 years of service. Across its working life at least 580 vessels were quarantined and more than 13,000 people passed through, of whom an estimated 572 died and were buried on the site. The land passed to the National Parks and Wildlife Service as part of Sydney Harbour National Park, and after Mawland Group took on the lease the buildings reopened in 2008 as the Q Station hotel, conference and dining complex.

title: The Spit: From Hand-Hauled Punt to a Bridge That Still Stops for Yachts

chapters.heading: Crossing the narrows
chapters.body: The Spit is the thin neck where Middle Harbour pinches almost shut, and for a long time crossing it meant trusting the water. A rowing boat carried people over from as early as 1829, and a hand-hauled punt took over around 1850 as traffic between the lower north shore and the land beyond began to grow. By 1889 demand had risen enough that a steam punt replaced the hand-hauled service.

chapters.heading: The first bridge, built in seven months
chapters.body: Manly had been booming as a seaside resort since the 1850s, and from the late 1870s its residents pressed for a proper bridge across the narrows between Seaforth and Mosman. The campaign ran on for decades, and the first Spit Bridge finally opened in 1924 after legislation passed late in 1923. It was a low-level timber structure of about 213 metres, funded by a loan that Manly Council raised on its own because Mosman Council across the water declined to contribute. In the first four weeks tolls were collected on 62,675 vehicles, and the loan was cleared by Christmas 1929.

images.caption: The first Spit Bridge under construction in September 1924, with the old punt still working in the foreground.

chapters.heading: Queues, trams and ferries through the gap
chapters.body: The timber bridge solved one problem and created others almost at once. By 1926 and 1927 the queues at the Spit were as long as they had been in the punt days, partly because motorists rarely carried the correct toll change. Tram passengers had it worse, alighting on one side, walking across the deck and boarding a second tram on the far bank. The bridge carried an opening span too, and Sydney ferries and showboats slipped through into Middle Harbour.

images.caption: The Sydney ferry Kirrule passes through the new first Spit Bridge in October 1924.

chapters.heading: A crash, a war and a long wait for steel
chapters.body: Surveys for a replacement bridge were undertaken in 1939, but the war stopped any funding. The decision finally came in May 1949, after a bus crash near the bridge killed four people and injured many more, when a new four-lane lift bridge was promised. Work on the present steel and concrete structure began in 1952, then dragged on for years as a chronic postwar shortage of steel and concrete and repeated industrial disputes held it up.

images.caption: The ferry Karingal passes through the old timber Spit Bridge in the years before its replacement.

chapters.heading: Opened without ceremony
chapters.body: Built by the Cleveland Bridge and Engineering Company of Middlesbrough in England, the new bridge of about 227 metres opened on 19 November 1958. By then it had taken almost eight years from the awarding of the contract and cost close to double the original estimate, so public goodwill had worn thin and no official opening ceremony was held. Its single-leaf bascule span gave a navigation channel of 24 metres, and it remains the only lift span on a major Sydney arterial road.

chapters.heading: Still lifting, still jamming the traffic
chapters.body: The bascule span still rises on a published schedule to let tall-masted yachts pass into Middle Harbour, and each lift sets off the weekend tailbacks that northern beaches drivers know too well. The Roads and Traffic Authority found in 2007 that traffic does not settle for about fifteen minutes after an opening, which is why lift times have since been trimmed. A plan to widen the bridge to six lanes, promised in 2002 and again in 2006, was abandoned around 2007 once the cost climbed towards roughly 115 million dollars and the engineering proved too hard.

images.caption: The Spit Bridge with its single-leaf bascule span raised, seen from the northwest in 2006.

title: Susannah Place: Four Doors on Gloucester Street

chapters.heading: Riley's hand, 1844
chapters.body: Edward and Mary Riley reached Sydney in 1838 as assisted immigrants, he from Wexford and she from County Carlow. By 1844 Edward had put up four small brick terraces at 58 to 64 Gloucester Street, in the crowded foreshore quarter known as The Rocks. He cut the words Susannah Place Anno Domini 1844 into the western wall, naming the row after the family's young milliner, Susannah Sterne, who had sailed out with them.

chapters.heading: A landlady who stayed
chapters.body: The Rileys lived at No. 62 and let the other three houses, with the 1845 rate book recording John Munro, Thomas Hall, Francis Cunningham and James Macknell as the first tenants. Edward died in 1853, leaving Mary to hold the row for more than twenty years until her own death in 1874. In a district full of absentee landlords she was a rare resident owner, a steady hand at one door while tenants came and went at the others.

images.caption: Susannah Place seen from Gloucester Street in The Rocks.

chapters.heading: The corner shop
chapters.body: A small grocery traded from the front room of No. 64, selling food and drink along with candles, brooms, soap and boiled lollies. A long line of shopkeepers took their turn behind the counter, among them ginger beer maker James Munro in the 1840s, George Hill in the 1880s and 1890s, and Hugo and Clara Youngein from 1904 to 1930. The little store served its neighbours for roughly ninety years before it finally closed.

chapters.heading: Plague and the wreckers
chapters.body: When bubonic plague broke out in 1900, the government resumed The Rocks under the Darling Harbour Wharves Resumption Act and set crews loose on the lanes. Photographers from the Department of Public Works recorded the cleansing, and the wreckers pulled down hundreds of houses across the quarter. Susannah Place was soundly built and stood through it all, though the families inside now rented from the state rather than from a private owner.

images.caption: A yard at the rear of Gloucester Street photographed during the 1900 plague cleansing operations.

chapters.heading: A green ban saves the row
chapters.body: By the early 1970s the Sydney Cove Redevelopment Authority wanted The Rocks remade, and in November 1971 residents received demolition notices. The Rocks Resident Action Group joined the Builders Labourers Federation, whose green bans on this neighbourhood were the first imposed on a working class community in Sydney. The bans held off the bulldozers, and over a hundred families had by then passed through the four doors, among them Irish immigrants, Greek and other shopkeepers, and Norwegian sailors.

chapters.heading: The Marshalls turn out the lights
chapters.body: Ellen and Dennis Marshall lived at No. 62 and acted as the unofficial caretakers of the row through its uncertain years. They were the last to leave, departing in 1990 and closing almost a century and a half of continuous occupation. In 1986 the Historic Houses Trust had sought the property, and Premier Neville Wran backed its acquisition as of critical heritage significance to the state.

chapters.heading: Kept as found
chapters.body: Conservation work ran from 1987 and finished in 1992 at a cost of about 250,000 dollars, with the aim of preserving rather than recreating. Susannah Place opened as a museum in 1993, its layers of wallpaper, paint and linoleum left in place and the corner shop fitted out to the look of the 1910s and 1920s from oral histories. On 10 December 1999 it joined the New South Wales State Heritage Register, and it is now run by Museums of History NSW.

images.caption: The restored Susannah Place terrace as it stands today on Gloucester Street.

title: Vaucluse House: A Convict's Cottage and a Statesman's Seat

chapters.heading: A knight in exile builds by the water
chapters.body: The first house at Vaucluse was the work of Sir Henry Browne Hayes, an Irish knight transported for life after he kidnapped the Quaker heiress Mary Pike and forced her into a sham marriage at Cork in 1797. He reached Sydney aboard the Atlas in July 1802 and within a few years had built a small stone cottage near South Head, living, as one account put it, in remarkable style and freedom for a convict. Hayes named the place Vaucluse after Fontaine-de-Vaucluse, the village near Avignon in southern France.

chapters.heading: Irish turf and a moat against the snakes
chapters.body: Local legend holds that Hayes, troubled by the snakes around his cottage, surrounded it with turf shipped out from Ireland in the belief that the soil of St Patrick's country would keep the reptiles away. The charm was said to have worked. Hayes also presided over a Masonic meeting at the house on 14 May 1803, an event remembered as the foundation day of Freemasonry in Australia. He was pardoned in 1809, the pardon honoured by Governor Macquarie, and sailed home to Cork in December 1812.

images.caption: Vaucluse House photographed around the 1930s, from the RAHS Osborne Collection.

chapters.heading: Wentworth buys a dynasty's seat
chapters.body: The estate passed through several hands, including Captain John Piper who held it from the 1810s, until William Charles Wentworth bought the property on 27 August 1827. Explorer of the Blue Mountains, barrister and co-founder of The Australian newspaper, Wentworth saw Vaucluse as the seat of a powerful family. He and his wife Sarah Cox, whose convict-tainted background barred them from polite colonial society, raised ten children here and steadily consolidated the holding until it stretched across hundreds of acres of the eastern headlands.

images.caption: William Charles Wentworth photographed by Dalton's Royal Photographic Gallery in the early 1860s.

chapters.heading: A Gothic house that was never finished
chapters.body: From the late 1820s the Wentworths wrapped Hayes's cottage in a much larger Gothic Revival house, with turrets, a kitchen wing and sandstone stables designed by George Cookney in 1829. Further additions came through the 1830s and 1840s, and an iron verandah with Gothic detailing was added around 1861 to 1862 after the family returned from Europe. The 1840s depression and Wentworth's own shifting plans meant the grand facade and formal entrance he imagined were never built, so the house keeps a faintly unfinished, asymmetrical look to this day.

chapters.heading: The man who won self-government
chapters.body: Wentworth's deepest mark was political rather than architectural. He led the campaign for representative and then responsible government in New South Wales, and in 1853 he chaired the select committee that drafted the colony's constitution. That work carried through to the constitution of 1855, which gave the colony self-government. He fell out badly with the radicals over his early scheme for a colonial peerage, which his opponents mocked as a bunyip aristocracy.

chapters.heading: A state funeral and a tomb on the hill
chapters.body: Wentworth died in England on 20 March 1872, having asked to be buried at Vaucluse. His body was brought home and on 6 May 1873 he was given the first state funeral held in New South Wales, the cortege drawing enormous crowds through Sydney. Sarah commissioned the mausoleum on a rise above the house, built between 1872 and 1874 by the Mansfield Brothers, where Wentworth and later Sarah were laid to rest.

images.caption: The Wentworth Mausoleum at Vaucluse, where William Charles and Sarah Wentworth are buried.

chapters.heading: Australia's first house museum
chapters.body: After the contents were auctioned around 1900, the government resumed part of the estate by 1910 to 1911 for a public reserve, and the house opened to visitors on weekends and holidays in 1915. That makes it one of the earliest house museums in Australia, kept as a window onto colonial life. The National Parks and Wildlife Service took it on in 1968, the Historic Houses Trust from 1980, and today it is cared for by Museums of History NSW, gardens, stables, mausoleum and all.

images.caption: Vaucluse House photographed by Sam Hood in November 1932.

title: Victoria Barracks: The Long Sandstone Garrison on Paddington Hill

chapters.heading: A site chosen on the windswept sand hills
chapters.body: By the late 1830s the old George Street barracks in town were cramped and hemmed in by the growing city, so Governor Bourke set out to move the soldiers somewhere with room to drill. Officers first eyed the Cleveland Paddocks, but residential building crept too close, and in 1840 the choice settled on the high ground along South Head Road, now Oxford Street, at Paddington Hill. The spot had everything an engineer wanted. It stood clear of the town on a commanding ridge, it sat near Busby's Bore for water, and the sand dunes hid good Hawkesbury sandstone that could be quarried on the spot.

chapters.heading: Barney's barracks rise from convict labour
chapters.body: Work began in February 1841 under Major George Barney, the Commanding Royal Engineer who also reclaimed the mud flats of Circular Quay and built the fort on Pinchgut that became Fort Denison. Convict gangs cleared the site and cut the sandstone, while contracted masons laid the walls, and from 1843 Lieutenant Colonel James Gordon carried on the supervision after Barney moved to other duties. Barney's plan was generous, with a main block for some 650 to 700 men, officers' quarters for thirty-nine, and a garrison hospital. The officers' quarters, finished early in 1842, are still regarded as the finest nineteenth century officers' mess built anywhere in Australia.

images.caption: A William Hetzer stereograph of about 1850 to 1870 looks south east past the Garrison Hospital to the main barrack block.

chapters.heading: The longest stone building in the south
chapters.body: The main barrack block was completed in 1846 and the whole complex was occupied by 1848, at which point the George Street barracks were given up for good. The long sandstone face runs about 742 feet, roughly 226 metres, and was long reputed to be the longest stone building in the Southern Hemisphere. The name Royal Victoria Barracks honoured the reigning queen, and a bell and clock were added in 1856. Around the buildings rose a high sandstone wall enclosing roughly thirty acres, with the Queen Victoria Gate facing Oxford Street and the Convict Gate on Moore Park Road.

images.caption: Charles Pickering photographed the men's quarters in 1872 for the New South Wales Government Printing Office.

chapters.heading: British redcoats give way to colonial troops
chapters.body: For its first two decades the barracks held British Army regiments, and it became the premier military training ground in the colony. In 1870 Britain withdrew its garrison from Australia, leaving defence to the colonies, and the New South Wales Artillery was among the first local units to move in. Fifteen years later, in 1885, the soldiers of the New South Wales contingent mustered at the barracks before sailing to fight in the Sudan, the first time an Australian colony sent troops to an imperial war.

chapters.heading: From Federation to two world wars
chapters.body: After Federation in 1901 the barracks passed to the new Commonwealth and became a principal base of the Australian Army. During the lean years of the early 1930s the Royal Military College, Duntroon, was temporarily moved here when its Canberra home was closed to save money. Through the Second World War the barracks ran as a busy command and administrative centre directing the army across New South Wales.

images.caption: In January 1923 the French rear admiral F L Gilly called on the district commandant and his officers beside a cannon at the barracks.

chapters.heading: A museum behind the prison walls
chapters.body: The District Military Prison, a tight block of twenty-five cells built in 1847, served generations of soldiers under punishment before its grim purpose ended. In 1985 the old prison reopened as the Army Museum of New South Wales, which the public can still visit. The Corps of Guides also runs tours of the wider precinct, taking visitors past the great sandstone facade and the parade ground.

images.caption: The Oxford Street gate to Victoria Barracks photographed in 2007.

chapters.heading: A garrison that never emptied
chapters.body: Victoria Barracks has held troops without a break since 1848, and the suburb of Paddington grew up tightly around its walls. It was added to the Commonwealth Heritage List in 2004 as one of the most important groups of Georgian military buildings in Australia. Today it remains a working Army base and headquarters, home to units such as the Australian Army Band Sydney, even as recent plans have begun to ask how parts of the precinct might one day open further to the public.

title: Warragamba: The Wall That Drowned a Valley

chapters.heading: A river marked for water
chapters.body: As early as 1845 the Polish explorer Pawel Strzelecki pointed to the Warragamba River as a natural source of water for the growing colony. The river runs through a deep gorge that opens into the broad Burragorang Valley, so a short, high wall could hold back an enormous lake. Schemes were floated from 1867 onward, but for decades nothing was built.

chapters.heading: De Burgh draws the plans
chapters.body: Ernest de Burgh, chief engineer for water supply and sewerage in the NSW Public Works Department, prepared a dam proposal in 1910 and followed it with detailed plans in 1918. The idea sat on the shelf through wars and depression while Sydney leaned on its older catchments. It took the hard drought of the late 1930s to force the government's hand.

chapters.heading: The emergency scheme
chapters.body: In 1940 a weir and pumping station were finished downstream as the Warragamba Emergency Scheme, a stopgap to draw river water during the drought. The scheme proved the catchment could carry Sydney's thirst. In 1943 the Metropolitan Water, Sewerage and Drainage Board sent geologist William Browne to test the gorge, and on 2 October 1946 the board formally accepted the site for a full dam.

chapters.heading: A town and a wall of concrete
chapters.body: Construction of the main dam began in 1948 under board engineer Thomas Upton, with the American dam authority Dr John Lucian Savage brought in to confirm the site. The board built the township of Warragamba to house the workforce, which peaked at around a thousand at the headworks and a thousand more on the pipeline. The finished wall stands 142 metres high and holds roughly 1.2 million cubic metres of concrete.

images.caption: Tunnel construction works at Warragamba Dam during the building of the wall.

chapters.heading: Lake Burragorang rises
chapters.body: Premier Robert Heffron officially opened the dam on 14 October 1960, after twelve years of work. Behind the wall the water rose to form Lake Burragorang, one of the largest reservoirs for urban water supply in the world, with a full capacity of more than 2,000 gigalitres. Fifteen workers died during the construction, the pipeline work and the clearing of the valley, and they are remembered by a memorial in nearby Haviland Park.

images.caption: The Warragamba Dam wall holding back Lake Burragorang.

chapters.heading: What the lake covered
chapters.body: The filling of Lake Burragorang drowned the Burragorang Valley, the heart of Gundungurra country. More than 4,800 hectares went under the water, and with it an estimated four in five of the recorded sites that mattered to Gundungurra people, including scar trees, rock art, burials, ceremony grounds and the paths of songlines. The Gundungurra had fought to keep these river flats from the earliest colonial surveys of the 1820s, and the lake took what the land grants had not.

chapters.heading: Spillways and a contested future
chapters.body: Flood studies in the 1980s warned the original spillway was too small, so the wall was modified and an auxiliary spillway was completed to pass extreme floods safely. The dam was listed on the NSW State Heritage Register on 18 November 1999. A later proposal to raise the wall by fourteen metres for flood mitigation drew fierce opposition over the threat to Gundungurra sites and World Heritage parkland, and the NSW Government announced in 2023 that it would not proceed.

images.caption: The main spillway in full flow during the wet year of 1974.

title: White Bay Power Station: The Engine of the Trams

chapters.heading: A power house for the trams
chapters.body: By 1910 Sydney's electric tram system was straining the supply that ran it, and the Railway Commissioners also wanted to electrify the city's suburban railway. Their answer was a large generating station on the Balmain peninsula, where a low-lying foreshore gave easy rail and dock access for coal in and ash out, and the harbour gave an endless supply of cooling water. Construction began in June 1912. White Bay was built first and foremost to keep the trams moving.

chapters.heading: Slowed by the Great War
chapters.body: Boilers and alternators were given a test run in July 1913, before the buildings around them were even finished. The First World War then dragged out the work, holding up materials and labour for years. The station finally came fully on line in May 1917, with English-built turbo-alternators from makers such as Willans and Robinson and Dick, Kerr and Company. From the start it fed the 25 cycle traction current that the trams and the electrified railway needed.

images.caption: White Bay Power Station seen from the water, the brickwork and stacks that grew through the early twentieth century.

chapters.heading: Growing through the boom
chapters.body: The station was never finished in one go. A second building phase between 1923 and 1928 added more 25 cycle machines for the tramways and the first 50 cycle, or ordinary mains, plant, lifting capacity well past one hundred megawatts. This was the building learning to do two jobs at once, running the trams and feeding the wider electricity supply that lit homes and ran factories across the inner west.

chapters.heading: From railways to the grid
chapters.body: In January 1953 the station passed out of the hands of the Railway Commissioners and into those of the Electricity Commission of New South Wales. Larger Parsons turbo-alternators were installed through the 1950s, and by 1958 the plant reached its greatest output of around 186 megawatts. As Sydney's trams were wound down toward their end in 1961, the old traction role faded and White Bay worked on as a straightforward contributor to the state grid.

images.caption: A heritage plan of the site showing the boiler house, turbine hall and coal handling structures.

chapters.heading: Last light on Christmas Day
chapters.body: White Bay outlasted every one of its sibling stations around the harbour, the longest serving of Sydney's metropolitan power stations. It generated its final electricity on Christmas Day 1983 and fell silent after sixty six years of work. Pyrmont, Ultimo and Bunnerong were demolished, but White Bay was left standing, soot stained and complete, an industrial cathedral marooned at the edge of the water.

chapters.heading: A film set in waiting
chapters.body: Through the decades after closure the empty halls were stripped of asbestos and most machinery, yet the great steel frames and gantries remained. Filmmakers were drawn to the grime and scale, and the station appeared in The Matrix Reloaded and Baz Luhrmann's The Great Gatsby among others. On 2 April 1999 it was added to the State Heritage Register, valued as a rare and largely intact early twentieth century harbourside generating complex. Around 2000 the Sydney Harbour Foreshore Authority bought the site for roughly four million dollars.

chapters.heading: Doors open again
chapters.body: After a restoration costing about one hundred million dollars, the building reopened to the public on 9 March 2024 as the centrepiece venue for the twenty fourth Biennale of Sydney, titled Ten Thousand Suns. For the first time in generations visitors could walk the cavernous turbine hall and stand beneath the chimneys that still mark the western gateway to the city. The plan now is for White Bay to anchor the Bays West renewal as a permanent home for art, culture and community.

images.caption: The power station under renovation in 2023, its twin stacks still standing over the inner west.

title: The Finger Wharf: Wool, War and a Green Ban

chapters.heading: A wharf for a new harbour authority
chapters.body: Woolloomooloo Bay had carried Sydney's shipping for decades when the new Sydney Harbour Trust set out to modernise it. The trust's engineer-in-chief, Henry Deane Walsh, designed a single great finger wharf to replace the old jumble of jetties on the bay. Work began in 1910 on ground that had once held the city's fish market.

images.caption: Workers lay the deck of the new Woolloomooloo Finger Wharf around 1912.

chapters.heading: The largest timber wharf in the world
chapters.body: The wharf was largely finished by 1913 and completed in 1915, with a thirty metre extension added in 1916. It runs about 410 metres long and 64 metres wide, carried on thousands of turpentine timber piles driven into the harbour floor. Reports of the day called it the largest wooden wharf in the world, and it remains the largest surviving timber pile wharf anywhere.

chapters.heading: Wool, ships and the working harbour
chapters.body: Through the 1920s the wharf became a major centre for the wool trade, with Sydney's only wool dumps where hydraulic presses crushed bales tight for shipping. A northern shed went up in 1926 for storage and carpentry. For about seventy years the wharf handled wool and cargo and berthed large ocean liners, the steady engine of a working port.

chapters.heading: Soldiers off to two wars
chapters.body: During both world wars the wharf served as an embarkation point for troops boarding converted passenger liners. Men marched onto its decks bound for the fighting overseas, and many came back the same way. That role gave the timber sheds a deep place in the memory of families across New South Wales.

chapters.heading: A door into a new country
chapters.body: In 1956 Shed No. 7 was converted into a passenger terminal, and the wharf became one of the principal arrival points in Sydney Harbour. For thousands of postwar migrants it was the first piece of Australia they set foot on. By the 1970s, containerisation and newer ports had drained away its cargo trade and left the great structure idle.

images.caption: Inside the long timber hall of the Finger Wharf, photographed in 2018.

chapters.heading: The fight to save it
chapters.body: By 1987 the Maritime Services Board and the government were proposing to pull the wharf down for a marina and resort. A conservation order under the Greiner government in 1989 was followed by fresh moves to allow demolition. When crews arrived in January 1991, locals blocked the gates and unions placed a green ban, backed by the Friends of the Finger Wharf, the architects and the National Trust, and the bulldozers were stopped.

chapters.heading: Saved, then reborn
chapters.body: After the public outcry the plan changed from demolition to renovation. A redevelopment was approved and the wharf reopened around 2000 as a hotel, apartments, restaurants and a marina, ready for the Sydney Olympics. It was listed on the NSW State Heritage Register in April 2000, and its loft apartments have drawn residents including the actor Russell Crowe.

images.caption: The restored Finger Wharf seen from the south east in 2023.

title: Wylie's Baths: A Family's Pool on the Coogee Rock Shelf

chapters.heading: The swimmer who cut a pool from the rock
chapters.body: Henry Alexander Wylie was an Irish-born house painter who happened to be one of the finest swimmers in the colony, taking the Australasian distance-diving title in 1896. He took out a special lease over the rock shelf below the high-water mark at the southern end of Coogee Beach and set about shaping a pool from the stone. The baths opened on 12 December 1907 and gave Coogee its first real public recreation ground by the water. From the start the sea washed straight in over the rocks, so the pool filled and freshened with every tide.

chapters.heading: Men and women in the same water
chapters.body: Wylie's Baths was one of the first swimming spots in Australia to allow men and women to bathe together, at a time when most beaches and baths kept the sexes strictly apart by hour or by fence. That single decision made the place feel modern and a little daring for its day. It also made the baths a natural home for serious women swimmers who needed proper water to train in. The National Trust later judged Wylie's to be the oldest surviving communal sea baths in the country.

chapters.heading: Mina Wylie trains for Stockholm
chapters.body: Henry's daughter Wilhelmina, known to everyone as Mina, was born in 1891 and grew up training in her father's pool alongside her friend and rival Fanny Durack. In 1912 the pair travelled to the Stockholm Olympics, the first Games to admit women swimmers, and stood together on the podium for the 100 metres freestyle. Durack took the gold and Mina took the silver, making them the first women in the world to win Olympic swimming medals. Between 1906 and 1934 Mina collected 115 state and national titles, and she was elected to the International Swimming Hall of Fame in 1975.

chapters.heading: The timber boardwalk takes shape
chapters.body: In 1912 Wylie added the high timber boardwalk that still hugs the cliff and encloses two sides of the pool. The deck turned a simple tidal pool into a proper sea-bathing establishment with room to sit, change and watch the surf. The pool itself runs about fifty yards along the rock and some thirty metres across. The weathered timber and the open ocean setting have made Wylie's a favourite of photographers and painters ever since.

images.caption: The timber deck of Wylie's Baths above the tidal pool at Coogee.

chapters.heading: Sunstrip and the long detour
chapters.body: Henry Wylie ran the baths for more than half a century before the lease changed hands. On 5 November 1959 it passed to Desmond Selby, who renamed the pool Sunstrip. The Wylie name disappeared from the rock shelf for nearly two decades. In 1978 the baths reopened under the control of Randwick Council and the original name was restored.

chapters.heading: Restoration and a place on the register
chapters.body: In 1994 architect Keith Cottier led a major restoration of the baths in partnership with Randwick City Council, work that went on to earn a Greenway Award from the Royal Australian Institute of Architects. The Wylie's Baths Trust took over day-to-day management in 1996 and has run the place for the community ever since. On 14 November 2003 Wylie's Baths was added to the New South Wales State Heritage Register. More than a century after Henry cut his pool from the rock, swimmers still climb down to the same shelf and slip into the same tide.

images.caption: Wylie's Baths set into the rock shelf south of Coogee Beach.

title: Anzac Parade: the road that marched to war and rode home on rails

chapters.heading: A sandy track to the racecourse
chapters.body: Before it carried any grand name, this was simply the way south from Moore Park toward the racecourse and the sandhills beyond. A road was formed in the 1860s to reach the revived Randwick Racecourse and the rifle ranges, camps and market gardens out on the dunes toward Botany Bay. For decades it was known plainly as Randwick Road, a working route across a thinly settled stretch of the eastern suburbs.

chapters.heading: Five roads become one
chapters.body: In 1917 the City of Sydney drew several discrete roadways into a single ceremonial avenue, joining Randwick Road to other sections such as Eastern Avenue and Bunnerong Road. The unified thoroughfare was named Anzac Parade in memory of the men of the First Australian Imperial Force who had marched along it. Many of those soldiers had paraded down the route from the Randwick and Kensington camps on their way to the harbour wharves to embark for the Great War.

chapters.heading: The obelisk at the head of the parade
chapters.body: To mark the renaming, a sandstone obelisk roughly six metres high was raised at the Moore Park end of the avenue and dedicated in 1917. It is one of the earliest Anzac monuments in the country, older than the Martin Place Cenotaph of 1927 and the Hyde Park Anzac Memorial completed in 1934. The Lady Mayoress unveiled the bronze lettering at a brief ceremony when the parade was formally declared open.

images.caption: An Anzac Corps memorial near the parade at the University of New South Wales Kensington campus.

chapters.heading: Trams down the middle
chapters.body: For much of the twentieth century trams ran along Anzac Parade on their way to Maroubra and La Perouse. South of Kingsford the cars used a reservation down the centre of the divided road, which is why the avenue still carries its broad grassy median today. The La Perouse line was the very last to operate in Sydney, and the final service ran on 25 February 1961 before buses took over the route.

images.caption: Anzac Parade runs south through Kingsford and Maroubra with its wide central median once used by trams.

chapters.heading: University avenue
chapters.body: The old army barracks at Kensington became the seed of a new institution after the Second World War, and the University of New South Wales now lines the eastern side of the parade. The National Institute of Dramatic Art also sits along the route, and the racecourse and Centennial Park bracket its northern reaches. A military road of departures had slowly become a corridor of learning and recreation.

chapters.heading: Rails return after sixty years
chapters.body: More than half a century after the last tram, steel returned to the median. The CBD and South East Light Rail brought the L2 line from Circular Quay through Moore Park and down Anzac Parade to Randwick, with passenger services beginning on 14 December 2019. The L3 branch to Kingsford followed on 3 April 2020, tracing much the same path the old trams had once taken to the south east.

images.caption: The Kingsford light rail stop on Anzac Parade ahead of the line opening.

title: Bridge Street: The Spine of the Infant Colony

chapters.heading: A stream that split the town
chapters.body: When the First Fleet came ashore in January 1788, the fresh water of the Tank Stream became the line that divided the new settlement. Governor Arthur Phillip and the civil officers took the eastern bank, while the marines and convicts were quartered on the western side. The route that would become Bridge Street began as the simple track linking the governor's house to the working town across the water.

images.caption: George Raper's view of the east side of Sydney Cove around 1789 shows the governor's residence at the head of the future Bridge Street.

chapters.heading: The first bridge in the colony
chapters.body: In October 1788 convicts threw a timber log bridge across the Tank Stream near where Pitt Street now crosses, the first bridge built in New South Wales. It was damaged and repaired in April 1792. A stone replacement was raised by the mason Isaac Peyton in 1803 to 1804, but the work was so poor that it collapsed soon after and had to be rebuilt.

chapters.heading: First Government House at the head of the road
chapters.body: At the eastern end stood First Government House, finished around 1789 from English bricks and local stone, with two storeys and six rooms. It served as the home and seat of eight successive governors and remained the hub of the colony for 56 years. The building was demolished in 1845 so the street could be pushed through to Macquarie Street, and its foundations now lie beneath the Museum of Sydney.

images.caption: John William Lewin's watercolour of about 1809 records First Government House before its demolition in the 1840s.

chapters.heading: Macquarie gives the street its name
chapters.body: Governor Lachlan Macquarie formally named Bridge Street in 1810, after the small crossing over the Tank Stream near the Pitt Street corner. By 1860 the stream had become little more than a foul sewer and was covered over entirely. The bridge that gave the street its name has long since vanished underground, yet the name has outlasted the water.

chapters.heading: The monumental quarter takes shape
chapters.body: Through the second half of the nineteenth century Bridge Street filled with grand sandstone offices of state. The old Treasury Building rose at the Macquarie Street corner from 1849, designed by Colonial Architect Mortimer Lewis in the Italian palazzo manner. James Barnet's Chief Secretary's Building followed, its first four levels built between 1873 and 1881 and its copper dome added in 1896.

images.caption: Fred Hardie photographed the Lands Office on Bridge Street in 1893, soon after its second stage was completed.

chapters.heading: The Lands Department and Macquarie Place
chapters.body: The Department of Lands Building, also by James Barnet, was raised in two stages, the first from 1876 to 1881 and the second from 1888 to 1892, its statues of explorers and surveyors looking down from the facade. Nearby Macquarie Place holds Francis Greenway's 1818 obelisk, from which road distances in the colony were measured, along with an anchor and cannon from HMS Sirius. Both the Lands and Chief Secretary's buildings were added to the State Heritage Register on 2 April 1999.

images.caption: The Department of Lands Building on Bridge Street still presents its full Victorian facade to the street today.

chapters.heading: Bridge Street today
chapters.body: Bridge Street now runs about 500 metres from George Street up to Macquarie Street, a short but dense corridor of government and financial offices. The old Treasury has become the InterContinental Sydney hotel, and a light rail stop now serves the street. The bridge is gone and the stream runs in tunnels below, but the line first drawn in 1788 still organises this corner of the city.

title: Broadway: The Wide Gateway West

chapters.heading: Out of the swamp
chapters.body: Long before it was grand, this was the low, boggy end of Parramatta Street, the colony's road west towards Parramatta. Blackwattle Creek crossed it at its lowest point and fed the Blackwattle Swamp in Ultimo, so the ground flooded and the air carried the smell of slaughterhouses and soap works. Travellers rested at nearby Lake Northam before tackling the climb up Brickfield Hill into town.

chapters.heading: Distillery, brewery and sugar
chapters.body: Industry came early because water and cheap land sat close together. Cooper's Distillery opened in 1826, and in 1835 Tooth and Co founded its Kent Brewery on the Chippendale side, a name that would dominate the street for the next 170 years. When John Harris sold blocks of his Ultimo Estate facing the street in 1833, hotels and public houses sprang up to serve the carters and drovers passing through.

chapters.heading: From Parramatta Street to George Street West
chapters.body: The toll gate where George and Pitt streets met marked the edge of town until the middle of the nineteenth century. In 1877 the authorities renamed Parramatta Street as George Street West, a way of declaring that this stretch belonged to the city rather than the vague country beyond. Trams reached the street in 1882, and the growth of the new Central Railway Station pulled crowds and commerce towards its western end.

images.caption: George Street West, the future Broadway, around 1910 looking from the heights of Grace Bros.

chapters.heading: The emporium with the clock towers
chapters.body: English brothers Joseph Neal and Edward Albert Grace had opened a small shopfront here in the mid 1880s. In 1904 they raised a five-storey building on the western corner of Bay Street, and through the 1900s their grand emporium with its landmark clock towers turned the precinct into one of Sydney's busiest shopping destinations. Bon Marche and Marcus Clark soon joined them, and the wide new Railway Square gave the street the airy feel that earned it a borrowed New York nickname.

images.caption: The clock towers of the former Grace Bros emporium still stand over Broadway.

chapters.heading: Becoming Broadway
chapters.body: The name Broadway, or The Broadway, had been informally attached to the street since Railway Square was widened, and Grace Bros adopted it more as a wish than a fact. Properties on the Chippendale side were gazetted for resumption in 1924, and the road was formally renamed Broadway in the mid 1930s. The widening that followed even forced St Benedict's Catholic Church to be shortened by about 16 feet, with its entrance moved to the old rear.

images.caption: A Santa Claus display at Grace Bros on Broadway in November 1946, photographed by Sam Hood.

chapters.heading: Last orders, then a green tower
chapters.body: Grace Bros traded for around ninety years before the Broadway flagship closed in 1995. Carlton and United had bought the Kent Brewery from Tooth's in 1983 and grown the holding to about six hectares, but the last beer was brewed there in the mid 2000s. Demolition began in 2008, and the site reopened in 2014 as Central Park, crowned by Jean Nouvel's plant-clad One Central Park tower.

images.caption: One Central Park rises on Broadway over the former Kent Brewery site.

chapters.heading: A street of students and shoppers
chapters.body: The old retail palaces did not vanish so much as change hands, with the Grace Bros complex reborn as the Broadway Shopping Centre. The University of Technology Sydney spread along the street from its formation in 1988, its brutalist tower still the tallest thing on Broadway, and Notre Dame opened a campus on the St Benedict's site in 2006. The wide gateway west now carries students and shoppers where carters once hauled their loads out of town.

title: George Street: The Colony's High Street

chapters.heading: A track worn beside the Tank Stream
chapters.body: Before there was a street there was a path. The Gadigal walked this line down to the harbour to fish and gather, and the first colonists followed it from 1788, wearing a rough track along the western side of the Tank Stream from the Rocks to the brick pits in the south. Water carriers used it so often that the northern end picked up the name Spring Row. The same stretch was also known as Sergeant Major's Row, after the military camp it served.

chapters.heading: Macquarie names the High Street
chapters.body: For its first two decades the road was usually just called the High Street, in the plain English habit of naming a town's main artery. In 1810 Governor Lachlan Macquarie straightened and widened it and gave it a proper name, George Street, in honour of King George III. It was among the first of the ordered, named streets Macquarie laid out as he tried to turn a straggling camp into a real town.

images.caption: John Rae painted George Street looking north in 1842, with the markets and old burial ground on the right.

chapters.heading: The Victorian commercial spine
chapters.body: By the middle of the nineteenth century George Street had grown into the colony's busiest commercial corridor, lined with banks, warehouses and shopfronts. Alfred Tischbauer painted the streetscape in 1883, capturing a thoroughfare already dense with carriages and crowds. The road surface itself improved in stages, from packed dirt to wooden block paving and later, in the 1930s, to asphalt.

images.caption: Alfred Tischbauer's 1883 view of George Street shows the busy Victorian commercial heart of the city.

chapters.heading: Emporiums and arcades
chapters.body: In the boom decades the great retail palaces rose along and beside the street. The Strand Arcade opened in 1892 and the Queen Victoria Building, fronting George Street, opened in 1898. Further south at Brickfield Hill stood Anthony Hordern and Sons, whose Haymarket store burned down on 10 July 1901 and whose grand New Palace Emporium opened in 1905. George Street had become the place where Sydney came to shop.

chapters.heading: The age of the electric tram
chapters.body: Sydney's first horse trams of the 1860s ran along Pitt Street, not George Street, so George Street had to wait for its turn. On 8 December 1899 an electric tramway opened along it, running through to Harris Street and quickly making the road the city's busiest transport artery. For sixty years the trams clanged past the emporiums until they were withdrawn in 1959 and replaced by diesel buses.

images.caption: An electric tram rounds the David Jones corner on George Street around 1900.

chapters.heading: Congestion and the pedestrian turn
chapters.body: The bus decades brought their own troubles. By 1987 the traffic was so thick that a walk from Circular Quay to Town Hall could beat the bus, and bus lanes followed in July 1997. The New South Wales government announced a light rail project on 13 December 2012, and construction tore up the street from October 2015. The disruption was long and bitterly debated by traders.

chapters.heading: Rails return to the spine
chapters.body: Light rail services began running on 14 December 2019, closing a loop back to George Street's days as a tram street. The central stretch was turned into a pedestrian mall in stages between 2019 and 2021, clearing the cars that had ruled it for half a century. Today the oldest street in the country carries trams and walkers again, much as it once did.

images.caption: A test light rail train runs on the newly pedestrianised George Street in September 2019.

title: King Street, Newtown: The Road That Time Forgot to Knock Down

chapters.heading: An old road south
chapters.body: Long before it had a name in English, King Street followed the line of an Aboriginal track running south from Sydney Cove towards Botany Bay and the Cooks River. Colonists later used the same route as the road out of town, calling it the Cooks River Road and then the Newtown Road. It was eventually named King Street, in honour of Philip Gidley King, the colony's third governor.

chapters.heading: How a grocer's sign named a suburb
chapters.body: The popular story is that the suburb took its name from a store run by John and Margaret Webster around 1832, whose sign read New Town Stores. Historians have since shown the names New Town and Newtown were already in use in the 1820s, so the grocer probably borrowed the name rather than invented it. Either way, the open ground along the road was filling with cottages and shops within a generation.

chapters.heading: The railway changes everything
chapters.body: On 26 September 1855 the colony's first railway opened, and Newtown was one of only four stops between Sydney and Parramatta. Cheap, fast transport turned the strip into one of Sydney's earliest suburban shopping centres, and tradesmen and shopkeepers came to make up roughly three quarters of the local workforce. By the 1880s and 1890s King Street was crowded with terraces, pubs and ornate shopfronts serving a dense working-class population.

images.caption: St Georges Hall at 352 King Street, built in 1887, is one of the strip's grand Victorian survivors.

chapters.heading: Trams, then their absence
chapters.body: For decades electric trams ground up and down King Street, threading through the shoppers and carting workers to the city. The tracks needed constant attention, and crews were still relaying rails along the route in the late 1920s. The trams were pulled out in the 1950s, leaving the buses and the buildings behind.

images.caption: Workers relay tram tracks in Newtown in 1927, when the strip still ran on rails.

chapters.heading: Saved by being unfashionable
chapters.body: Through much of the twentieth century Newtown was a poor, blue-collar suburb, and King Street grew shabby. That neglect turned out to be its salvation. At the very moment when Victorian buildings were being torn down across Sydney, Newtown was simply too unprofitable to redevelop, so the late Victorian and Federation shopfronts were left standing almost intact.

chapters.heading: The dreamer on the wall
chapters.body: Over two nights in August 1991, artists Andrew Aiken and Juilee Pryor and their collective painted a huge portrait of Martin Luther King on a wall at 305 King Street, with the Earth from space beside it and the words I have a dream. Marrickville Council had twice refused permission, so it went up as a guerrilla mural, reportedly using a borrowed cherry picker and a thousand dollars of paint. It became the most famous and longest-surviving of Newtown's many murals, later joined by an Aboriginal flag at its base.

chapters.heading: The inner west's spine
chapters.body: Gentrification arrived from the 1970s and gathered pace, and King Street became the bohemian heart of the inner west, full of cafes, bookshops, music venues and second-hand stores. It is now widely regarded as the longest and most complete late-Victorian and Federation commercial strip in Australia, and development controls protect that character. The grand old shopfronts still stand, now serving students, artists and one of the city's busiest night-time crowds.

images.caption: King Street's Victorian shopfronts glow at dusk in 2019.

title: Macquarie Street: The Government Ridge

chapters.heading: A street gazetted for dignity
chapters.body: Macquarie Street was gazetted in October 1810, the easternmost street of the town, running south from the Government Domain to Hyde Park along the ridge east of the Tank Stream. Governor Lachlan Macquarie wanted a broad ceremonial thoroughfare that would give his rough penal settlement the look of an ordered colonial capital. From the start it was meant to be the best address in the colony, the face the government turned to the world.

chapters.heading: Built with rum
chapters.body: The colony's first proper hospital rose on the street between 1811 and 1816, paid for by an arrangement that gave the contractors a monopoly on rum imports rather than money from a reluctant British Treasury. People soon called it the Rum Hospital. It was built as three colonnaded sandstone blocks, and Macquarie's emancipist architect Francis Greenway criticised both its design and its shoddy construction.

images.caption: The former Rum Hospital wing serving as Parliamentary Buildings, photographed by Charles Pickering in 1872.

chapters.heading: Greenway's Georgian frontage
chapters.body: Greenway gave the southern end its finest buildings, the Hyde Park Barracks for convicts in 1819 and St James' Church facing it across Queen's Square, consecrated in 1824. These elegant Georgian works fixed the street's character as a place of public institutions. The Barracks would later house female immigrants and then destitute women, a reminder that grand buildings often change their purpose.

chapters.heading: From hospital wings to seats of state
chapters.body: The two surplus wings of the old hospital found new lives. The Legislative Council began meeting in the northern wing from 1829, and on 22 May 1856 it opened as the home of the new bicameral Parliament of New South Wales, now the oldest continuous parliament in Australia. The southern wing became a branch of the Royal Mint in 1854 after the gold rush, striking coin until the operation closed in 1926.

images.caption: A stereoscopic photograph of Macquarie Street and the government buildings, taken between 1860 and 1863.

chapters.heading: The best address in the colony
chapters.body: By the middle of the nineteenth century the street carried fine terraces and the residences of leading colonists, the kind of address that signalled standing. The painter Joseph Fowles recorded its mid-century mood, all open road and well-dressed riders. Cultural institutions gathered here too, the Free Public Library that grew into the State Library of New South Wales, and the Mitchell Library wing begun in 1907.

images.caption: Joseph Fowles painted Stephen Butts on a white horse in Macquarie Street around 1850.

chapters.heading: The Harley Street of the south
chapters.body: From the late nineteenth century the upper end filled with the consulting rooms of Sydney's leading doctors and specialists, drawn by the nearness of Sydney Hospital. The concentration of physicians and surgeons earned it the nickname of the Harley Street of the south. The terraces gave way to purpose-built medical chambers, and the street became as much about private practice as public power.

chapters.heading: The street as shorthand for power
chapters.body: In the twentieth century the name Macquarie Street became plain shorthand for the state government itself, the way Canberra or Whitehall stand in for a whole machinery of rule. New towers and the Law Courts at Queen's Square joined the old sandstone, and the medical precinct held its ground at the southern end. Today Parliament, the courts, the libraries and the specialists still sit side by side along the ridge Macquarie laid out.

images.caption: Macquarie Street and its sandstone government buildings in 2011.

title: Military Road: The Track to the Guns

chapters.heading: A first battery, then a road
chapters.body: A small battery was thrown up at Georges Head as early as 1801, when fear of French and Spanish raiders haunted the young colony. For seventy years after that the peninsula stayed largely bush and farm, reached only by rough tracks. The made road came later, built to serve a new and far heavier line of guns.

chapters.heading: Hauling the guns, 1871
chapters.body: When British troops withdrew in 1870, the colony took charge of its own defence and the Colonial Architect James Barnet laid out fresh batteries at Georges and Middle Heads, built between 1871 and 1873. To move the heavy gun barrels out to the headlands, work parties cut a proper military road from Milsons Point across the peninsula in 1871. Local memory holds that it took some three months and around 250 soldiers to roll the barrels the whole way. The route kept the name Military Road.

images.caption: A Broadhurst postcard showing the tram terminus at Mosman in the early twentieth century.

chapters.heading: The first electric tram in the colony
chapters.body: On 20 September 1893 an electric tramway opened from Ridge Street in North Sydney, running along Falcon Street and then out along Military Road to Spit Junction. It was the first permanent electric tram line in New South Wales, worked at first with experimental cars brought out from America. The trams turned a quiet defence road into a commuter spine almost overnight.

images.caption: An electric tram rounds the inclined road at Mosman Bay around 1897.

chapters.heading: Reaching the harbour and The Spit
chapters.body: In March 1897 the line was pushed down to Mosman wharf, linking the trams to the harbour ferries. In 1900 a branch ran on from Spit Junction along Spit Road towards The Spit, opened with some ceremony. The junction where the lines met gave Spit Junction its name, and it remains the busy heart of the strip today.

images.caption: The opening ceremony for the Spit Junction tram line in 1900.

chapters.heading: The high street grows up
chapters.body: With the trams came people, and with the people came shops. By around 1910 a continuous run of shopfronts lined Military Road, and it had become the high street of the lower north shore. Banks, picture theatres and grocers filled in behind the tram poles, fixing the road's commercial character for good.

chapters.heading: From rails to road
chapters.body: The trams ran for more than sixty years before the system was wound up, with the last Mosman service in June 1958. The rails came up and buses and cars took over, but the road kept the shape the tramway had given it. Military Road now carries the traffic of the lower north shore past the same shopfronts that grew up beside the tracks.

title: The Road to South Head

chapters.heading: The Maroo, a path before the road
chapters.body: Long before any survey peg went in, people of the Cadigal walked a ridge route out to the harbour mouth, a track known locally as the Maroo. It crossed country that was home to the Cadigal and likely the Biddigal and Birrabinnagal as well. The colonial road followed much the same line, so the modern arterial sits on a path that is far older than 1788.

chapters.heading: A signal flagged from the heights, 1790
chapters.body: In 1790 a signal station was set up on high ground near South Head to warn the struggling settlement of approaching ships. The trouble was that it could only be reached by boat, which made it slow to service and slow to relay news. The need to connect that lookout to the town overland is the reason the road exists at all.

images.caption: The South Head signal station that the road was first cut to reach.

chapters.heading: Harris cuts a track for a hundred pounds, 1803
chapters.body: In 1803 the surgeon John Harris took on a contract to clear a track from Sydney Town to the signal station, about fifteen feet wide, for one hundred pounds. Convict labour did the cutting through the scrub and sandstone ridges. The result was passable for a horse but rough and dangerous for any cart, so it was a beginning rather than a proper road.

chapters.heading: Macquarie makes a road, 1811
chapters.body: Governor Macquarie had the track upgraded in 1811 using convict and military labour, widening it so wheeled vehicles could finally use it. He pushed the work partly for defence and partly to reach the signal station and the lighthouse he was planning. From this point the route was genuinely a road out to South Head rather than a bridle path.

chapters.heading: The lighthouse on the road, 1818
chapters.body: Macquarie's lighthouse, designed by the convict architect Francis Greenway, was built near the road between 1816 and 1818 and first lit in 1818. It was the first lighthouse in Australia and gave the route a clear purpose beyond the signal flags. Greenway's soft sandstone tower decayed over the decades and was replaced in 1883 by a near replica designed by the Colonial Architect James Barnet.

images.caption: A drawing of the original Greenway lighthouse at South Head, made around 1840 to 1850, before it was replaced in 1883.

chapters.heading: A toll trust and a rival road
chapters.body: A second route closer to the harbour shore, the New South Head Road, was begun in 1831 and gradually took over much of the through traffic. To pay for upkeep, a government act of 1848 created a trust that put up turnpike gates and charged tolls. The trust managed the money poorly and the surface stayed rough for decades, and it was not wound up until 1904.

chapters.heading: Marine villas and the well to do
chapters.body: Through the middle of the nineteenth century the harbour-mouth heights at Vaucluse, Rose Bay and Watsons Bay filled with grand marine villas as the wealthy claimed the views. By the time this house at Bondi was photographed in 1937 the road carried suburban homes and gardens along much of its length. The character of the road had shifted from a defence track to a fashionable address.

images.caption: A home on Old South Head Road at Bondi, photographed by Sam Hood in 1937.

chapters.heading: Trams to the Gap, then the car
chapters.body: The tram line reached Watsons Bay in 1909, with a single track cut through rock in Gap Park that twisted down to the terminus and carried day-trippers to the eastern headlands. That service to Watsons Bay closed in 1960 and the cutting was later revegetated. Today the road is a busy eastern suburbs arterial, formally declared Main Road 339 in 1933, still running out to the lighthouse and the cliffs at the harbour mouth.

images.caption: Old South Head Road and the signal station at Vaucluse seen from the north today.

title: Oxford Street: The Road East

chapters.heading: A track along the ridge
chapters.body: Long before the British arrived, the Gadigal walked a route east along the sandy ridge that ran from the future town towards the southern headland of the harbour. In 1803 convicts cleared this track so the colony could reach the new signal station at South Head. It was rough work along a spine of poor sandstone soil, and for years the road was little more than a cleared path for foot and horse.

chapters.heading: South Head Road takes shape
chapters.body: In 1811 the ridge track was properly formed for wheeled traffic and became known as South Head Road. When the New South Head Road was begun in 1831, running closer to the water through Rushcutters Bay, the old ridge road lost its role as the main route to the headland. The original road then served excursions and the slow spread of suburb out towards Paddington.

chapters.heading: The barracks and the building boom
chapters.body: The construction of Victoria Barracks at Paddington through the 1840s, completed in 1848, pulled settlement and trade along the road. After the gold rushes of the 1850s the strip filled with shops and terraces, and in 1875 the section between Hyde Park and Paddington was renamed Oxford Street after the famous London shopping street. The name signalled what the road had become, a commercial high street rather than a country track.

chapters.heading: The busiest mile outside the city
chapters.body: A horse tramline opened along Oxford Street in 1880, running out to Moore Park and Randwick Racecourse, with a Bondi Junction line following in 1881. By the late 1880s the street was one of Sydney's most prominent shopping high streets, lined with drapers, tailors and emporiums. Zink and Sons, the tailors, began trading in 1895 and still work the street today, and Buckingham's department store opened in 1894.

images.caption: Oxford Street photographed by Fred Hardie in the early 1890s when it was a thriving high street.

chapters.heading: Taylor Square and the widening
chapters.body: Taylor Square was carved out in 1907 after the demolition of a hotel at the meeting of Bourke, Flinders and Oxford streets, and was named for the Lord Mayor, Allen Taylor. An underground public lavatory built there in 1907 is the oldest of its kind surviving in Sydney. By 1910 the lower end of the street was a crowded jumble of trams, hansom cabs and horse carts, photographed at Hyde Park Corner just before the road was widened.

images.caption: Oxford Street looking from Hyde Park Corner in 1910, just before the road was widened.

chapters.heading: Trams gone, fortunes faded
chapters.body: The trams ran along Oxford Street until 25 February 1961, when Sydney's tram system was finally wound up in favour of buses. The old high street drifted into a quieter, shabbier stretch through the 1960s, and Buckingham's department store burned down on Anzac Day in 1968. Yet the cheaper rents and the run of late-night venues set the scene for the street's next life.

images.caption: Oxford Street in 1968, by then a faded shopping strip on the edge of the city.

chapters.heading: The night of 24 June 1978
chapters.body: On the night of 24 June 1978, marking nine years since the Stonewall riots, a crowd led by the Gay Solidarity Group set off down Oxford Street towards the city. Police blocked the way at Hyde Park, confiscated the lead truck and its loudspeaker, and the marchers turned towards Kings Cross where officers moved in and arrested 53 people. Many were beaten in the cells, and the Sydney Morning Herald printed the names and occupations of those charged. All charges were dropped by the end of 1979, and the marchers became known as the 78ers.

chapters.heading: The heart of LGBTQ Sydney
chapters.body: From that confrontation grew the Sydney Gay and Lesbian Mardi Gras, and Oxford Street became the recognised home of queer Sydney, its venues, bars and parade route earning it the name the Golden Mile. In February 2013 a temporary rainbow crossing was painted at Taylor Square to mark the 35th Mardi Gras, the first of its kind in Australia. Today the street balances its role as a shopping and nightlife strip with its standing as the symbolic centre of the city's LGBTQ life.

images.caption: The 2013 rainbow crossing at Taylor Square, the first painted crossing of its kind in Australia.

title: Parramatta Road: The Colony's First Highway

chapters.heading: A track cut through the bush
chapters.body: Boats up the river were the surest way between Sydney Cove and the new farms at Rose Hill, the settlement that became Parramatta. Even so, between 1789 and 1791 convict gangs hacked a rough overland track through the bush to join the two places. It was barely three metres wide, and in wet weather it turned to mud, but it was the first inland road the colony ever had.

chapters.heading: Surveyed and named
chapters.body: The track was laid out as a proper route around 1797 under Surveyor-General Augustus Alt, and it soon carried the name the road to Parramatta. The early colony struggled to keep it open. After heavy rain in June 1805 the Sydney Gazette reported the road was simply impassable, with no bridges over the creeks that crossed it.

chapters.heading: Macquarie's turnpike
chapters.body: Governor Lachlan Macquarie arrived in 1810 determined to fix the colony's roads, and he put convict chain gangs to work widening and stone-paving the route. He opened it as a turnpike on 10 April 1811, the first toll road in Australia, with a tollgate near present-day Railway Square and another out towards Parramatta. The tolls funded upkeep, and by 1815 the road had returned a profit of about 465 pounds.

chapters.heading: Inns, coaches and the long ride west
chapters.body: Coaching inns sprang up along the road, and a stage cart service began running in 1814. By 1823 a regular stage coach left Sydney at seven in the morning and reached Parramatta by half past nine. The journey that had once taken a cart most of a day was now something a traveller could do before lunch.

images.caption: The University of Sydney seen from Parramatta Road in the early 1870s.

chapters.heading: Rail, trams and the ribbon suburbs
chapters.body: The Sydney to Parramatta railway opened in 1855 and took much of the long-distance traffic off the road. Steam trams began running along Parramatta Road in 1883, and the corridor filled with shops and terraces as suburbs such as Annandale, Leichhardt, Petersham and Camperdown grew up beside it. The road became a busy local high street rather than a lonely highway between two towns.

images.caption: Older shopfronts still line the road at Camperdown today.

chapters.heading: Factories and the working road
chapters.body: By the early twentieth century the road was sealed, the tramlines were lifted in the 1920s, and motor traffic took over. Big factories rose along the corridor, among them the Peak Frean biscuit works at Ashfield, an Art Deco landmark photographed by Ted Hood in 1937. The road carried the goods and workers of an industrial inner west.

images.caption: The Art Deco Peak Frean biscuit factory at the corner of Frederick Street and Parramatta Road, Ashfield, photographed in 1937.

chapters.heading: Auto Alley and after
chapters.body: From the post-war years the road became Sydney's great strip of car showrooms and neon, a stretch known as Auto Alley. The M4 motorway later siphoned off the through traffic, and the road slid into a reputation for congestion and tired shopfronts. Plans under the 2016 Parramatta Road Urban Transformation Strategy now aim to rebuild it with thousands of new homes, the latest chapter for Australia's oldest highway.

images.caption: Parramatta Road near the University of Sydney in 2005.

title: Pitt Street: The Commercial Spine

chapters.heading: A track beside the Tank Stream
chapters.body: In the first years of the settlement a rough track ran roughly parallel to George Street, keeping to the eastern side of the Tank Stream that gave the young town its water. The stream rose in swampy ground near what is now Market and Park Streets and ran down a shallow valley to Sydney Cove. Pitt Street grew up alongside that line, and the buried watercourse still runs beneath parts of the modern street.

chapters.heading: Pitt Row and the naming
chapters.body: The street is one of the earliest named in Sydney, first known as Pitt Row. It honoured William Pitt the Younger, the British Prime Minister of the day. The naming dates to the colony's earliest years under Governor Arthur Phillip, not to Macquarie's time, though by Macquarie's governorship after 1810 the street was well established as part of the town grid.

chapters.heading: The Victorian commercial street
chapters.body: Through the middle of the nineteenth century Pitt Street hardened into a place of commerce. The Royal Exchange opened in 1853 at the corner of Pitt and Bridge Streets, and solemn banks rose in the revivalist styles of the age. This 1878 view, made for the City of Sydney Improvement Board, shows the west side near King Street with its awnings, verandahs and crowded shopfronts.

images.caption: The west side of Pitt Street near King Street, photographed in December 1878 for the City of Sydney Improvement Board.

chapters.heading: Trams and the retail boom
chapters.body: The first horse trams ran along Pitt Street from the old Redfern railway station to Circular Quay from 1861, and steam and then electric trams followed by the early 1900s. The finest shops gathered between King and Market Streets, and the Strand Arcade opened in 1891 as a Victorian shopping arcade with an iron and glass roof. By the turn of the century Pitt Street was the busy commercial and financial heart of the city.

images.caption: The corner of Bridge and Pitt Streets decorated for the visit of the Duke of Cornwall in May 1901.
