chapters.heading: Between the wars
chapters.body: By the 1930s Pitt Street carried the full weight of a modern city, with crowds, trams and great institutions lining the way. Sam Hood caught it on a wet day around 1933, umbrellas open on the stretch between the General Post Office and the Commonwealth Bank. The city trams kept running along the street until the system closed in 1957, after which buses took over.

images.caption: Pitt Street on a rainy day around 1933, between the GPO and the Commonwealth Bank, photographed by Sam Hood.

chapters.heading: The Mall
chapters.body: In February 1987 the block between King and Market Streets closed to traffic and became the Pitt Street Mall. The change was not entirely smooth, and vehicles were briefly reintroduced to the King to Hunter Street section in March of that year as traffic concerns were worked through. The Mall settled into its role as one of the busiest shopping strips in the country.

images.caption: Pitt Street looking south from Market Street toward the Mall, photographed in 2024.

chapters.heading: Pitt Street today
chapters.body: The street still runs as a commercial spine from near the old Tank Stream down through the city centre. The pedestrian Mall draws tens of thousands of people on an ordinary weekday, with the Strand Arcade, department stores and the towers above the old Centrepoint site clustered around it. A cycleway installed in 2020 became permanent in 2021, a small sign that the street keeps changing as Sydney does.

title: Victoria Road: The Long Road to Ryde

chapters.heading: A punt instead of a road
chapters.body: Before there was any road worth the name, travellers bound for Ryde and the orchard country took Parramatta Road, then the Great North Road, then a punt across the Parramatta River. That punt ran between Bedlam Point and Abbottsford and had carried stock and carts since 1831. The Pyrmont and Glebe Island bridges of 1857 made the city side easier to reach and sharpened the call for a more direct line out to the north-west.

chapters.heading: The first crossing at Gladesville, 1881
chapters.body: On 1 February 1881 the first Gladesville Bridge opened across the Parramatta River between Drummoyne and Huntleys Point, ending the old reliance on the Bedlam Point punt. It was a low steel lattice truss bridge on sandstone piers, with a swing span at the southern end that opened to let tall ships and high-funnelled steamers through. Among those that needed the gap were the sixty-miler colliers running coal down from Newcastle to the gasworks at Mortlake.

images.caption: The first Gladesville Bridge photographed around 1900 with its low steel spans and central swing section.

chapters.heading: Villages, trams and a working river
chapters.body: With the river bridged, small villages along the route at Drummoyne and Gladesville grew into proper suburbs. A tramline was laid across the Gladesville Bridge about twenty years after it opened, and in time the trams and carts together choked the narrow deck at peak hours. Below them the Parramatta River stayed a busy working waterway, with ferries and colliers threading past the swing span.

images.caption: The Sydney ferry Birkenhead passes beneath the old Gladesville Bridge in the early 1900s.

chapters.heading: Victoria Road takes its modern shape
chapters.body: The road was formally pulled together as a single arterial under the Main Roads Act of 1924, and Main Road No. 165 was gazetted on 8 August 1928. Through the Depression years the Department of Main Roads bought up and pulled down buildings that crowded the road reserve, holding the ground for a future six lanes. At Ryde a four-lane subway under the railway opened on 24 July 1937, doing away with a dangerous level crossing.

images.caption: Bridge Street at Drummoyne looking towards Gladesville before the road was rebuilt, photographed before 1946.

chapters.heading: The great concrete arch, 1964
chapters.body: A new four-lane Iron Cove Bridge opened on 30 June 1955, and the old swing bridge at Gladesville was replaced by an enormous concrete arch that opened on 2 October 1964. Designed by Anthony Gee with G. Maunsell and Partners and reviewed by the French engineer Eugene Freyssinet, it was built by Reed and Mallik of England and Stuart Brothers of Sydney. Its single arch spanned 305 metres, the longest concrete arch in the world, and it held that record until 1980. It is also remembered as one of the first large bridges to be designed with the help of a computer.

chapters.heading: The A40 today
chapters.body: Victoria Road now runs about twenty kilometres from Rozelle out through Drummoyne, Gladesville, Ryde and Ermington to Parramatta, carried as Route A40 since the alphanumeric changeover of 2013. It remains one of the busiest and most congested corridors in the inner west, crawling at little more than walking pace in the morning peak. Sandstone abutments of the 1881 bridge still stand on the riverbanks, and the 1964 arch was added to the State Heritage Register on 1 October 2014.

title: William Street: The Grand Approach to the Cross

chapters.heading: A road cut for the gentry
chapters.body: William Street was pushed through farmland in 1834 to link the town with the fashionable ridge of villas at Darlinghurst, Potts Point and Elizabeth Bay. It was named in honour of King William IV, who sat on the throne when the road opened. The Surveyor-General Thomas Mitchell had wanted the line set further south, but it was laid out in its present position while he was away exploring the interior. From the start it was the principal route to the eastern foreshore suburbs.

chapters.heading: The boulevard that never quite arrived
chapters.body: In 1908 a Royal Commission for the improvement of Sydney recommended that this crowded approach be widened into a proper boulevard. The decision to resume the southern side of the street followed in 1909, and the resumptions were carried out between 1910 and 1914. Planners dreamed of a Sydney answer to the Champs-Elysees, a grand sweep climbing the hill to the Cross. The street was broadened, yet the dignified avenue of the drawings never fully took shape.

images.caption: William Street around 1930, from the Royal Australian Historical Society collection.

chapters.heading: Motor mile and the busiest corner in town
chapters.body: Between the wars William Street became the heart of Sydney's motor trade, lined with showrooms and the offices of the great car brands. Yorks Motors, in William House, was made the sole New South Wales distributor of Plymouth and Chrysler cars in 1932. Trams climbed the hill towards the junction with Darlinghurst Road and Victoria Street, which grew into one of the city's busiest and most clogged corners. The street had settled into its role as the bright and slightly raffish way into the Cross.

chapters.heading: From rails to rubber
chapters.body: The trams that had defined the climb were withdrawn when the line closed on 10 July 1960, their work handed over to buses and the new Eastern Suburbs railway. Traffic only thickened, and in time engineers burrowed beneath the bottleneck. The Kings Cross Tunnel opened on 15 December 1975, carrying through traffic under the worst of the snarl. William Street kept its glow of neon and its reputation as a place that was equal parts glamour and grime.

images.caption: William Street in 1968, still very much Sydney's motor mile.

chapters.heading: The Coke sign and the gateway to the Cross
chapters.body: In 1974 a vast Coca-Cola billboard rose above the William Street and Darlinghurst Road junction, its original neon built from rows of red tubing and hundreds of fluorescent lamps. Known fondly as the Gateway to the Cross, it became the most famous sign in the country and a landmark more than an advertisement. A left-hand panel was added in 1990. The old neon was taken down in 2015, and a new energy-saving LED version was switched on for the first time on 15 September 2016.

images.url: https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/Special:FilePath/William Street and Kings Cross Coke sign, Sydney.jpg?width=1200
images.caption: The Coca-Cola sign glowing above William Street at night in 2008.
images.link: https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:William Street and Kings Cross Coke sign, Sydney.jpg

chapters.heading: A street remade for the new century
chapters.body: The street kept changing into the 2000s as the Cross City Tunnel and related roadworks reshaped the surface above. Footpaths were widened, plane trees were planted and the carriageway was tidied in an effort to soften the long traffic corridor. The car showrooms have largely given way to apartments, bars and offices climbing the rise. After almost two centuries William Street remains exactly what it was built to be, the grand approach that delivers Sydney up the hill to Kings Cross.

images.caption: William Street at dusk in 2012, looking west toward the city from the Kings Cross end.

title: The Man Who Made Sydney Exist

chapters.heading: A teacher's son from Bread Street
chapters.body: Arthur Phillip was born on 11 October 1738 in the London parish of Allhallows, the second child of Jacob Phillip, a language teacher who had come from Frankfurt, and Elizabeth Breach. The family was not wealthy. At twelve he entered the Greenwich Hospital school for the sons of seamen, and by his mid-teens he had begun his life at sea aboard a merchant whaler.

chapters.heading: Two navies and a long apprenticeship
chapters.body: Phillip enlisted in the Royal Navy and served through the Seven Years' War, earning his lieutenant's commission in 1761. Peace left him on half pay for long stretches, so in 1774 he took service with the Portuguese navy and fought with distinction against Spain. By the time the Admiralty looked for someone to plant a colony on the far side of the world, he was a steady, experienced and unusually thoughtful officer.

images.caption: Francis Wheatley painted this portrait of Arthur Phillip in 1786, the year he was chosen to command the First Fleet.

chapters.heading: The First Fleet sets sail
chapters.body: Appointed first Governor of New South Wales in October 1786, Phillip oversaw the fitting out of eleven ships carrying convicts, marines and stores. The fleet left Portsmouth on 13 May 1787 and crossed half the planet by way of Tenerife, Rio de Janeiro and the Cape of Good Hope. It reached Botany Bay in mid January 1788 after a voyage of more than eight months.

chapters.heading: Raising the flag at Sydney Cove
chapters.body: Botany Bay proved open, swampy and short of fresh water, so Phillip pushed a few miles north and found the deep, sheltered anchorage of Port Jackson. On 26 January 1788 he landed at a cove with a good stream and raised the flag, naming the settlement Sydney after Lord Sydney. He had brought roughly a thousand people to a shore that no European had ever settled.

images.caption: Algernon Talmage's 1937 painting imagines the founding of the settlement at Sydney Cove on 26 January 1788.

chapters.heading: Holding a starving colony together
chapters.body: The early years were grim. Crops failed in unfamiliar soil, supply ships did not come, and rations were cut again and again until officer and convict ate the same meagre share. Many of his marine officers wanted nothing to do with the place, and one called it the outcast of God's works. Phillip pushed farming inland to Parramatta and kept the colony alive by sheer steadiness until relief arrived.

chapters.heading: The Eora and the spear at Manly
chapters.body: Phillip was instructed to live in friendship with the first peoples of the harbour, and he tried hard to keep that faith. He had Bennelong and Colebee captured so he might learn their language and ways, a coercive start that nonetheless grew into real relationships. When he was speared in the shoulder at Manly Cove in September 1790, he forbade any reprisal, treating the wound as the payback custom of the Eora rather than an act of war.

images.caption: The Port Jackson Painter recorded the spearing of Governor Phillip at Manly Cove around 1790.

chapters.heading: Departure and final years at Bath
chapters.body: Chronic pain in his side wore Phillip down, and he sailed for England in December 1792 after almost five years in the colony, taking Bennelong and another Eora man, Yemmerrawanne, with him. He returned to naval service in the wars against France and rose to rear admiral. He married Isabella Whitehead in 1794 and settled in Bath, where he died on 31 August 1814 at the age of seventy five.

chapters.heading: Legacy
chapters.body: Phillip is remembered as the founder of Sydney and the first governor of New South Wales, the man whose patience and judgement carried a doomed-looking experiment through its worst years. His conciliatory approach to the Eora stood in sharp contrast to much of what followed, even as the colony he planted dispossessed the people who first welcomed his boat. Memorials to him stand in Bath Abbey and St James' Church in Sydney.

images.caption: A Wedgwood tribute vase in Bath honours Phillip as founder and first governor of Australia.

title: The Banjo: A City Man Who Wrote the Bush

chapters.heading: A grazier's son from Narrambla
chapters.body: Andrew Barton Paterson was born on 17 February 1864 at Narrambla, near Orange in central New South Wales, the eldest of seven children. His father, Andrew Bogle Paterson, was a Scottish-born grazier, and his mother, Rose Isabella Barton, came from Boree Nyrang station nearby. When the boy was about seven the family moved to Illalong station in the Yass district, where the world of horsemen, drovers and bush races that would later fill his verse took shape around him.

chapters.heading: Rockend and the road to Sydney Grammar
chapters.body: For his Sydney schooldays Barty was sent to board with his widowed grandmother, Emily Mary Barton, at her stone cottage 'Rockend' beside the Parramatta River at Gladesville. She was a cultivated, well-read woman, and it was she who nurtured his early taste for poetry. From the river he travelled in to Sydney Grammar School, matriculating at sixteen before turning to the law.

images.caption: A young Andrew Barton Paterson photographed in Sydney by the Falk Studio between 1885 and 1890.

chapters.heading: The solicitor who became 'The Banjo'
chapters.body: Paterson was admitted as a solicitor on 28 August 1886 and practised in the city, but his real life was in verse. His poems began appearing in The Bulletin under the pen name 'The Banjo', taken from a racehorse his family had owned. Clancy of the Overflow and The Man from Snowy River caught the public imagination, and in 1895 Angus and Robertson published the collection The Man from Snowy River, which sold strongly and made him the most popular poet in the country.

chapters.heading: Waltzing Matilda at Dagworth
chapters.body: In 1895 Paterson travelled to the Macpherson family's Dagworth station near Winton in western Queensland. There he heard Christina Macpherson play a tune she had picked up at Warrnambool, and he set words to it on the spot. The result was Waltzing Matilda, a song of a swagman, a billabong and a stolen sheep that has since become Australia's best-loved bush ballad and unofficial anthem.

chapters.heading: The Bulletin debate with Lawson
chapters.body: Across 1892 and 1893 Paterson and Henry Lawson carried on a famous quarrel in the pages of The Bulletin. Lawson painted the bush as a place of drudgery and despair, while Paterson, in poems such as In Defence of the Bush, answered with a sunnier and more romantic vision. It was partly a genuine argument and partly a paid performance, and both men knew the bush largely from the vantage of Sydney.

chapters.heading: War correspondent and soldier
chapters.body: Paterson sailed for South Africa in 1899 as a correspondent for the Sydney Morning Herald and the Age, riding with the columns and reporting the relief of Kimberley and the fall of Bloemfontein and Pretoria. He later reported the unrest in China. When the First World War came he drove ambulances with the Australian Voluntary Hospital in France, then took a commission and rose to major, commanding the Australian Remount Squadron that supplied horses to the army in Egypt.

images.caption: A studio portrait of Banjo Paterson from around the 1890s, held by the National Library of Australia.

chapters.heading: Marriage, the land and later years
chapters.body: On 8 April 1903 Paterson married Alice Emily Walker of Tenterfield station, and they had two children, Grace and Hugh. He edited the Sydney Evening News and later the Sydney Sportsman, and tried grazing at Coodra Vale and wheat farming near Grenfell, neither of which prospered. He gathered most of his work in Collected Verse in 1923, broadcast for the ABC, and was appointed C.B.E. in 1939.

chapters.heading: Legacy in song and on the note
chapters.body: Paterson died in Sydney on 5 February 1941, aged seventy-six. His ballads remain part of the common Australian voice, recited and sung far beyond the bush they describe. Since 1993 his portrait has appeared on the ten dollar note, fittingly replacing the old note that carried Henry Lawson, and statues and plaques mark his memory from Binalong to the Snowy country.

images.caption: A statue at Cooma commemorating Paterson's poem The Man from Snowy River.

title: Barangaroo: The Woman Who Refused the Colony

chapters.heading: Country and the women of the water
chapters.body: Barangaroo belonged to the Cammeraygal, the largest and most influential clan of the Eora, whose Country lay along the lower north shore of Sydney Harbour. The harbour was the heart of Eora life, and women were its main food providers. From bark canoes they fished with line and hook while watching over children, and the rhythm of that work ran through everything they knew.

images.caption: William Bradley painted Sydney Cove in 1788, the harbour country of the Eora that the First Fleet had just begun to occupy.

chapters.heading: First contact at Port Jackson
chapters.body: When the First Fleet arrived in 1788, the survey party led by Arthur Phillip met Cammeraygal women near Manly, one of the earliest recorded encounters between the British and Eora women. Lieutenant William Bradley painted the scene from his journal. These were the people and the waters into which Barangaroo would soon step as a figure of standing and resistance.

images.caption: Lieutenant William Bradley recorded the first interview with the Native Women at Port Jackson in 1788.

chapters.heading: A keeper of law and a fierce match
chapters.body: Barangaroo was no ordinary fisherwoman. After smallpox had killed so many elders, she was one of a small number of women who still held the knowledge of laws, teaching and women's rituals, and she exercised that authority over younger women. By all accounts she had presence and a strong will, and she would need both in the years that followed.

chapters.heading: Marriage to Bennelong
chapters.body: Having lost her first husband and two children, Barangaroo married Bennelong sometime after his escape from British captivity in May 1790. He was a younger, ambitious man, and observers described their union as both affectionate and tempestuous. They were seen feasting together and just as often arguing fiercely, especially as Bennelong spent more of his time among the colonists.

images.caption: An early portrait thought to depict Bennelong, the Wangal man who was Barangaroo's second husband.

chapters.heading: Refusing the colony
chapters.body: Where younger Eora women agreed to wear the clothes the British offered, Barangaroo refused outright, keeping only a thin bone through her nose. She objected to Bennelong's visits to Sydney and is said to have broken his fishing spear in protest. Part of her anger came from what the colony threatened, because the British hauled in huge catches that undercut the women's control of the harbour's food. When she was made to watch a convict flogged for stealing Aboriginal fishing gear in 1791, she grabbed at the whip and turned on the soldier.

chapters.heading: Dilboong and death
chapters.body: In August 1791 Barangaroo gave birth to a daughter, Dilboong. Bennelong wanted the child born at Government House, but Barangaroo chose to labour alone in the bush, and the officer David Collins noted her gathering firewood soon after. She became ill not long after the birth. Bennelong sent for Willemerring, a karadji or clever healer from Broken Bay, but help did not reach her and she died later that year.

chapters.heading: Cremation and a name on the water
chapters.body: Bennelong gave Barangaroo a traditional cremation, her fishing gear laid beside her, with a few chosen Eora and several British officers present. He then carried her ashes carefully into the garden of Government House, near what is now Circular Quay. Dilboong lived only a few months more. In 2007 the harbour headland on the western edge of the city was renamed Barangaroo, returning her name to the shoreline she had fished and refused to surrender.

images.caption: The Barangaroo precinct on Sydney Harbour now carries her name on the western foreshore.

title: Bennelong: The Wangal Man Who Walked Two Worlds

chapters.heading: Born to the Wangal
chapters.body: Bennelong came into the world around 1764, a Wangal man of the Eora, whose Country followed the southern shore of the harbour between the cove the British named Sydney and the river at Parramatta. He carried several names across his life, among them Woollarawarre, Wogultrowe, Boinba and Bundebunda, which some sources link to a hawk. He was a man of standing, with kin ties reaching south to the Georges River and Botany Bay and north across the water to Pittwater.

chapters.heading: Taken at Manly Cove
chapters.body: In November 1789 British marines seized Bennelong and another man, Colebee, near Manly, acting on Governor Phillip's wish to capture someone who could bridge the gap between the two peoples. Colebee soon got away, but Bennelong stayed for about six months. In that time he picked up English quickly and spoke of the smallpox that had swept through the Eora and killed so many of them. He escaped in May 1790, yet he did not simply vanish.

images.caption: An engraving of Bennelong published in 1798.

chapters.heading: The hut on the point
chapters.body: Bennelong returned to the settlement on his own terms and built a working relationship with Arthur Phillip that ran deeper than mere usefulness. The two men exchanged names in the Eora way, with Phillip taking the name Woollarawarre. At Bennelong's request a small brick hut with a tiled roof was put up for him in 1790 on the eastern point of Sydney Cove. Tubowgule, the place the Gadigal knew for its white clay, has been called Bennelong Point ever since.

images.caption: Bennelong Point, known as Tubowgule, photographed around 1873.

chapters.heading: Diplomat between two peoples
chapters.body: Bennelong worked as a negotiator and interpreter, moving between the Eora and the colonists with a skill the British rarely credited. He helped steer both sides toward an uneasy tolerance and used his knowledge of the newcomers to his own advantage. He was no mere informant. He read the British carefully, kept his own counsel, and held his place within Eora law and kinship throughout.

chapters.heading: The long voyage to England
chapters.body: In December 1792 Bennelong sailed for England with Phillip and the young Yemmerrawanne, becoming among the first Aboriginal people known to have made the journey. In London they saw the city, attended the theatre and were reportedly presented to King George III, though no firm record of that meeting survives. Yemmerrawanne fell ill and died at Eltham in May 1794, aged about nineteen, and was buried in the churchyard there. Bennelong endured the cold and the loss and finally sailed home aboard HMS Reliance, reaching Sydney in September 1795.

images.caption: Arthur Phillip, painted by Francis Wheatley in 1786, was Bennelong's counterpart and travelling companion.

chapters.heading: Home to the river
chapters.body: Bennelong came back to a changed world. Phillip had long since departed, and the marriage he returned to had ended. Rather than linger in the settlement as a curiosity, he turned toward the Parramatta River and the clans there. He married again, fathered a son named Dicky around 1803, and gradually slipped out of the colonial record on his own terms.

chapters.heading: An elder on his own Country
chapters.body: In his last years Bennelong lived among his people near Kissing Point, on land worked by the ex-convict brewer James Squire, who counted him and his clan as friends. Far from being a broken outcast, he held the standing of a senior Wangal elder. He died on 3 January 1813, aged about forty-nine, and was buried in Squire's orchard. A large gathering of Aboriginal men is said to have marked his passing in the customary way.

chapters.heading: A name on the harbour
chapters.body: For generations Bennelong was remembered through a colonial lens, dismissed as a man who failed to be civilised. That reading has been overturned by historians and by his descendants, who see a man of agency who chose his own people and his own law. His likely grave at Putney was pinpointed by research in 2010, and the New South Wales government bought the site in 2018 to make a lasting memorial. His name endures on the headland beneath the Opera House and on the federal electorate that bears it.

title: The Man Who Sailed Around His Own Country

chapters.heading: A Garigal man of Broken Bay
chapters.body: Bungaree was born around 1775 into the Garigal people, whose Country lay around Broken Bay and Brisbane Water, north of Sydney. His people carried the Garigal language onto the north shore of Port Jackson. He grew up in a world already shifting under the arrival of the British, and in the 1790s he came south into the new settlement, where his easy intelligence and command of English soon set him apart.

images.caption: Alphonse Pellion drew Aboriginal men at their camp near Sydney in December 1819.

chapters.heading: First voyages along the coast
chapters.body: Bungaree first went to sea in 1798, sailing with Matthew Flinders on HMS Reliance to Norfolk Island. The next year he joined Flinders again aboard the sloop Norfolk on a survey north along the coast, where his skill at reading Country and talking with the peoples they met proved its worth. These early trips marked him out as the man Flinders would want when a far larger voyage came.

chapters.heading: Around the continent with Flinders
chapters.body: When Flinders set out in HMS Investigator in 1801 to chart the entire coastline of New Holland, he took Bungaree as his intermediary. Through 1802 and 1803 the ship worked its way around the continent and across to Timor, and at landing after landing Bungaree stepped forward to negotiate safe and peaceful meetings with local peoples. By the end of the voyage he had become the first Australian-born person to circumnavigate the continent. Flinders commended his even disposition and his brave conduct.

images.caption: Bungaree stands in a cocked hat and coat in this colonial-era portrait.

chapters.heading: Diplomat of the harbour
chapters.body: Back in Sydney, Bungaree became the figure newcomers met first. He would row out to greet arriving ships and perform a faultless imitation of the bow and bearing of each governor he had known, a turn that delighted onlookers and quietly reminded them whose Country they had landed on. He spoke English well, kept a sharp sense of humour, and moved with confidence between the Eora world and the colonial one. In 1817 he sailed north-west again, this time with Phillip Parker King, who likewise praised his judgement and his skill at making contact.

chapters.heading: The farm at Gurrugal
chapters.body: On 31 January 1815 Governor Macquarie reserved land for Bungaree and his people at Georges Head, known as Gurrugal, near present-day Mosman. The governor provided huts, a boat, clothing, seed and farming tools, and convict instructors. He also hung on Bungaree a crescent-shaped brass breastplate inscribed Chief of the Broken Bay Tribe, a colonial invention since the Eora had no such office. The farming venture did not take, but the headland gave Bungaree a fine vantage over every ship entering the harbour.

chapters.heading: The face the colony remembered
chapters.body: Sometime in the 1820s the artist Augustus Earle painted Bungaree in a discarded officer's coat and cocked hat, standing against Sydney Harbour. The picture was engraved and reproduced widely, becoming one of the most familiar Australian images of the era. It is a complicated portrait, at once a record of a real and respected man and a piece of colonial theatre, and Bungaree clearly understood and used both readings.

images.caption: Augustus Earle's portrait of Bungaree, A Native Chief of New South Wales, in the 1820s.

chapters.heading: Death and legacy
chapters.body: Bungaree died at Garden Island on 24 November 1830 and was buried at Rose Bay, beside his wife Matora. He was survived by Cora Gooseberry, the widow who carried the title of queen on the streets of Sydney for another two decades. He left a name that endures across the map and the record, from Bribie Island in the north to the harbour he watched over, as a man who sailed around his own Country and met the colony on his own terms.

title: The Emigrant's Friend

chapters.heading: Northampton and a marriage of conscience
chapters.body: Caroline Jones was born on 30 May 1808 near Northampton, England, into a prosperous farming family with strong evangelical and philanthropic habits. At twenty-two she married Captain Archibald Chisholm of the East India Company, a Roman Catholic some thirteen years her senior. She converted to Catholicism around the time of the marriage, and reportedly agreed to it only on the understanding that she could continue her charitable work.

images.caption: An engraving of Caroline Chisholm after a daguerreotype by Antoine Claudet, published in 1845.

chapters.heading: India and the first school
chapters.body: When Archibald was posted to Madras in 1832, Caroline founded the Female School of Industry for the Daughters of European Soldiers. It taught practical skills to girls who would otherwise have had few prospects, and it set the pattern for everything that followed. She had already learnt that the way to help vulnerable women was to give them training, shelter and a path to honest work.

chapters.heading: Sydney, 1838, and the women on the wharf
chapters.body: The Chisholms landed in Sydney aboard the Emerald Isle in September 1838 with their young sons and settled at Windsor. When her husband returned to military service in 1840, Caroline stayed on and turned her attention to the immigrant ships. Each fresh arrival brought single women who were jobless and homeless, and she began meeting the vessels herself and finding positions for the girls.

chapters.heading: The Female Immigrants' Home
chapters.body: In 1841 Chisholm approached Governor Sir George Gipps, who doubted that women belonged in public affairs, and won the use of part of the old immigration barracks on the strict condition that no government money be spent. The Female Immigrants' Home she opened there sheltered up to ninety-six women at a time and ran the only free employment registry in Sydney. It was funded entirely by public subscription and by her own relentless effort.

images.caption: The Hyde Park convict barracks in Sydney painted about 1820, later used as a female immigration depot.

chapters.heading: Drays into the bush
chapters.body: From 1842 Chisholm refused to let women languish in the crowded town when labour was wanted in the country. She sent circulars to magistrates and clergymen asking about wages and accommodation, then personally escorted parties of immigrants inland, establishing resting stages and registries at about a dozen rural centres. Over roughly seven years she placed some 11,000 people in homes and jobs across New South Wales.

chapters.heading: England and the Family Colonisation Loan Society
chapters.body: The Chisholms returned to England in 1846, where Caroline collected hundreds of immigrants' written statements as evidence and lobbied officials over emigration. In 1849, after years of inaction, she founded the Family Colonisation Loan Society, which lent families their passage money and found them work in the colony, with repayment by easy instalments. Charles Dickens championed her cause in his journal Household Words.

images.caption: An 1852 oil portrait of Caroline Chisholm by Angelo Collen Hayter, held by the State Library of New South Wales.

chapters.heading: The goldfields years
chapters.body: Chisholm sailed back to Australia in 1854, returning to a colony transformed by the gold rushes. She toured the Victorian diggings and pressed for shelter sheds along the rough routes that families walked to the goldfields, with several built by the end of 1855. Ill health later forced her to slow down, though she still lectured on land reform and opened a girls' school.

chapters.heading: Last years and a long afterlife
chapters.body: The Chisholms left Australia for good in 1866 and lived modestly in England on a small pension. Caroline died in London on 25 March 1877 and was buried at Northampton beneath a headstone reading 'The emigrant's friend'. In 1967 the new Commonwealth honoured her on the paper five-dollar note, picturing her work for immigrant families, before that note was retired in 1992.

images.caption: The reverse of the original paper Australian five-dollar note honoured Caroline Chisholm's work with immigrants.

title: Cathy Freeman: The Flame and the 400

chapters.heading: Slade Point and the family line
chapters.body: Cathy Freeman was born on 16 February 1973 at Slade Point, near Mackay, the daughter of Cecelia, a Kuku Yalanji woman born on Palm Island, and Norman Freeman, a Birri Gubba man born at Woorabinda. She grew up with brothers and an older sister, Anne-Marie, who lived with cerebral palsy and died in 1990. Freeman later learned that her paternal great-grandfather, Frank Fisher, had served in the 11th Light Horse Regiment in the First World War.

chapters.heading: A girl who could run
chapters.body: As a child Freeman played netball, basketball and touch football, but it was running that set her apart. Her stepfather Bruce Barber coached her to early regional and national titles, and a sports scholarship took her to Kooralbyn International School, where the Romanian coach Mike Danila set a hard training regime. The talent was obvious well before the medals arrived.

chapters.heading: Auckland, 1990
chapters.body: At sixteen Freeman was picked for Australia's 4x100 metres relay team at the 1990 Commonwealth Games in Auckland and came home with gold. She was the first Aboriginal athlete to win a Commonwealth Games title and one of the youngest medallists in the history of the Games. That same year she was named Young Australian of the Year.

chapters.heading: Victoria, 1994, and the flag
chapters.body: At the 1994 Commonwealth Games in Victoria, Canada, Freeman won both the 200 and 400 metres. After her wins she ran her victory laps draped in both the Aboriginal and Australian flags, and Australia's chef de mission Arthur Tunstall publicly rebuked her for it. The dispute reached federal parliament, and most of the country sided with the runner rather than the official. Freeman simply carried both flags again after her next win.

images.caption: Cathy Freeman photographed during a visit to Papua New Guinea in 2008.

chapters.heading: Atlanta and the world titles
chapters.body: At the 1996 Atlanta Olympics Freeman ran 48.63 seconds in the 400 metres, an Australian record, and finished second to France's Marie-Jose Perec. She answered by winning the world title at Athens in 1997, then again at Seville in 1999 after a year lost to injury. Through 1999 she did not lose a single 400 metres race.

chapters.heading: The cauldron and the gold
chapters.body: Chosen to light the flame at the opening of the Sydney Games on 15 September 2000, Freeman climbed to the centre of a pool of water in a white fireproof suit and set the cauldron alight as it rose around her. On 25 September she won the 400 metres final in 49.11 seconds before roughly 112,000 people, then took her lap of honour carrying the Aboriginal and Australian flags tied together. It remains one of the most watched moments in Australian sport.

images.caption: Cathy Freeman runs the 400 metres final at the Sydney Olympics on 25 September 2000.

chapters.heading: The shoes and the record of it
chapters.body: The green and silver hooded suit and the shoes she wore that night became objects of memory in themselves, the spikes now held in the Olympic Museum at Lausanne. Freeman ran once more for Australia in the winning 4x400 metres relay team at the 2002 Manchester Commonwealth Games, then retired in 2003. She had been named Australian of the Year in 1998, before her greatest race was even run.

images.caption: The spikes Cathy Freeman wore for her 2000 Olympic 400 metres gold, displayed at the Olympic Museum in Lausanne.

chapters.heading: After the track
chapters.body: In 2007 Freeman founded the Cathy Freeman Foundation, later renamed, to support education in remote Indigenous communities. She was appointed a Companion of the Order of Australia in 2026 and was an inaugural inductee to the Stadium Australia Hall of Fame in 2025. In 2023 she joined other Australians of the Year in publicly backing the Voice referendum, still using her name for the cause she ran for in Victoria all those years before.

title: The Boy from Bowral: 99.94

chapters.heading: A boy from Cootamundra and Bowral
chapters.body: Donald George Bradman was born on 27 August 1908 at Cootamundra in the New South Wales Riverina, the youngest of five children of George and Emily Bradman. Around 1910 the family moved to Bowral in the Southern Highlands, the town that would claim him as its own. The famous account of his childhood has him hitting a golf ball against the curved brick stand of a backyard water tank with a single cricket stump, training the reflexes and timing that later seemed almost superhuman.

images.caption: Don Bradman photographed around 1930, near the start of his Test career.

chapters.heading: Sydney takes notice
chapters.body: Bradman came down from the bush to play grade cricket for the St George club in Sydney from the 1926-27 season, a long train trip each way that he made without complaint. His first-class debut came in December 1927 for New South Wales against South Australia at the Adelaide Oval, where he scored 118 after being drafted in late. The Sydney Cricket Ground soon became his stage, and he was clearly a young player headed for the Test side.

chapters.heading: 452 at the SCG
chapters.body: In January 1930, still only twenty-one, Bradman made an unbeaten 452 for New South Wales against Queensland at the Sydney Cricket Ground. It was the highest score in first-class cricket anywhere in the world, beating Bill Ponsford's 437, and it stood as the record until Hanif Mohammad passed it in 1959. The innings removed any lingering doubt about his place on the tour of England that year, where he plundered 974 runs in the Test series, including 334 in a single match at Headingley.

images.caption: Bradman practising his drive in 1936, the shot that defined his run-scoring.

chapters.heading: Bodyline
chapters.body: Bradman's run-scoring was so relentless that England devised a plan specifically to stop him. During the 1932-33 Ashes series, captain Douglas Jardine set his fast bowlers to aim short and at the body with a packed leg-side field, a tactic that became known as Bodyline. It bruised batsmen and strained relations between the two countries. Bradman's average fell to a merely excellent 56.57, and England won the series, but the controversy reshaped the laws of the game.

chapters.heading: Marriage and the move to Adelaide
chapters.body: On 30 April 1932 Bradman married Jessie Menzies at St Paul's Church in Burwood, a partnership that lasted until her death in 1997. In 1934 he accepted a stockbroking position arranged through South Australian cricket interests and moved to Adelaide to captain that state. The shift took the most famous sportsman in the country away from Sydney, though his reputation had been built on the grounds of New South Wales.

chapters.heading: The Invincibles
chapters.body: After the Second World War, in which he served briefly in the air force and the army before being discharged in 1941 for ill health, Bradman returned to lead Australia one final time. His 1948 team toured England without losing a single match and won the Ashes four-nil, earning the name the Invincibles. For Bradman it was the most satisfying chapter of his playing life, free of the divisions that had dogged the 1930s.

images.caption: Don Bradman at the Adelaide Oval in 1946, two years before the Invincibles tour.

chapters.body: Bradman's last Test innings came at The Oval in August 1948. He needed just four runs to retire with a career average of exactly 100. Facing the leg-spinner Eric Hollies, he was bowled second ball for nought, leaving his average at 99.94. The figure is now shorthand for the man himself, beyond the reach of any batsman before or since.

chapters.heading: Knight and elder statesman
chapters.body: Bradman was knighted in 1949, the only Australian cricketer so honoured for services to the game. He spent decades as an administrator and selector, guiding Australian cricket through difficult periods including the World Series upheaval of the late 1970s. He died at Kensington Park in Adelaide on 25 February 2001, aged ninety-two, and his ashes were later scattered at Bradman Oval in Bowral. The Sydney Cricket Ground remembers his beginnings with the Bradman Stand.

images.caption: Don Bradman with his branded bat, photographed by Sam Hood around 1932.

title: Toby Barton: A Nation for a Continent

chapters.heading: A Glebe boy with a head for Latin
chapters.body: Edmund Barton was born at Glebe on 18 January 1849, the youngest child of William Barton, a London-born accountant, and Mary Louisa. He went to Fort Street Model School, then to Sydney Grammar, where he was captain and dux in the early 1860s. At the University of Sydney he won prizes in classics and graduated with first-class honours in 1868, taking his Master of Arts in 1870. His schoolmates' nickname for him, Toby, followed him all his life.

images.caption: Edmund Barton photographed around 1889, by then a Queen's Counsel and member of the New South Wales Legislative Council.

chapters.heading: The Bar, the bench of the House, and a famous cricket riot
chapters.body: Barton was admitted to the Bar in December 1871 and slowly built a practice, working the country circuits as a young barrister. In 1879 he umpired a notorious match at the Sydney Cricket Ground between New South Wales and the visiting English side, where a disputed decision sparked a crowd riot. That same year he entered the New South Wales parliament, and in 1883, aged just thirty-four, he became Speaker of the Legislative Assembly, admired for his coolness in the chair.

chapters.heading: Taking up the Federation cause
chapters.body: When Henry Parkes called for union at Tenterfield in 1889, Barton wrote to congratulate him and threw himself behind the movement. He served twice as Attorney-General of New South Wales and pressed Federal resolutions through the colonial parliament in the early 1890s. Between 1893 and 1897 he carried the argument to the people, speaking at hundreds of meetings across the colony when the cause looked far from certain.

chapters.heading: Drafting the Constitution
chapters.body: Barton topped the poll for the convention that met from 1897, and his colleagues elected him its leader and chairman of the drafting committees. Working alongside Samuel Griffith, John Downer and Richard O'Connor, he drove the writing of the Constitution through long and exhausting sessions in Adelaide, Sydney and Melbourne. When the first referendum narrowly failed in 1898, he did not give up, and the amended scheme was carried at the second attempt.

images.caption: The drafting committee of the Commonwealth Constitution in 1898, with Edmund Barton seated at centre between John Downer and Richard O'Connor.

chapters.heading: First Prime Minister
chapters.body: On 1 January 1901 Barton was sworn in as Australia's first Prime Minister at the Federation Pavilion in Centennial Park, at the inauguration of the Commonwealth before an enormous crowd. He took the External Affairs portfolio himself and won office in his own right at the first federal election. His government built the machinery of the new nation almost from scratch, and it was fitting that the first Prime Minister of Australia was native-born.

images.caption: Edmund Barton photographed in 1903, around the end of his term as Prime Minister.

chapters.heading: The laws of a young Commonwealth
chapters.body: Barton's ministry passed some of the new nation's defining and most contested early laws. The Immigration Restriction Act of 1901 gave legal shape to the White Australia Policy, and the Pacific Island Labourers Act provided for the deportation of Islander workers from the Queensland canefields. His government also extended the federal franchise to women in 1902, an early and important step that sat awkwardly beside the racial exclusions of the same period.

chapters.heading: A founding justice of the High Court
chapters.body: In September 1903 Barton resigned the prime ministership to become one of the three founding justices of the High Court of Australia, sitting under Chief Justice Samuel Griffith alongside Richard O'Connor. He proved a careful and impartial judge with a quick mind, helping to shape the early reading of the Constitution he had done so much to write. He remained on the court for the rest of his life.

images.caption: The three inaugural justices of the High Court of Australia in 1903, with Barton among them alongside Griffith and O'Connor.

chapters.heading: Death and legacy
chapters.body: Barton died suddenly of heart failure at Medlow Bath in the Blue Mountains on 7 January 1920, aged seventy, and was buried in South Head General Cemetery at Vaucluse. He had been made a knight of the Order of St Michael and St George in 1902. He is remembered as the patient, persuasive leader who turned six colonies into one country, and a plaque near Arundel Street in Glebe marks the inner-city suburb where his life began.

title: Elizabeth Macquarie

chapters.heading: A Highland childhood
chapters.body: Elizabeth Henrietta Campbell was born on 13 June 1778 at Kilcalmonell in Argyll, the youngest daughter of John Campbell of Airds. She grew up among the lochs and farms of the Scottish west coast, a world of practical estate management that would later shape her interest in land and design. She was a distant cousin of Lachlan Macquarie and did not marry until she was nearly thirty.

chapters.heading: Marriage and a long voyage
chapters.body: Elizabeth married Colonel Lachlan Macquarie on 3 November 1807, when he was forty-six and she was twenty-nine. Soon after, he was appointed Governor of New South Wales. The couple left Britain and sailed for months to the other side of the world, arriving in Sydney on 31 December 1809. Elizabeth stepped ashore into a rough convict town that her husband intended to remake.

chapters.heading: A governor's wife with an eye for design
chapters.body: Elizabeth was no mere ornament at Government House. She took a close interest in gardening and farming, and is credited with helping pioneer hay-making in the colony alongside Elizabeth Macarthur. She had brought from Britain a library of books on architecture, and her husband and the architect Francis Greenway leaned on them as they planned Sydney's public buildings. Government House itself was extended and dressed up during these years, as seen in Joseph Lycett's 1819 view of the residence above the cove.

images.caption: Joseph Lycett's 1819 watercolour of Government House, Sydney, seen from the cove.

chapters.heading: The road and the chair
chapters.body: Elizabeth planned the road that ran around the inside of the Government Domain out to the rocky point at its end, and the point and the road both took her name. In 1810 convicts cut a bench into the exposed sandstone at the point, a seat with sweeping views down the harbour toward the open sea. It became known as Mrs Macquarie's Chair, and a tradition grew that she would sit there to watch for ships arriving from home.

images.caption: The carved sandstone seat known as Mrs Macquarie's Chair, photographed about 1870 to 1875.

chapters.heading: Reform and travel
chapters.body: Elizabeth shared her husband's belief that convicts could be reformed rather than simply punished, and she took a particular interest in the welfare of women convicts and of Aboriginal people. She also travelled widely for a woman of her day, crossing to Van Diemen's Land in 1811, over the Blue Mountains in 1815, and up to the Hunter River in 1818. These journeys gave her a wide view of the young colony beyond the town.

chapters.heading: Private grief
chapters.body: Behind the public work lay deep loss. Elizabeth's first child, Jane Jarvis Macquarie, was born in 1808 and died at only three months of age. A son, Lachlan, was born in Sydney in 1814 and survived to adulthood, the great joy of his parents' later years. He would die young and without children in 1845, so the family line came to an end.

chapters.heading: Return to Scotland and death
chapters.body: When Lachlan Macquarie's term as governor ended, the family sailed for Britain in February 1822 and reached the Macquarie estate of Jarvisfield on the Isle of Mull in late 1823. Lachlan died soon after, in 1824, leaving Elizabeth a widow on the family lands. She lived on quietly in the Highlands and died at Gruline House on Mull on 11 March 1835. A statue at Campbelltown is one of the few public likenesses of her in New South Wales.

images.caption: A statue of Elizabeth Macquarie in Mawson Park, Campbelltown, New South Wales.

title: The Forger Who Drew a City

chapters.heading: A Bristol Building Family
chapters.body: Greenway was born in 1777 at Mangotsfield, just outside Bristol, into a family that had worked as stonemasons, builders and architects across the west country for generations. He trained in that world and set up his own practice in Bristol. The business failed, and the failure set the stage for the crime that would change his life.

chapters.heading: Forgery and a Death Sentence
chapters.body: In March 1812 Greenway was convicted of forging a document tied to a building contract. The court sentenced him to death, the standard penalty for forgery in that era. The sentence was commuted to fourteen years of transportation, which is how a respectable English architect came to be loaded onto a convict ship bound for the far side of the world.

chapters.heading: Arrival in the Colony
chapters.body: He reached Sydney on 17 February 1814 aboard the transport General Hewitt, and his wife Mary and their three children followed later that year. Greenway wasted no time, opening a private office in George Street even while still a convict. In 1815 Macquarie engaged him to assess the troubled Rum Hospital, and his blunt verdict won him both attention and his first enemies.

chapters.heading: Macquarie's Architect
chapters.body: In March 1816 Greenway was appointed civil architect and assistant engineer at three shillings a day, with quarters, a horse and forage. The partnership with Macquarie was the making of him. The governor wanted a colony that looked like a permanent society rather than a gaol, and Greenway gave him the buildings to prove it.

images.caption: A pencil portrait of Francis Greenway held by the State Library of New South Wales.

chapters.heading: The Lighthouse and a Pardon
chapters.body: His first major commission was the Macquarie Tower lighthouse on South Head, built of local sandstone and completed in 1818. Macquarie was so pleased that he granted Greenway a conditional emancipation. When the Hyde Park Barracks opened in 1819, the governor went further and made the pardon absolute, so the work itself earned back the architect's liberty.

chapters.heading: Barracks, Church and Obelisk
chapters.body: The Hyde Park Barracks gave Sydney its first proper home for male convicts and remains one of the finest Georgian buildings in the country. St James' Church in King Street, begun in 1819, gave the town a spire and for decades stood as its tallest structure. The 1818 obelisk in Macquarie Place became the zero point from which every public road in New South Wales was measured.

images.caption: The Hyde Park Barracks, designed by Greenway to house male convicts and completed in 1819.

chapters.heading: Bigge's Austerity and the Fall
chapters.body: In 1819 Commissioner John Bigge arrived to investigate Macquarie's costly public works, and the era of grand building came to an end. Greenway, prickly and convinced he was underpaid, quarrelled with everyone and finally presented the government with a bill for eleven thousand pounds in fees, despite being a salaried employee. He was dismissed on 15 November 1822 and refused for a time to leave his official residence.

images.caption: St James' Church in King Street, Greenway's Georgian town church begun in 1819.

chapters.heading: Obscurity and an Unlikely Honour
chapters.body: Greenway never recovered his standing. He took up a land grant on the Hunter River, did occasional private work, and died there in 1837, buried in an unmarked grave near East Maitland. In 1966 Australia put his portrait on the ten dollar note, where it stayed until 1993, making the convicted forger the unlikeliest figure ever to grace a nation's money.

images.caption: The 1818 Macquarie Place obelisk, the point from which colonial road distances were measured.

title: The People's Poet, and the City That Broke Him

chapters.heading: A goldfields childhood
chapters.body: Henry Lawson was born on 17 June 1867 in a tent on the Grenfell diggings in central New South Wales, the eldest surviving child of Niels Hertzberg Larsen, a Norwegian sailor turned miner who anglicised his name to Peter Lawson, and Louisa Albury. The family chased gold and selections around Mudgee through his early years. Around the age of nine an illness left him hard of hearing, and by fourteen the deafness was near total and incurable. It set him apart from other children and turned him inward toward watching and listening in his own way.

images.caption: A monument at Grenfell marks the goldfields site where Henry Lawson was born in 1867.

chapters.heading: Sydney and his mother's circle
chapters.body: In 1883 the boy joined his mother in Sydney, where she had moved after the marriage broke down. He was apprenticed as a coach painter and studied at night for a matriculation he never passed. Louisa Lawson was becoming a force of her own as a publisher and suffragist, and her radical friends drew her son toward republican politics. Their talk and their newspapers gave him both his early subjects and his first outlets in print.

chapters.heading: The Bulletin makes a poet
chapters.body: On 1 October 1887 the Sydney Bulletin printed 'A Song of the Republic', Lawson's first published poem, and a young deaf coach painter became a writer with a public. His first short story, 'His Father's Mate', followed in the Bulletin in December 1888. Poems such as 'Faces in the Street' showed he could write the city's poverty as sharply as anyone, and by the early 1890s he was one of the most talked about names in Australian letters.

images.caption: John Longstaff painted this portrait of Henry Lawson in 1900, now held by the Art Gallery of New South Wales.

chapters.heading: The road to Bourke
chapters.body: In September 1892 the Bulletin editor J.F. Archibald gave Lawson five pounds and a rail ticket to Bourke and sent him into the drought stricken west. He tramped the back country, carried his swag, and saw a hardness that shocked him. He told his aunt the reader could have no idea of the horrors of the country out there. The trip gave him the material for his greatest stories, including 'The Union Buries Its Dead' and 'The Bush Undertaker'.

chapters.heading: The debate with Banjo Paterson
chapters.body: Through 1892 and 1893 Lawson and Banjo Paterson carried on a public argument in verse in the Bulletin over what the bush really was. Lawson opened with 'Up the Country', mocking the romantic picture of the outback, and Paterson answered with 'In Defence of the Bush'. Lawson wrote from the hard edge of drought and loneliness, Paterson from the saddle and the open plain. The exchange came to stand for two enduring ways of seeing the Australian country.

chapters.heading: While the Billy Boils
chapters.body: In 1896 Angus and Robertson published While the Billy Boils, the prose collection that fixed Lawson's place in Australian literature. In the same year his verse appeared as In the Days When the World Was Wide. On 15 April 1896 he married Bertha Bredt after a fast and intense courtship, and they had a son, Joseph, and a daughter, Bertha. The couple tried New Zealand, where he taught at a remote Maori settlement, before returning to Sydney.

images.caption: A photograph of Henry Lawson taken about 1902, in his middle years.

chapters.heading: London, drink and Starvinghurst
chapters.body: The family sailed for London in 1900, where publishers brought out Joe Wilson and His Mates and Children of the Bush, but money troubles and Bertha's breakdown forced them home by 1902. The marriage collapsed into a judicial separation in 1903 on grounds of habitual drunkenness. Between 1905 and 1910 Lawson did several short stretches in Darlinghurst Gaol, mostly for drunkenness and unpaid maintenance for his children. He called it Starvinghurst, and his 1908 poem 'One Hundred and Three', his prison number, turned the experience into a bitter cry against the place.

chapters.heading: A state funeral and a statue
chapters.body: In his last years Lawson was a frail and haunted figure on the Sydney streets, kept going by the devoted care of Mrs Isabel Byers, who looked after him for close to twenty years. A Commonwealth Literary Fund pension of a pound a week came in 1920. He died of a cerebral haemorrhage at Abbotsford on 2 September 1922, and on 4 September was given the first New South Wales state funeral granted to a citizen for distinction alone. George Lambert's bronze of him, flanked by a swagman and a dog, was unveiled in the Domain in 1931.

images.caption: George Lambert's memorial to Henry Lawson, unveiled in the Domain in 1931, shows the poet with a swagman and a dog.

title: The Ivory Turner Who Made a Nation

chapters.heading: A Warwickshire boyhood
chapters.body: Henry Parkes was born on 27 May 1815 near Coventry in Warwickshire, the youngest of seven children of Thomas Parkes, a struggling tenant farmer, and his wife Martha. The family knew real hardship, and as a boy Henry worked at rope making and in a brickyard before being apprenticed to an ivory and bone turner in Birmingham. He grew up with little formal schooling, but a hunger for reading and self improvement that never left him.

images.caption: The building in Canley near Coventry identified as the birthplace of Sir Henry Parkes.

chapters.heading: Steerage to Sydney
chapters.body: In July 1836 Parkes married Clarinda Varney at Edgbaston, and when his own turning business failed the couple decided to start again in the colonies. They sailed from Gravesend aboard the Strathfieldsaye and reached Sydney on 25 July 1839, their first child born at sea only days before. Parkes took whatever work he could find, labouring on a country estate and toiling in a brass foundry, before setting up as an ivory turner and dealer in fancy goods in Hunter Street.

chapters.heading: The Empire and the printed word
chapters.body: Late in 1850 Parkes founded the Empire, a daily newspaper that became a platform for his liberal, anti transportation and democratic views. He poured everything into it, hoping to use the press to lift and direct colonial public life. The paper carried him into the Legislative Council in 1854 and the new Legislative Assembly in 1856, but its mounting debts overwhelmed him, and in 1858 the Empire collapsed and Parkes was forced into insolvency.

images.caption: Tom Roberts painted this portrait of Sir Henry Parkes in 1893.

chapters.heading: Five times premier
chapters.body: Parkes first became premier of New South Wales in May 1872, and returned to the office four more times across two decades, the last from 1889 to 1891. He dominated colonial politics through force of personality and a gift for oratory rather than steady administration. His ministries pushed through reform after reform, and he was knighted as K.C.M.G. in 1877 and raised to G.C.M.G. in 1888.

chapters.heading: Schools for the people
chapters.body: Parkes believed deeply that an educated population was the foundation of a free society. His Public Schools Act of 1866 reorganised colonial education and set requirements for trained teachers, and the Public Instruction Act of 1880 went further, building a system of free, compulsory and largely secular state schooling. This work shaped the public school system of New South Wales that endures today.

chapters.heading: The Tenterfield Oration
chapters.body: On 24 October 1889, speaking at the School of Arts in the northern town of Tenterfield, Parkes called for the Australian colonies to unite under one federal government and parliament. It was an appeal made directly to the public rather than to politicians alone, and he repeated the argument many times over the following months. The Tenterfield Oration is remembered as the spark that set Federation in motion.

images.caption: A plaque at the Tenterfield School of Arts commemorates the oration Parkes delivered there in 1889.

chapters.heading: The people's park
chapters.body: A long standing believer in open space for ordinary people, Parkes drove the creation of Centennial Park out of the old Lachlan Swamps water reserve. The park was dedicated on 26 January 1888 to mark the centenary of the colony, and Parkes called it the people's park. In 1901 it became the setting where the Commonwealth of Australia was formally proclaimed.

chapters.heading: Debts, death and legacy
chapters.body: Parkes was a poor manager of his own money and went bankrupt more than once, leaving very large debts at his death. After Clarinda died in 1888 he married twice more, to Eleanor Dixon and then to Julia Lynch. He died at Annandale in Sydney on 27 April 1896, almost five years before the nation he had championed came into being, and was buried at Faulconbridge in the Blue Mountains beside his first wife.

images.caption: An obelisk at Faulconbridge commemorates Sir Henry Parkes and his parents.

title: The Governor Who Built in Stone

chapters.heading: A Hebridean boyhood
chapters.body: Macquarie was born on 31 January 1762 on the small island of Ulva, off the Isle of Mull in the Inner Hebrides of Scotland. His family were poor Highland tenant farmers, and as a boy he was sent to be schooled in Edinburgh. He came from a world far removed from the gentry he would one day rule among, and the climb out of it shaped a man who never quite forgot what a second chance was worth.

chapters.heading: A soldier across three continents
chapters.body: Macquarie volunteered for the British Army in 1776 and served in North America during the War of Independence, then in Jamaica and at length in India. He saw action at the siege of Cochin and in the campaigns against Tipu Sultan, and in 1801 he took part in the Egyptian expedition that drove the French from Alexandria. Decades of soldiering in distant garrisons gave him both a fortune and the habit of command. His first wife, Jane Jarvis, died of consumption in 1796, and in 1807 he married his Scottish kinswoman Elizabeth Campbell.

chapters.heading: Restoring order after the Rum Rebellion
chapters.body: Macquarie reached Sydney late in December 1809 and was sworn in on New Year's Day 1810. He had been sent to mend a colony thrown into chaos by the overthrow of Governor Bligh, and he moved at once to cancel the grants and appointments the rebel officers had made. With his own regiment behind him he broke the grip of the New South Wales Corps and set about governing a settlement that had been treated, until then, as little more than a holding pen.

images.caption: A portrait of Lachlan Macquarie painted in 1874 by Arthur Levett Jackson.

chapters.heading: Building a town worth the name
chapters.body: Macquarie was determined that Sydney should look and work like a real town rather than a camp. He laid out streets, set aside Hyde Park as public ground, and founded a string of settlements including Windsor, Richmond, Liverpool and Bathurst. In the emancipated architect Francis Greenway he found the talent to match his ambition, and together they raised the public buildings along Macquarie Street, among them the Hyde Park Barracks built between 1817 and 1819 to house convict men.

images.caption: The Hyde Park Barracks on Macquarie Street, designed by convict architect Francis Greenway under Macquarie.

chapters.heading: A light at the harbour mouth
chapters.body: Among Macquarie's works was the first lighthouse in Australia, built on South Head to guide ships into Port Jackson. Greenway designed it, the foundation stone was laid in July 1816, and the governor was so pleased with the result that he granted the architect a full pardon at the site. The tower stood for decades until a sturdier replacement rose beside it, and the two were photographed side by side before the original came down.

chapters.heading: The emancipist cause
chapters.body: Macquarie believed that a convict who had done his time deserved to rejoin society as a free citizen. He appointed former convicts as magistrates and to other offices, and he invited emancipists to dine at Government House. The free settlers and officers who styled themselves the exclusives regarded this as an outrage, and their resentment, carried in letters home, would help bring the governor down.

chapters.heading: Appin, 1816
chapters.body: For all his talk of improvement, Macquarie answered frontier resistance with force. In April 1816 he ordered a military expedition against the Dharawal and Gundungurra peoples of the Cumberland Plain after clashes between settlers and Aboriginal people. On 17 April, soldiers under Captain James Wallis drove a group over a gorge near Appin and killed at least fourteen men, women and children. The bodies of two named men were afterwards hung in trees as a warning, a brutality that sits beside the buildings and the benevolence.

chapters.heading: The Bigge inquiry and a bitter homecoming
chapters.body: Macquarie's spending and his emancipist policy drew sharp criticism in London, and in 1819 Commissioner John Thomas Bigge arrived to investigate. Bigge favoured a harsh penal colony serving free settlers, and his reports undercut almost everything Macquarie valued. Hurt and exhausted, the governor sailed for Britain in 1822, fought for the pension and the vindication he felt he was owed, and died in London on 1 July 1824.

chapters.heading: Father of Australia, and the weighing of it
chapters.body: Macquarie's body was carried home to Mull and buried on his estate, Jarvisfield. His widow Elizabeth had the words Father of Australia inscribed on the tomb, a claim made after his death rather than granted in his lifetime. His name is now stamped across the country, on a university, a river, a street and a harbour. The reckoning of his legacy runs through both the city he built in stone and the killing he ordered at Appin.

images.caption: The monument to Major-General Lachlan Macquarie in Hyde Park, Sydney.

title: The Man Who Gave Flight Away

chapters.heading: A boy who sailed late to Sydney
chapters.body: Lawrence Hargrave was born at Greenwich, near London, on 29 January 1850, the son of a lawyer who soon emigrated to New South Wales. Lawrence stayed behind to finish his schooling and only reached Sydney on 5 November 1865, aboard the La Hogue, joining the family at fifteen. He failed his matriculation but found his footing in the engineering workshops of the Australasian Steam Navigation Company, where he learned to work metal with his own hands.

images.caption: Lawrence Hargrave photographed around 1890.

chapters.heading: Reefs, rivers and the wreck of the Maria
chapters.body: Before he ever thought of flight, Hargrave went exploring. He joined a prospecting voyage to New Guinea in 1872 on the Maria, which struck a reef on the Queensland coast with heavy loss of life. He survived, and over the following years sailed again as ship's engineer, working with the naturalist Luigi D'Albertis to chart the Fly River and its tributaries. He filled notebooks with careful drawings of the country and its people.

chapters.heading: An observer of the heavens
chapters.body: Back in Sydney, Hargrave took a post as an astronomical observer at Sydney Observatory from 1879, working under the government astronomer Henry Chamberlain Russell. He observed the transit of Mercury in 1881 and the strange sunsets that followed the eruption of Krakatoa. The patient, methodical habits of the observatory stayed with him for the rest of his working life.

chapters.heading: The gentleman inventor turns to flight
chapters.body: An inheritance freed Hargrave from paid work and let him become, in his own way, a full-time inventor. From 1884 he laid his ideas before the Royal Society of New South Wales, beginning with a paper on the trochoidal plane. In 1889 he built a compressed-air engine driven by three rotating cylinders, an early rotary engine whose configuration would power aircraft for the next quarter century. He tested flapping-wing and screw-driven models at Rushcutters Bay, learning as much from failure as from success.

images.caption: One of Hargrave's box kites aloft, as illustrated in Popular Science Monthly in 1897.

chapters.heading: Sixteen feet above Stanwell Park
chapters.body: Hargrave found that cellular, or box, kites gave far more lift and stability than flat surfaces. On 12 November 1894, at Stanwell Park beach below his home, he linked four box kites in a train and let them carry him about sixteen feet into the air, with the kites tethered by piano wire and his assistant James Swain on hand. It was a small, careful flight, and it proved that a man could be lifted by a stable heavier-than-air machine.

images.caption: Hargrave and James Swain assemble box kites for the lift experiment at Stanwell Park in 1894.

chapters.heading: Giving the secret away
chapters.body: Hargrave refused to patent his inventions, believing that secrecy held back the whole field of flight. He published everything in the journals of the Royal Society, and his box-kite layout was taken up by European pioneers whose first aeroplanes were essentially powered box kites. The illustrated magazines of Britain and America carried his work, yet the men who flew with his ideas were rarely the ones who named him.

images.caption: A McClure's Magazine illustration from 1896 showing Hargrave lifted sixteen feet by a tandem of his box kites.

chapters.heading: Rejection at home, a refuge in Munich
chapters.body: From the early 1900s Hargrave lived above the harbour at Woollahra Point and tried to find a permanent home for his collection. He offered his models to museums in Australia, Britain, the United States and France, and was turned down again and again, often because institutions would not display them on his terms. In 1909 the Bavarian government agreed to take them, and 176 of his models went to the Deutsches Museum in Munich. He felt the rejection at home keenly.

chapters.heading: A son lost, and a face on the note
chapters.body: Hargrave's only son, Geoffrey, was killed in action at Gallipoli in May 1915. Already worn down and grieving, Lawrence underwent surgery for appendicitis, developed peritonitis and died in Sydney on 6 July 1915, aged sixty-five. He was buried at Waverley Cemetery above the sea. From 1966 to 1994 his dark, bearded features and his flying machines appeared on the reverse of the Australian twenty dollar note, a belated salute to the man who gave flight away.

title: Molly Haydock, the Merchant of George Street

chapters.heading: A Lancashire girl, orphaned and unruly
chapters.body: Mary Haydock was born at Bury in Lancashire on 12 May 1777, and lost both parents while still a child. She was raised by her grandmother, with little to hold a spirited girl in check. The escapade that changed her life was, by most accounts, a high-spirited piece of mischief rather than hardened crime.

chapters.heading: Tried as James Burrow
chapters.body: In 1790 she was caught with a stolen horse, dressed as a boy and giving the name James Burrow. The court at Stafford uncovered her true identity at trial and sentenced the thirteen year old to seven years transportation. It was a brutal punishment for a child, and it sent her to the far side of the world.

images.caption: A letter Mary wrote to her aunt Penelope Hope from Sydney on 8 October 1792, soon after she arrived.

chapters.heading: Arrival and a fortunate marriage
chapters.body: She reached Sydney on the Royal Admiral in October 1792 and was assigned as a nursemaid to Major Francis Grose. On 7 September 1794 she married Thomas Reibey, an Irishman in the service of the East India Company whom she had met during the voyage out. The match would prove the making of her commercial life.

chapters.heading: Building the business with Thomas
chapters.body: The Reibeys worked the Hawkesbury, farming the river flats and carrying grain and cargo down to Sydney in their own boats. They built a stone house and trading store near Macquarie Place, named Entally House after a suburb of Calcutta. The trade grew steadily into coastal shipping, importing and property.

chapters.heading: Widowed, and taking the reins
chapters.body: Thomas Reibey died on 5 April 1811 after a long illness, leaving Mary with seven children. She had managed the affairs during his frequent absences and now took full control. In 1812 she opened a new warehouse in George Street and pressed on, buying ships including the John Palmer and, in 1817, the brig Governor Macquarie.

images.caption: The George Street houses of prosperous merchants Isaac Nichols, Mary Reibey and Sarah Wills, painted in 1829.

chapters.heading: The merchant queen of the colony
chapters.body: By 1820 Mary Reibey held around a thousand acres of land, much of it by grant, and was worth roughly twenty thousand pounds. Her interests reached across George Street, Macquarie Place and the Rocks, with farms on the Hawkesbury and ships trading beyond the harbour. That year she also sailed to England with two of her daughters, returning the following year. She was not to be trifled with, and in 1817 was even convicted of assaulting a debtor she felt had wronged her.

chapters.heading: Respectability, charity and later years
chapters.body: In the emancipist society of Macquarie's Sydney, Mary rose to a position of standing and affluence, and was a favourite of the governor himself. She gave to churches and to education, becoming a governor of the Free Grammar School in 1825. In her later years she eased back from active trade and lived on her rents, building a country retreat and moving toward a quieter life.

chapters.heading: Death and a face on the note
chapters.body: Mary Reibey died at Newtown on 30 May 1855, aged seventy eight, and was first buried at the Old Sandhills cemetery before later reinterment at La Perouse. Her sons carried the Reibey name into the mercantile life of Van Diemen's Land. Today her portrait appears on the Australian twenty dollar note, the convict girl turned merchant looking out at the country she helped build.

images.caption: A watercolour on ivory miniature of Mary Reibey from about 1835, the only known portrait of her.

title: The Woman Who Gave the Bush a Face

chapters.heading: An English Birth and a West Australian Childhood
chapters.body: Cecilia May Gibbs was born on 17 January 1877 at Sydenham, then in Kent, into a family of keen amateur artists. Her father Herbert sailed for South Australia in 1881 and the rest of the family followed, eventually settling in Western Australia by the mid 1880s. The wide, dry bush of the new colony became the landscape of her imagination, and she later said she could draw before she could walk.

chapters.heading: Training in London
chapters.body: Determined to make art her life, Gibbs travelled back to England around the turn of the century to study. She worked at art schools in London, including the Cope and Nichol school and the Chelsea Polytechnic, pushing herself so hard that her health repeatedly broke down. She returned to Western Australia more than once to recover before committing fully to her career.

chapters.heading: Sydney and the First Gumnut Babies
chapters.body: In 1913 Gibbs settled in Sydney, taking a small studio in the city and crossing the harbour by ferry to work. That same year her little bush babies made an early public appearance on a book cover she illustrated. The native plants she had loved as a child in the west now became the people of her drawings.

images.caption: An early May Gibbs postcard shows her gumnut babies playing cricket.

chapters.heading: Snugglepot and Cuddlepie
chapters.body: Her first gumnut book appeared in 1916, and in 1918 came Snugglepot and Cuddlepie, the work that made her famous. The first edition sold more than 17,000 copies, an extraordinary number for a wartime children's book. Combined later with its sequels, it has never gone out of print.

images.caption: A pen and ink illustration of around 1918 by May Gibbs for the Snugglepot and Cuddlepie tales.

chapters.heading: A Marriage and a House on the Harbour
chapters.body: Gibbs married Bertram James Ossoli Kelly, a mining agent, on 17 April 1919. With money from her books and a plot found by her mother on the Neutral Bay foreshore, she had the architect B. J. Waterhouse design a small, charming cottage. It was finished in 1925 and named Nutcote, and she lived and worked there for the rest of her life.

chapters.heading: Bib and Bub and a Working Life
chapters.body: From 1924 Gibbs drew the Bib and Bub comic strip for the newspapers, keeping it going until 1967, alongside other strips and a steady stream of illustration. She is remembered as Australia's first professional woman cartoonist. She was appointed a Member of the British Empire in 1955 for her services to children's literature.

chapters.heading: Quiet Years and a Generous End
chapters.body: Her husband died in 1939 and many close friends in the years that followed, and Gibbs grew increasingly private at Nutcote. She died on 27 November 1969, having had no children of her own. She left the copyrights and royalties from her work to children's charities, including the bodies now known as Northcott and the Cerebral Palsy Alliance.

images.caption: The blue plaque at Nutcote was the first such heritage plaque unveiled in New South Wales.

chapters.heading: Saving Nutcote
