chapters.body: In the late 1980s a developer threatened to demolish Nutcote, and a national Save Nutcote campaign was launched in 1987 to protect it. After a court battle, North Sydney Council resolved to take over the property and later purchased it. The house was restored and reopened as a museum in 1994, showing the home much as it looked in Gibbs' day.

title: Patyegarang: The Voice in the Notebooks

chapters.heading: A young woman of the Eora
chapters.body: Patyegarang was an Eora woman of Sydney Harbour, born around 1775 and aged about fifteen when the events that preserved her name took place. Sources differ on her exact clan, with some describing her as Cadigal of the harbour's southern shore and others suggesting she may have been Cammeraygal from the northern side. She spoke the language now usually called the Sydney language, and she carried detailed knowledge of her Country, its waters and its sky.

images.caption: William Bradley's watercolour shows Sydney Cove soon after the First Fleet arrived in 1788, the world into which Patyegarang's life was thrown.

chapters.heading: Tarra and the observatory
chapters.body: Lieutenant William Dawes of the marines had set up an observatory and a small hut on the rocky western point of Sydney Cove, a place the Eora knew as Tarra and the colonists later named Dawes Point. Dawes had come out with instruments and a recommendation from the Astronomer Royal, and he lived there through his years in the colony. Patyegarang came often to that hut, and it became the setting for one of the most remarkable exchanges of early colonial Sydney.

images.caption: Joseph Lycett's view looks across Sydney Cove from Dawes Point, the headland the Eora called Tarra.

chapters.heading: The lessons
chapters.body: Through 1790 and 1791 Patyegarang taught Dawes her language and learned English from him in return. She gave him vocabulary, grammar and whole snatches of conversation, and she corrected him when he got it wrong. Dawes filled two notebooks with what passed between them, and Patyegarang, whom he often called Patye, is the figure who appears most often in their pages. The result is the most extensive record we have of the original language of Sydney.

chapters.heading: Her own words
chapters.body: The notebooks keep her voice in small, vivid scenes. She tells Dawes to put out the candle, Nyimun candle Mr D, and when he asks why she cannot sleep she answers Kandulin, because of the candle. She offers the tender word putuwa, to warm one's hand by the fire and then to squeeze gently the fingers of another person. The pages also record harder things, the fear and resentment of a people living through smallpox and punitive raids, spoken plainly to a man trusted enough to hear them.

chapters.heading: Departure and silence
chapters.body: In December 1791 Dawes left the colony with the marines, by some accounts wishing he could stay. With his departure the notebooks come to an end, and Patyegarang disappears from the written record entirely. Nothing certain is known of the rest of her life. She remains visible only through the words she gave to a man who wrote them down.

images.caption: Dawes Point, or Tarra, drawn in 1821 after a battery had replaced the observatory where the notebooks were written.

chapters.heading: Rediscovery and revival
chapters.body: Dawes's notebooks sat unread in a London library for almost two centuries. In the early 1970s the Australian librarian Phyllis Mander-Jones recognised their importance at the School of Oriental and African Studies, where they are catalogued as Manuscript 41645. In 1993 the linguist Jakelin Troy drew on them for her study The Sydney Language, and they now underpin the work of reviving that language today.

chapters.heading: Her name today
chapters.body: Patyegarang has become a presence in contemporary Australian culture as well as in linguistics. In 2014 Bangarra Dance Theatre staged Patyegarang, a work built around her life and her exchange with Dawes. Kate Grenville reimagined the pair in her novel The Lieutenant, and the Patji-Dawes Award now honours outstanding teachers of Australian languages in her memory. The young woman who taught a coloniser her tongue is remembered as one of the first teachers of an Aboriginal language to Europeans.

title: Pemulwuy: The Rainbow Warrior of the Bidjigal

chapters.heading: A Bidjigal man of the saltwater country
chapters.body: Pemulwuy was born about 1750 into the Bidjigal clan, whose country lay south of the harbour around the Georges River and the shores of Kamay, later named Botany Bay. He grew up in the world of the Eora, speaking a Dharug dialect and living by fishing, hunting and the seasonal round of his people. A blemish in his left eye and a turned left foot set him apart, and to the Eora these were the marks of a carradhy, a man of knowledge and spirit power.

images.caption: A watercolour of Botany Bay around 1789, the saltwater country of Pemulwuy's Bidjigal clan.

chapters.heading: The spearing of John McIntyre, 1790
chapters.body: In December 1790 Pemulwuy speared John McIntyre, Governor Arthur Phillip's gamekeeper, who was widely loathed and feared by the Eora. McIntyre died of the wound some months later. The spear was barbed with small pieces of red stone, a detail the colonists used to identify Pemulwuy as a man of the woods clans. Phillip sent out a punitive party with orders to bring back heads, but the soldiers found no one and the expedition failed.

images.caption: A view of Sydney Cove in 1792 by the Port Jackson Painter, the young colony at the time of Pemulwuy's early raids.

chapters.heading: Twelve years of resistance
chapters.body: From 1792 Pemulwuy led a sustained campaign against the farms pushing into Eora and Dharug country. He struck at Prospect, Toongabbie, the Georges River, Parramatta, Brickfield Hill and the Hawkesbury, raiding for food and exacting payback for settler violence. David Collins described him as a most active enemy to the settlers, and his attacks turned the frontier into something close to open warfare.

chapters.heading: The man the bullets could not kill
chapters.body: In March 1797 Pemulwuy led a raid on the government farm at Toongabbie. Settlers and soldiers tracked him to the outskirts of Parramatta, where in the fighting he was hit with seven pieces of buckshot in his head and body, and several of his companions were killed. He was carried to the hospital, yet he recovered and escaped with an iron still on his leg. Among the Dharug the story spread that firearms could not kill him, and that belief drew warriors to follow him.

chapters.heading: Outlawed by Governor King
chapters.body: Pemulwuy's raids made him the colony's most wanted man. On 1 May 1801 Governor Philip Gidley King issued an order allowing Aboriginal people near Parramatta, Prospect and the Georges River to be shot on sight. Later that year a proclamation outlawed Pemulwuy by name and offered a reward for his death or capture. King privately admitted that the man was a brave and independent character, even as he called him a terrible pest to the colony.

images.caption: Philip Gidley King, the governor who outlawed Pemulwuy and later sent his head to Joseph Banks.

chapters.heading: Death and a head sent to England
chapters.body: Pemulwuy was shot dead around 1 June 1802, by most accounts at the hand of Henry Hacking. His head was cut off, preserved in spirits, and shipped to the naturalist Sir Joseph Banks in London aboard the Speedy. King wrote to Banks that despite being a pest to the colony, Pemulwuy had been brave and independent. The head was never located in any English collection, and its whereabouts remain unknown.

images.caption: The engraving of Pimbloy in a canoe, from James Grant's 1803 voyage narrative, believed to be the only surviving depiction of Pemulwuy.

chapters.heading: Tedbury and a resistance that did not end
chapters.body: Pemulwuy's death did not end the fight. His son Tedbury, also recorded as Tjedboro, kept up attacks on the colonists for years afterward. He was shot at Parramatta in 1810 after a series of raids and robberies. Through father and son, the Bidjigal carried armed resistance through two decades of the early colony.

chapters.heading: A warrior remembered
chapters.body: Pemulwuy is now honoured as one of the clearest figures of Aboriginal armed resistance in early Sydney, with a suburb in western Sydney named for him and tributes at the National Museum of Australia. In 2010 Prince William said he would help work toward returning Pemulwuy's remains to his people, though the skull has never been found. For his descendants and for many First Nations people, the call to bring him home continues.

images.caption: The Pimbloy engraving has become the enduring face of Pemulwuy's memory.

title: The Native-Born Patriot of Vaucluse

chapters.heading: Born on the Surprize
chapters.body: Wentworth came into the world in August 1790 aboard the Second Fleet ship Surprize, off Norfolk Island. His mother Catherine Crowley was serving a sentence for theft, and his father D'Arcy Wentworth was a surgeon who had faced highway robbery charges in England before sailing for the colony. The boy was sent to school in England, where he failed to win a place at the East India Company College or the military academy. He returned to Sydney in 1810 and was soon granted land and a post by Governor Macquarie.

chapters.heading: Over the Mountains
chapters.body: For a quarter-century the Blue Mountains had hemmed the colony in. In May 1813 Wentworth, then in his early twenties, joined Gregory Blaxland and William Lawson with servants, horses and dogs and pushed along the ridgelines rather than the gullies. They found a viable route to the open country beyond, which led to the founding of Bathurst and the opening of the inland to settlement. Wentworth Falls in the mountains still carries his name.

images.caption: A page from Wentworth's journal of the 1813 expedition across the Blue Mountains.

chapters.heading: A Free Press and a Sharp Tongue
chapters.body: Wentworth studied law at the Middle Temple in London and kept terms at Cambridge, where his poem on Australasia took second place for the chancellor's medal. Back in Sydney, he and Robert Wardell founded The Australian in October 1824, the first newspaper in the colony beyond the reach of the governor. From its pages, and from the bar, he pressed for trial by jury, an elected assembly, and an end to the discrimination against emancipists and their children. His clashes with Governor Ralph Darling were fierce and public.

images.caption: A studio portrait of Wentworth taken in Sydney in the early 1860s.

chapters.heading: Land, Wealth and Vaucluse
chapters.body: Wentworth inherited substantially from his father in 1827 and built up vast pastoral runs across the colony, becoming one of its wealthiest men. In 1827 he bought the Vaucluse estate on the south side of the harbour and turned the cottage there into a fine Gothic Revival mansion. After his marriage in 1829 it became both family home and political headquarters. Long after his death it opened to the public as Australia's first official house museum.

images.caption: Vaucluse House, the harbourside estate Wentworth made his family seat.

chapters.heading: Marriage Across the Class Line
chapters.body: In 1825 Wentworth had represented Sarah Cox, a blacksmith's daughter of convict descent, in a successful breach-of-promise case. On 26 October 1829 he married her at St Philip's Church in Sydney. The match scandalised the exclusives, which suited Wentworth well enough. The couple had ten children, and Sarah managed the Vaucluse household through decades of his political life.

chapters.heading: The Bunyip Aristocracy
chapters.body: Wentworth led the committee that drafted the constitution giving New South Wales responsible self-government, enacted in 1855. By then the radical of the 1820s had become a conservative grandee, and he proposed a colonial peerage to sit in an unelected upper house. Daniel Deniehy ridiculed the idea as a bunyip aristocracy, and the proposal was dropped, though the appointed Legislative Council survived. His attitudes to Aboriginal people were among the harshest of the era, and he opposed allowing them to give evidence in court.

chapters.heading: A University for the Colony
chapters.body: Wentworth believed education was the path by which a poor man could rise, and he argued for a secular university open on merit rather than birth or creed. He carried the legislation that established the University of Sydney, incorporated in 1850 and opened in 1852, the first university in Australia. A statue of him stands in the university's Great Hall.

chapters.heading: A State Funeral Home to Vaucluse
chapters.body: Wentworth retired from colonial politics and sailed for England, returning only briefly around 1861 as President of the Legislative Council. He died at Merley House in Dorset on 20 March 1872, leaving a large fortune. His body was carried home, and on 6 May 1873 the colony held its first state funeral, with great crowds lining the route to Vaucluse, where he was buried in a vault on the estate. The historian Manning Clark later called him Australia's greatest native son.

images.caption: An 1872 oil portrait of Wentworth by James Anderson, held by the State Library of New South Wales.

title: De Groot and the Slashed Ribbon

chapters.heading: A horseman in the official party
body: On 19 March 1932 the crowds gathered to watch Premier Jack Lang open the Sydney Harbour Bridge. Among the mounted figures near the ribbon was Francis de Groot, a Dublin-born antique dealer and a member of the right-wing New Guard, who had slipped into the procession in a borrowed uniform.

Before Lang could cut the ribbon, de Groot spurred his horse forward and slashed it with his sword, declaring the bridge open in the name of the people of New South Wales. He was pulled from his horse and arrested within moments.

images.caption: Francis de Groot in New Guard uniform on his horse, 19 March 1932.
De Groot is pulled from his horse after slashing the ribbon.
Francis de Groot, photographed in 1932.

chapters.heading: The ribbon retied
body: Officials knotted the ribbon back together and Lang cut it as planned, so the ceremony went ahead. De Groot was fined for offensive behaviour and briefly held for a psychiatric assessment, then released.
The stunt became one of the most retold moments in Sydney's history, a piece of theatre that has outlived the politics that drove it.

title: Centennial Park and the Birth of a Nation

chapters.heading: A People's Park
body: Having secured his fifth re-election as Premier of New South Wales in 1887, Sir Henry Parkes wished for a grand proposal to mark the 1888 Centenary of the foundation of the colony. He had been a long time advocate of the need for open space for recreation, and seized upon the idea of creating a park to fulfil two needs: a people’s park and a grand setting for a State House to celebrate the Centenary.

Governor Lord Carrington opened it on 26 January 1888 marking exactly 100 years since European settlement with a tree-planting ceremony and an emphatic speech to 'tens of thousands' of people, decrying the "great obligation that rests upon you as free people to see that no power, no combination, invades your right in the enjoyment of this great boon". The site had been a chain of swampy ponds that once fed Busby's Bore, the tunnel that carried Sydney's water supply from the 1830s, so the new parkland turned a working water reserve into open ground for the whole city.

images.caption: The Opening of Centennial Park

chapters.heading: The Birth of a Nation
body: Thirteen years later the park took on a far larger role. On 1 January 1901, 60,000 people gathered in Centennial Park to witness the proclamation of the Federal Constitution, uniting six formerly independent colonies as one Commonwealth of Australia. A reception at the Domain for Australia’s first Governor-General, Lord Hopetoun, was followed by a military and official procession through the city to Centennial Park. The procession entered Centennial Park through Paddington Gates, elaborately decorated for the occasion, ending at Federation Valley, where a domed pavilion had been specially constructed. As part of the ceremony, Queen Victoria’s official proclamation was read by Lord Hopetoun, and Federal ministers were then sworn in after a twenty-one gun salute. A granite stone laid in 1901 marks the spot. The present Federation Pavilion, a domed rotunda designed by Alec Tzannes, was built over that stone in 1988 for the bicentenary, and it still draws visitors to the exact ground where the ceremony took place.

images.caption: The documents are signed and swearing in ceremony of the first Australian Government, Centennial Park
Crowds gathering for the ceremony of the inauguration of the Federation of Australia in Centennial Park
The Paddington Gates were decorated for the occasion
A military salute to the Duke of York

title: Warrane becomes Sydney Cove

chapters.heading: Warrane
body: Known by the Gadigal people as Warrane, for thousands of years this cove served as pristine fishing grounds. Men speared fish from the rocks along the shore while women fished with lines from their nowies, the bark canoes they balanced over deep water.

images.caption: Aboriginal Australians spearing fish and diving for shellfish (Joseph Lycett, 1817)

chapters.heading: Sydney Cove
body: However, the arrival of the First Fleet shattered this reality. Initially, Governor Arthur Phillip meant to settle further south at Botany Bay, but he quickly discovered it was not suitable. The bay lay open to the weather, the anchorage was poor, and there was no dependable fresh water. So he turned north into Port Jackson and found this cove, where the water ran deep right up to the shore and a small stream came down to meet it. On 26 January 1788 he brought the fleet in and raised the flag. He named the place Sydney Cove after Thomas Townshend, Viscount Sydney, the home secretary who had authorised the venture.

images.caption: The First Fleet in Sydney Cove (John Allcot, 1937)

chapters.heading: A Lost Shoreline
body: British settlement had begun, the Indigenous people were driven out and Warrane was reshaped into Sydney Cove. Almost nothing of that first shoreline can be seen today. The stream that drew Phillip was dammed and, during the drought of 1790, tanks were cut into the sandstone to hold water. That work gave the watercourse the name it still carries, the Tank Stream, and it now runs hidden in a drain beneath the city. The little beach where the boats are thought to have grounded lies buried under reclaimed land near Circular Quay.

images.caption: Sydney as a little village (George Evans, 1802)

title: The Garden Palace Fire

chapters.heading: The Pride of Sydney
body: Designed by James Barnet for the Sydney International Exhibition of 1879 (the first world's fair held in the southern hemisphere), the majestic Garden Palace rose over the city in just eight months. Sitting on the site of today's Conservatorium of Music, the Palace boasted a 244m long exhibition hall, topped with great dome that locals liked to compare with St Paul's in London, soaring 64m above the harbour. Visitors to the Sydney exhibition totalled 1,117,563 between September 1879 and April 1880, a remarkable achievement in a colony with 650,000 residents. After the exhibition closed on 20 April 1880, the Palace was used as an auditorium and gallery and to house the first mining and technological museum. It also provided office space for a number of government departments, and many of the artefacts from the exhibition remained stored inside, making its fiery destruction all the more tragic.

images.caption: The Garden Palace
The Garden Palace overlooking Sydney Harbour
A panoramic view of the harbour from the top of the Palace
The Sydney Exhibition - note the statue of Queen Victoria that lived under the dome
The Sydney Exhibition
The Sydney Exhibition
Post-Exhibition: the grand exhibition hall

chapters.heading: In A Matter of Minutes
body: At 5:40am on 22 September 1882 the largely timber building caught fire and was gone in just 40 minutes, with reports of blackened iron pieces landing as far away as Rushcutters Bay. The extent of the loss was devastating. Records, including those of land occupations, the 1881 Census details, and railway surveys, all went up in flames. So too did 300 uninsured canvasses from the Art Society's annual exhibition, the grand organ and the foundation collection of the Technological and Mining Museum (now the Powerhouse Museum). Most heartbreaking of all, the entire set of the Aboriginal artefacts compiled by the Australian Museum for the exhibition were destroyed, a cultural loss that could never be recovered.

images.caption: The burning of the Garden Palace as seen from the North Shore (JC Hoyte, 1882)
After the fire
After the fire
After the fire

chapters.heading: A Devestating Loss
body: Almost nothing of the Palace survives on the ground today beyond the garden's old gates. The loss has stayed with the city, and in 2016 Wiradjuri/Kamilaroi artist Jonathan Jones traced the building's vanished 20,000 square metre footprint with thousands of white shields in his installation work barrangal dyara (meaning skin and bones in the local Gadigal language), echoing the masses of rubble that was all that remained.

images.caption: The garden's old gates are all that remain
barrangal dyara (skin and bones) by Jonathan Jones (2016)

title: The 1900 Bubonic Plague

chapters.heading: The Response
body: The colonial and city governments instituted a three-pronged approach to controlling the disease in Australia. The first involved transporting infected individuals and anyone they may have had contact with to the quarantine station at North Head. In the first nine months of 1900, 1,759 people were quarantined. Of these only 263 were confirmed cases. The second was intensive cleaning: the newly formed Plague Department went street by street, disinfecting houses and burning waste, and, in some cases, demolished entire sections of the inner city and dock area. The third was a rat extermination program. In March the city council began organising teams to exterminate the rat population. The government paid two pence per rat delivered to an incinerator on Bathurst Street. Eventually, more than 108,000 rats were killed by government employees.

images.caption: City of Sydney Ratcatchers, 1900
Official Plague Proclamation
Plague Instructions for Households

chapters.heading: The Legacy
body: Those cleansing operations were photographed street by street, which left one of the most complete visual records of any Australian neighbourhood at the turn of the century. The resumptions also handed much of The Rocks to the state, and that public ownership is a large part of why the old quarter survived later waves of redevelopment.

images.caption: Photographs of Cleansing Operations
Photographs of Cleansing Operations
Photographs of Cleansing Operations

title: Bondi: the birth of surf lifesaving

chapters.heading: The People's Beaches
body:
Throughout the 1800s, conservative attitudes towards clothing in public areas meant that
it was illegal to swim at Sydney beaches during the day. People could swim only in the early morning and late evening, and men and women could never bathe together. As Australian cities grew and became more crowded and polluted, doctors suggested that sea air and salt water could help make people’s health better. At the same time, people began to protest Sydney's daytime swimming laws, with William Gocher's 1902 famous challenge of the law at Manly setting a new standard. Combined with better public transport and fewer working hours meant more people began to enjoy the beaches and the crowds began to arrive.

images.caption: Beachgoers at Bondi Beach

chapters.heading: The Birth of Surf Lifesaving
body: However, larger crowds meant more drownings, and groups started to form with the goal of protecting beachgoers. The Bondi Surf Bathers' Life Saving Club formed at a meeting at the Royal Hotel on 21 February 1907, with its aim plainly stated as the rescue of the distressed. Surf Life Saving Australia recognises it as the oldest such club in the world. Neighbouring Bronte has long pressed its own claim, but a review of the records in 2005 found Bondi's the earliest that could be properly documented.

images.caption: Bondi Surf Club members pose in front of Bondi’s first ‘clubhouse’ – a canvas tent – circa 1907
North Bondi Clubhouse, 1920
The surf life saving reel was invented at Bondi Beach in 1906 by Lyster Ormsby

chapters.heading: A Lasting Legacy
body: Much of what we now think of as global beach culture was worked out on this sand. The red-and-yellow flags, the reel and line for hauling a rescuer back through the surf, and the figure of the bronzed volunteer lifesaver all trace back to these early Sydney clubs.

images.caption: Surf lifesaving in action
Red and yellow flags and volunteer surf lifesavers are a legacy of the early Bondi surf lifesavers.

title: The Shark Arm Case

chapters.heading: A Tiger Shark on Display
body: On 17 April 1935, a fisherman hooked a small shark off Coogee Beach. As he was reeling it in, a four-metre tiger shark came and swallowed the smaller shark, providing the fisherman with a fortuitous double-catch. Astounded, the fisherman took the larger shark to the nearby Coogee Aquarium Baths, where it would make a wonderful attraction for the following Anzac Day weekend.

At the time, Sydney was gripped by a shark hysteria, following the three shark attacks in late February and early March. Bounty hunters were employed to help rid Sydney's beaches of the menace, and so the new attraction drew large crowds. For several days the shark seemed quite active and had a voracious appetite, but on 25 April, Anzac Day, it began acting strangely: it appeared ill, moved slowly and was seemingly disoriented. Then suddenly there was a great commotion in the pool, and while spectators watched, the shark vomited up a human arm, thought to have been swallowed by the smaller shark.

images.caption: The Coogee Acquarium
The tiger shark on display
Coogee Beach in 1891, with the domed aquarium in the background

chapters.heading: A Murder Case
body: Initially, it was presumed to be another shark-induced tragedy, and an investigation was launched into identifying the victim. Although the gastric juices of sharks are highly acidic, the arm was shockingly well-preserved: it was estimated that the arm could have been in its stomach for between eight and 18 days, and there was a still recognisable tattoo of two boxers shaping up to fight on the forearm. But the biggest twist was yet to come. A medical examination of the arm revealed that it had not been bitten off by a shark, but had been sawed off by a knife or another sharp instrument.
Sydney had a murder case on its hands.

images.caption: The identifiable tattoo

chapters.heading: The Victim
body: The revelation swept through Sydney, and it wasn't long before the victim was identified. After reading a description of the tattoo in the Sydney Truth, Edwin Smith contacted police to suggest that the arm belonged to his brother Jim. This was quickly confirmed by fingerprints obtained from the astoundingly well-preserved arm: Jim Smith was the victim.

A bankrupt builder and former SP bookmaker and boxer, Smith had begun to drift onto the edges of the underworld, becoming involved in the illegal gambling that plagued 1930s Sydney. Police investigations found that he had last been seen drinking with his long-time friend and expert forger Patrick Brady, in the Cecil Hotel at Cronulla, after which they had then returned to a cottage rented by Brady on the shore of Gunnamatta Bay.

images.caption: Sydney “Truth” Newspaper 5th May 1935
Jim Smith

chapters.heading: The Suspects
body: Brady was thus the new focus of the investigation, and a Cronulla cab driver provided the next lead. On the morning after Jim Smith was seen for the last time, Brady hailed the cab in Cronulla and asked to be taken to North Sydney, where the cab was directed to pull up outside a house that turned out to be the home of middle-class businessman Reginald Lloyd Holmes.

Holmes ran a highly successful boatbuilding business on the harbour foreshore at McMahons Point on Lavender Bay, and was involved in underworld activites. Using the speedboats built at his boatshed, he operated a lucrative smuggling ring which picked up cocaine, cigarettes and other contraband thrown overboard from ships passing off Sydney Heads. Smith was sometimes an employee of Holmes, and often drove one of the speedboats.

However, they had fallen out over a failed insurance scam, and it was speculated that Smith had begun to blackmail Holmes, using the boatbuilder's position in society as leverage. The police had their key suspects in Brady and Holmes, but all they had was circumstantial evidence. Brady and Holmes were both brought in to Central Police Station, but both claimed not to know the other.

images.caption: Patrick Brady (left) and Reginald Lloyd Holmes (right)
Patrick Brady after his arrest

chapters.heading: A Star Witness
body: The case seemed stalled until 20 May, when Holmes attempted suicide on one of his boats in the middle of the harbour. He survived, the water police were alerted, and for four hours they pursued Holmes out past Circular Quay, through the mid-morning ferry traffic, right down Sydney Harbour until, finally, he gave up just outside Sydney Heads. Holmes was arrested and agreed to be a witness against Brady, who was subsequently charged with Smith's murder.

He told Detective Sergeant Frank Matthews that Brady had killed Smith, dismembered his body and stowed it into a trunk that he had then thrown into Gunnamatta Bay. He then claimed Brady had come to his home, showed him the severed arm and threatened Holmes with murder if he did not receive £500 immediately. Holmes also admitted that after Brady had left his home, he travelled to Maroubra and discarded Smith's arm in the surf.

images.caption: Sydney "Truth" Report June 16th 1935

chapters.heading: The Case Falls Apart
body: On 11 June 1935, Holmes withdrew £500 from his account and left home late that evening, telling his wife he had to meet someone.  At 1:20 am the next morning, just hours before the start of the inquest into Smith's death, Holmes's body was found slumped over the wheel of his car, shot dead in the deserted docks area of Dawes Point.

The prosecutors lost their star witness, and the case fell apart. The cab driver's description of a frightened, dishevelled Brady who wouldn't take his hand out of his pocket was not enough, and the trial was over in less than two days. Brady was acquitted and walked from the court a free man.

Many speculated that Smith was murdered by Brady on orders from underworld figure Eddie Weyman. Smith had been a police informer (colloquially known as a 'fizzer' or 'fizgig' at the time), and it was his information that saw Weyman and an associate caught red-handed raiding a bank. Holmes would have revealed this, and so Weyman's underworld associates eliminated this problem.

images.caption: Sydney Sun Newspaper June 15th 1935

chapters.heading: Shark Arm Case: Unsolved
body: Gripped by a crime wave, Sydney in the 1930s was dangerous place for police informants. There was open gang warfare in the streets of Kings Cross and East Sydney, battles over control of cocaine distribution and prostitution: Darlinghurst was commonly known as Razorhurst, after the gangs' most used weapon.

No charges ever stuck, and the Shark Arm Case remains unsolved. With both Smith and Holmes, it could have been a case of 'cleaning up': getting rid of those who had fallen out with some of Sydney's leading criminals. Either way, it is undoubtedly the most bizarre true crime story in Sydney's history.

images.caption: Cover of book 'The Shark Arm Case' by Vince Kelly, published by Horwitz Publications 1963

title: Rose Bay: Australia's First International Airport

chapters.heading: Flying Boats: A Technological Marvel
body: Flying boats came to Sydney Harbour in January 1937 when Australia agreed to participate in the Empire Air Mail Scheme (EAMS). It was decided that Rose Bay would become the terminus of the United Kingdom to Australia airmail route and Australia's first international airport for flying boats. The only suggested alternative was Botany Bay, but a large breakwater would have had to been built to provided the calm waters required to land. Rose Bay was naturally sheltered, and so in May 1938, Qantas Empire Airways shifted its operations to Sydney. The flying boats quickly evolved to carry passengers, and the first full service to Southampton left on 5 July that year. The base was officially opened a month later, on 4 August.

images.caption: Empire Class Flying Boats carried mail and passengers in style from Sydney to London in 1938-1939
Construction of hanger for flying boats, Rose Bay June 1939
Lord Huntingfield opens flying boat service, Rose Bay 4 August 1938

chapters.heading: The Kangaroo Route
body: Passengers travelled in genuine comfort, island-hopping up the coast and across Asia, the Middle East and Europe with around thirty stops along the way. The trip to London took about ten days, and the route soon earned the name that stuck to it for decades, the Kangaroo Route. There were fifteen passenger seats, an air crew of two and a cabin crew of three to service the flight.

images.caption: Gateway to the world - Rose Bay flying boat base 1939
Spacious cabins on board Qantas flying boats
Imperial Airways inflight menu for the RMA Coorong flying between Brisbane and Sydney 12 July 1938

chapters.heading: Flying Boats in the War
body: The onset of World War II saw services increasingly disrupted, and the flying boats were used by the RAAF to support the war effort, carrying supplies and providing reconnaissance. Two weeks after World War II ended, on 16 September 1945, nine Catalina flying boats landed at Rose Bay, repatriating Australian prisoner of war survivors from Japanese camps. They were greeted by 50,000 spectators, and hundreds of small boats were there to meet the returned POWs.

images.caption: Australian prisoners-of-war returning home. Released Eighth Division men coming ashore by barge from the catalinas at Rose Bay 16 September 1945

chapters.heading: A Quiet Decline
body: Flights to London resumed on 18 May 1946, famous for their luxury. In 1947 a return flight to Australia from England cost more than one hundred times the average annual wage. The advent of jet airliners in the 1950s saw the decline of the flying boats, although they did keep providing services to Lord Howe Island until 1974. The sightseeing seaplanes that still take off from the bay today are the last working trace of that age.

images.caption: Ansett advertisement for Hayman Island 'swift flying boats can whisk you there in a matter of hours' March 1952
The sightseeing seaplanes of Rose Bay today
